title: News to Numbers: A Comparative Analysis of Traditional and API-driven LLMs in Stock Return Prediction
abstract: This thesis investigates the comparative effectiveness of two large language model (LLM) deployment paradigms—zero-shot inference via commercial APIs and supervised fine-tuning of transformer-based models—for stock return prediction using financial news. Leveraging a novel dataset of over 30,000 Dow Jones Newswire (DJN) articles aligned with firm-level returns from 1989 to 2020, the study systematically evaluates OpenAI’s GPT-4 in a zero-shot setting against fine-tuned BERT-based models trained on identical text–return pairs. The analysis employs a rolling-window cross-validation framework to ensure robustness across market regimes and emphasizes not only predictive accuracy but also operational feasibility, cost efficiency, and scalability. Results demonstrate that both modeling approaches produce statistically significant predictive power above random baselines, yet with distinct trade-offs. GPT-4 achieves 53.38% directional accuracy and a Sharpe ratio of 1.076, highlighting its ability to extract meaningful signals without domain-specific tuning. Its operational simplicity, low setup cost, and per-call flexibility make it particularly attractive for rapid deployment and lightweight financial applications. By contrast, the supervised BERT model delivers markedly stronger financial performance, attaining an annualized Sharpe ratio of 4.08 and mean daily returns of 12.81 basis points. These superior outcomes underscore the value of domain-specific adaptation, but they come at the cost of substantial GPU resources, specialized expertise, and ongoing maintenance requirements. Temporal analysis further reveals that both approaches maintain predictive robustness across diverse economic conditions—including the dot-com era, the 2008 financial crisis, and post-crisis market environments—though performance metrics vary with shifts in efficiency and volatility. Importantly, GPT-4 provides consistent positive alphas despite its modest accuracy, while fine-tuned BERT models extract higher-magnitude signals during volatile periods, reinforcing the economic relevance of tailored architectures. The findings contribute to the growing literature at the intersection of financial economics and natural language processing by offering the first unified empirical framework to compare API-driven and locally fine-tuned LLMs for financial prediction. They highlight a fundamental trade-off in institutional strategy: API-based models democratize access to advanced language capabilities with minimal infrastructure, while fine-tuned models yield superior risk-adjusted returns for institutions capable of sustaining the computational investment. This research advances both theory and practice by demonstrating that textual data continues to contain exploitable information for asset pricing and that the operational context—budget, infrastructure, and regulatory considerations—should guide the choice of modeling paradigm. Ultimately, the results suggest that financial institutions must navigate between accessibility and performance, as the integration of LLMs into quantitative trading evolves from experimental adoption toward strategic deployment.
Author: Bai, Rong
abstract_manipulated: This thesis delves into the comparative effectiveness of two prominent language model deployment paradigms—zero-shot inference via commercial APIs and supervised fine-tuning of transformer-based models—for the purpose of stock return prediction utilizing financial news. A unique dataset comprising over 30,000 Dow Jones Newswire (DJN) articles synchronized with firm-level returns from the period spanning 1989 to 2020 is harnessed for this investigation. OpenAI’s GPT-4 is rigorously assessed in a zero-shot context against fine-tuned BERT-based models trained on matching text–return pairs. A rolling-window cross-validation framework is employed to ensure robustness across varying market conditions, giving due attention not only to predictive accuracy but also to operational feasibility, cost efficiency, and scalability. The findings suggest that both modeling methodologies exhibit statistically significant predictive capability beyond random benchmarks, albeit with discernible trade-offs. GPT-4 attains a directional accuracy of 53.38% and a Sharpe ratio of 1.076, underscoring its adeptness in extracting meaningful signals sans domain-specific adjustments. Its operational simplicity, minimal setup cost, and on-demand adaptability render it particularly appealing for swift deployment and lightweight financial applications. Conversely, the supervised BERT model showcases notably superior financial performance, achieving an annualized Sharpe ratio of 4.08 and mean daily returns of 12.81 basis points. These superior outcomes underscore the importance of domain-specific customization, albeit at the expense of considerable GPU resources, specialized expertise, and ongoing maintenance requirements. Temporal analysis further reveals the sustained predictive robustness of both approaches across diverse economic circumstances—including the dot-com era, the 2008 financial crisis, and post-crisis market environments—although performance metrics fluctuate with changes in efficiency and volatility. Crucially, GPT-4 consistently generates positive alphas despite its moderate accuracy, while fine-tuned BERT models capture more pronounced signals during periods of volatility, reinforcing the economic significance of tailored architectures. The study enriches the expanding body of literature at the confluence of financial economics and natural language processing by introducing a unified empirical framework for comparing API-driven and locally fine-tuned LLMs for financial prediction. It underscores a fundamental strategic dilemma for institutions: API-based models broaden access to advanced language capabilities with minimal infrastructure, whereas fine-tuned models deliver enhanced risk-adjusted returns for institutions able to sustain the computational outlay. This research advances both theoretical understanding and practical application by demonstrating the continued presence of exploitable information for asset pricing within textual data and by advocating that the operational context—encompassing budgetary constraints, infrastructure capacities, and regulatory requirements—dictates the selection of modeling paradigms. Ultimately, the results posit that financial institutions must navigate the fine line between accessibility and performance as the integration of LLMs into quantitative trading transitions from experimental exploration to strategic implementation.

title: Labor Market Shocks and Heterogeneous Adjustment: A Semi-Nonparametric Approach
abstract: This study examines how labor markets adjust to international trade shocks in recent decades, with a particular focus on the China trade shock that has fundamentally reshaped global manufacturing since the 1990s. To estimate the effect, the project will combine structural economic modeling with semi-parametric machine learning techniques to understand heterogeneous labor market responses across demographic groups and industries. By merging rigorous economic theory with flexible estimation methods, we aim to provide nuanced insights into the transition costs workers face when responding to trade disruption, addressing a gap in the current literature that often treats adjustment frictions as homogeneous across populations. After the estimation, we'll apply agent-based modelling framework on simulated counterfactual sample to experiment with different tariff intervention and agent features to further understand the dynamics and welfare implication.
Author: Xu, Jingzhi
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the mechanisms by which labor markets adapt to perturbations from international trade in recent epochs, with a specific emphasis on the China trade upheaval that has fundamentally reshaped the global manufacturing landscape since the 1990s. The estimation of the impact will be facilitated through the amalgamation of structural economic modeling with semi-parametric machine learning techniques, in order to comprehend the diverse responses of labor markets across demographic cohorts and sectors. By synthesizing robust economic theory with flexible estimation methodologies, our endeavor is to offer nuanced insights into the transitional costs borne by workers in the wake of trade disruptions, thereby addressing a lacuna in the extant literature that frequently treats adjustment frictions as uniform across populations. Subsequent to the estimation process, an agent-based modeling framework will be deployed on a simulated counterfactual sample to experiment with different tariff interventions and agent attributes, thereby enhancing our understanding of the dynamics and welfare implications at play.

title: The Cost of Decentralization? Welfare Comparisons between Double Auctions and Automated Market Makers in Transactive Energy Markets
abstract: This paper quantifies the welfare implications of adopting an Automated Market Maker (AMM) in place of the simultaneous time-delimited double auction used in the Transactive Energy Service System (TESS). We develop an agent-based simulation of a peer-to-peer electricity market populated by heterogeneous demand, solar, utility, and battery agents, and evaluate market outcomes under varying technical and operational parameters. Battery strategies are modeled both through value function iteration (VFI) dynamic programming and an alternative informed trader heuristic that exploits anticipated intertemporal price differentials. Regression analysis on simulation output reveals that, across a broad range of scenarios, the AMM delivers statistically significant gains in total social welfare relative to the double auction. While the VFI battery strategy often generates negative surplus under the AMM, these losses are offset by gains to other agents. The informed trader battery eliminates the negative battery surplus without diminishing total welfare, indicating that strategic adaptation can improve equity of surplus distribution while maintaining efficiency. These results suggest that the cost of decentralization—defined as welfare loss from substituting an AMM for a double auction—can be negative in certain contexts, implying a potential net benefit. However, we find that part of the AMM’s advantage stems from its implicit capacity flexibility, which may not be feasible in practical deployment. We conclude by outlining future research to assess AMM performance under realistic capacity constraints and more diverse trading behaviors.
Author: Bhatt, Nalin
abstract_manipulated: The welfare implications of replacing the simultaneous time-delimited double auction with an Automated Market Maker (AMM) in the Transactive Energy Service System (TESS) are quantified in this study. An agent-based simulation of a peer-to-peer electricity market, featuring heterogeneous demand, solar, utility, and battery agents, is constructed to assess market outcomes under varying technical and operational parameters. Battery strategies are formulated using value function iteration (VFI) dynamic programming and an alternative informed trader heuristic exploiting anticipated intertemporal price differentials. Regression analysis applied to the simulation output demonstrates that, in a wide range of scenarios, the AMM yields statistically significant enhancements in total social welfare compared to the double auction. Although the VFI battery strategy may result in negative surplus under the AMM, these losses are counterbalanced by gains elsewhere in the system. The informed trader battery strategy mitigates negative battery surplus without compromising overall welfare, illustrating that strategic adaptation can enhance equity in surplus distribution while preserving efficiency. These findings indicate that the cost of decentralization, which refers to the welfare reduction associated with substituting an AMM for a double auction, can potentially be negative under certain conditions, suggesting a net benefit. However, it is noted that a portion of the AMM's advantage is attributable to its implicit capacity flexibility, a feature that may pose challenges in practical implementation. The study concludes by proposing future research directions to evaluate AMM performance under realistic capacity constraints and a wider array of trading behaviors.

title: The Art of Positivity in Drawing: Unveiling the Impact of Positive Mood States on Visual Creativity via Deep Learning
abstract: This study integrates visual creative tasks with artificial intelligence techniques to study the flexibility pathway proposed by the dual pathway to creativity model. According to this model, positive activating moods enhance originality—the uncommonness of ideas, solutions, or products— by increasing cognitive flexibility, which facilitates the switching between thoughts and supports the exploration and connection of diverse ideas. Diverging from the predominance of verbal tasks, this study employs the Incomplete Shape Drawing task to track both the dynamics of the creative process indicative of cognitive flexibility and the originality of the final completed drawings. Using validated film clips for mood induction, I randomly assigned 90 participants to one of three groups: High-Arousal Positive Mood, Low-Arousal Positive Mood, and Neutral Control. Participants then completed three rounds of the Incomplete Shape Drawing Task (with qualitatively distinct stimuli), each followed by a section to provide a written narrative on their thought process. Flexibility was assessed through two modalities: (1) stroke-level drawing behavior using the Compositional Stroke Embedding model to compute entropy and Bhattacharyya distance; and (2) semantic-level flexibility using Divergent Semantic Integration to quantify the integration of conceptually distant ideas in participants’ narratives. Originality was evaluated using AuDrA, a deep learning model trained on human originality ratings from the same task. The results indicated that while mood induction effectively altered participants’ self-reported arousal and valence, no significant differences in flexibility or originality were observed across mood conditions. However, correlational and multilevel regression analyses suggested two distinct modes of flexibility: persistent exploratory breadth and adaptive switching. Crucially, only adaptive switching flexibility measures significantly predicted originality. Together, these results raised caveats on transient mood manipulations in creativity research, while illuminating the value of process-level, adaptive flexibility as a core mechanism underlying originality. Moreover, it demonstrates the utility of integrating drawing-based tasks with artificial intelligence to capture the dynamic, cross-modal mechanisms underpinning original thoughts.
Author: Cong, Tianyue
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation incorporates visual artistic tasks with artificial intelligence methodologies to explore the flexibility pathway posited by the dual pathway to creativity framework. In accordance with this theoretical framework, optimistic activating states may potentially boost originality—the novelty of concepts, solutions, or artifacts— by augmenting cognitive flexibility, which enables the transition between mental constructs and facilitates the exploration and linkage of varied ideas. Departing from the prevalent focus on linguistic tasks, the Incomplete Shape Drawing task is employed in this inquiry to monitor both the dynamics of the creative process indicative of cognitive flexibility and the originality of the final completed drawings. Through the utilization of validated film excerpts for mood manipulation, a total of 90 participants were randomly allocated to one of three experimental groups: High-Arousal Positive Mood, Low-Arousal Positive Mood, and Neutral Control. Subsequently, participants engaged in three iterations of the Incomplete Shape Drawing Task (featuring qualitatively distinct stimuli), each succeeded by a segment prompting participants to provide a written account of their cognitive processes. Flexibility was evaluated through two distinct lenses: (1) stroke-level drawing behavior utilizing the Compositional Stroke Embedding model for entropy computation and Bhattacharyya distance analysis; and (2) semantic-level flexibility employing Divergent Semantic Integration to quantify the amalgamation of conceptually distant ideas within participants' narratives. Originality was gauged using AuDrA, a deep learning algorithm trained on originality ratings from human evaluators for the same task. The findings revealed that while the manipulation of mood states did effectively alter participants' self-reported arousal and valence, no statistically significant discrepancies in flexibility or originality were discerned across the different mood conditions. Nevertheless, correlational and multilevel regression examinations indicated the presence of two distinct modes of flexibility: persistent exploratory breadth and adaptive switching. Critically, solely the measures of adaptive switching flexibility exhibited a significant association with originality. These outcomes underscored reservations regarding the efficacy of transient mood alterations in the context of creativity research, while shedding light on the significance of adaptive flexibility at the process level as a foundational mechanism underpinning original ideation. Furthermore, this study underscores the utility of amalgamating drawing-based tasks with artificial intelligence methodologies to capture the intricate, cross-modal mechanisms that underlie the formulation of unique thoughts.

title: Mapping Informality and Violence: Machine Learning Insights into Crime Patterns Across South African Police Jurisdictions
abstract: This study examines the relationship between neighborhood informality and crime rates in South Africa, where rapid urbanization has led to criminal organizations stepping in to provide services within some of these settlements. Applying K-means clustering and Local Moran’s I analysis to 1,164 police jurisdictions, we observe that the cluster with the highest level of informality also exhibited the lowest crime rates. Furthermore, in clusters representing moderately dense urban areas, linear regression reveals a statistically significant negative correlation between levels of informality and crime rates. Machine learning models, including KNN and random forest, show that crime can be predicted with low MSE, with k-complexity emerging as a key feature. Finally, a longitudinal case study on crime and growth data offers further insight into potential causal relationships, though the results are not statistically significant. These findings have implications for urban governance and public safety policies in developing countries.
Author: Liang, Yingyi
abstract_manipulated: The investigation of the association between neighborhood informality and crime rates in South Africa, where rapid urbanization has facilitated the emergence of criminal organizations providing services within certain settlements, is undertaken in this analysis. By applying K-means clustering and Local Moran’s I analysis to a dataset comprising 1,164 police jurisdictions, it is observed that the cluster exhibiting the highest level of informality is concurrently associated with the lowest crime rates. Moreover, within clusters representing moderately dense urban areas, a statistically significant negative correlation between levels of informality and crime rates is revealed through the utilization of linear regression analysis. Machine learning algorithms, such as K-nearest neighbors (KNN) and random forest, suggest that crime prediction can be achieved with a low mean squared error (MSE), with k-complexity identified as a salient feature. Additionally, a longitudinal case study examining crime patterns and growth data provides further insights into potential causal links, although the outcomes do not achieve statistical significance. These findings carry implications for urban governance strategies and the formulation of public safety policies in developing nations.

title: Locked In or Lifted Up? Impact of Kenya land settlement plan on long-term economics development
abstract: This paper examines the long-term effects of Kenya’s post-independence land settlement policy, focusing on the "Million Acre Scheme," a large-scale redistribution initiative implemented in the 1960s. Using a geographic regression discontinuity (RD) design and multiple waves of Demographic and Health Survey (DHS) data, the study evaluates how formal land rights granted through the settlement program have shaped economic development outcomes over time. The analysis links a newly digitized historical map of settlement boundaries to geocoded household data and reveals that individuals living within settlement areas exhibit significantly higher wealth levels but lower educational attainment—particularly among women and those located far from markets. These results suggest that while land tenure security may have spurred investment and asset accumulation, it also constrained household mobility and reduced incentives to invest in human capital. The findings underscore the importance of balancing tenure formalization with broader access to economic and educational opportunities when designing land reform policy.
Author: Hui, Yu
abstract_manipulated: This study investigates the enduring impacts of Kenya’s post-independence land settlement strategy, with a focus on the "Million Acre Scheme," a large-scale redistribution program implemented in the 1960s. Employing a geographic regression discontinuity (RD) design and leveraging multiple waves of Demographic and Health Survey (DHS) data, an assessment is made regarding the extent to which formal land entitlements conferred through the settlement initiative have influenced economic development outcomes over an extended period. By connecting a recently digitized historical map of settlement boundaries to geocoded household data, it is revealed that individuals residing within settlement zones exhibit notably elevated levels of wealth but diminished educational achievements—particularly notable among women and those situated at a considerable distance from market centers. These outcomes potentially imply that while land tenure security might have stimulated investment and accumulation of assets, it also imposed constraints on household mobility and diminished motivations for investment in human capital. The results emphasize the necessity of striking a balance between formalizing land tenure and ensuring wider access to economic and educational prospects in the formulation of land reform policies.

title: Financing Under a Mandatory Single-payer Universal Health Insurance System
abstract: This paper develops a stylized two-part tariff model to evaluate the fiscal and distributional effects of introducing income-based coinsurance in Egypt’s new Universal Health Insur- ance System (UHIS). Using 2019/20 Household Income, Expenditure and Consumption Survey (HIECS) data, I derive closed-form expressions for household reservation prices for healthcare under mandatory premium payments and calibrate the model with empirically estimated demand elasticity. Numerical simulations compare the status-quo design, flat 7 percent premiums and 10 percent coinsurance with broad medical exemptions, to alter- native schedules that segment coinsurance by income quintile and decile while imposing financial protection caps. Relative to the simulated baseline deficit of EGP 332 billion, the best-performing decile schedule shrinks the funding gap to EGP 52 billion: an 84 percent improvement. The results demonstrate that coinsurance can operate as a redistributive instrument, not merely a cost-containment device, and offer a tractable framework for other low- and middle-income countries that seek to balance equity and solvency under universal health insurance mandates.
Author: Abdelbaki, Nour
abstract_manipulated: This paper engages in the development of a stylized dual-tier tariff model for the appraisal of the fiscal and distributional ramifications arising from the introduction of income-based coinsurance within Egypt's nascent Universal Health Insurance System (UHIS). Leveraging data from the 2019/20 Household Income, Expenditure and Consumption Survey (HIECS), closed-form expressions for household reservation prices for healthcare under obligatory premium payments are derived, followed by the calibration of the model utilizing empirically estimated demand elasticity. Through numerical simulations, a comparison is drawn between the existing design, characterized by uniform 7 percent premiums and 10 percent coinsurance with expansive medical exemptions, and alternative schedules that stratify coinsurance based on income quintile and decile, while incorporating caps for financial protection. In contrast to the baseline deficit simulation of EGP 332 billion, the most effective decile-oriented schedule reduces the funding shortfall to EGP 52 billion, representing an 84 percent enhancement. The findings indicate the potential for coinsurance to serve as a tool for redistribution, beyond its role as a mechanism for cost containment, thereby offering a practical framework for other nations with limited resources that aim to navigate the delicate balance between equity and financial stability within the context of universal health insurance mandates.

title: Intergenerational Educational Spillovers in Pakistan
abstract: Education provides a channel for poverty escape via intergenerational mobility (Zuo et. al, 2023). Pakistan’s stifled mobility and consistent political instability provides an opportunity to learn more about the impact of structural and policy changes on intergenerational mo- bility. This study examines the spillover effects of 1972 nationalization policy on children’s educational outcomes in Pakistan with a particular focus on intergenerational spillovers mediated through fathers’ education. A comprehensive microdataset is used from Pakistan Social and Living Standards Measurements (PSLM) 2019-20 survey. To analyze the impact of policy regional level Difference-in-Difference (DiD) approach was adopted. To quantify the causal impact further a complementary Instrumental Variable approach was also uti- lized. Findings suggests that 1972 nationalization policy improved educational access in restricted sample case, especially in rural areas with meagre 0.33 additional schooling on average. Whereas, IV approach reveal that Fathers’ education showed strong intergener- ational spillovers, as an additional year of schooling increased a child’s education by 1.03 years. However, DiD results suggested that overall educational attainment decline was ob- served post policy. Additionally, impact on child’ earning outcomes also confirms that just the supply side reforms are not sufficient for interegenerational mobility unless high quality of schooling is also ensured.
Author: Zeb, Sania
abstract_manipulated: Education serves as a conduit for escaping poverty through intergenerational mobility (Zuo et. al, 2023). The constrained mobility and enduring political volatility in Pakistan present a unique opportunity to delve deeper into the ramifications of structural and policy shifts on intergenerational mobility. This investigation delves into the spillover effects of the 1972 nationalization policy on the educational outcomes of offspring in Pakistan, specifically emphasizing intergenerational spillovers mediated by paternal education. Leveraging a comprehensive microdataset extracted from the 2019-20 Pakistan Social and Living Standards Measurements (PSLM) survey, a regional-level Difference-in-Difference (DiD) approach was employed to scrutinize the policy's impact. To enhance the causal inference, an additional Instrumental Variable approach was also deployed. The outcomes tentatively imply that the 1972 nationalization policy marginally enhanced educational accessibility within a constrained sample subset, notably in rural locales, manifesting in an average increase of 0.33 schooling years. Conversely, the Instrumental Variable analysis indicates a notable intergenerational transmission through paternal education, with each additional schooling year for fathers correlating with a 1.03-year increment in their offspring's education. Nonetheless, the DiD results hint at an overall decline in educational attainment following the policy implementation. Furthermore, the repercussions on children's earning potential affirm that mere supply-side reforms do not suffice for intergenerational mobility unless accompanied by a guarantee of high-quality education.

title: Concept Over Craft: The Acceleration of Creative Peaks in Early 20th Century Modernism
abstract: This study identifies a historical shift in classical music from experimentalism to conceptualism, particularly between Late Romanticism/Impressionism and Early 20th Century Modernism. Using professional performance frequency since 1996 as a retrospective measure of innovation, we estimate composer-level age-performance profiles for 35 canonical figures. Regression results reveal significant lifecycle differences: pre-1874 composers peaked later, consistent with sustained technical refinement, whereas post-1874 Modernists peaked notably earlier, often in their twenties, reflecting conceptual innovation. These findings persist through various robustness checks, controlling for instrumentation, duration, composer identity, and calendar year effects. The results support Galenson’s theory of artistic archetypes and highlight how shifting aesthetic values profoundly influenced both the timing and historical recognition of musical creativity.
Author: Cui, Yuanhan
abstract_manipulated: This investigation discerns a historical transition in the realm of classical music from experimentalism to conceptualism, with a specific focus on the period spanning Late Romanticism/Impressionism and Early 20th Century Modernism. By utilizing the frequency of professional performances as a retrospective metric of innovativeness since 1996, composer-level age-performance profiles for 35 esteemed figures are estimated. The regression outcomes unveil notable variations in lifecycles: composers predating 1874 reached their zenith at a later stage, a trend indicative of sustained technical advancement, while post-1874 Modernists experienced peak achievements at a notably earlier age, frequently during their twenties, symbolizing a penchant for conceptual ingenuity. These empirical insights persist across a range of rigorous sensitivity analyses, accounting for factors such as instrumentation, duration, composer attribution, and temporal effects. The present outcomes lend credence to Galenson’s framework of artistic archetypes and underscore how evolving aesthetic paradigms profoundly influenced the temporal dynamics and retrospective acknowledgment of musical innovation.

title: What stop people from taking an ‘almost free’ COVID-19 vaccine?
abstract: This essay explores why the COVID-19 vaccine take-up rate is relatively lower in the adult group, despite its benefits and effectiveness. Leveraging a Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes (BLP) demand framework, I estimate how local pandemic severity and demographic factors shape individual’s vaccination behavior across states. The analysis reveals that disadvantaged groups—those with lower education, poorer health, and unemployment—are more likely to postpone their vaccination plans, while younger individuals are more responsive to changes in perceived risk. These findings call for careful policy design that accounts for spatial and age-specific heterogeneity in vaccination hesitancy.
Author: Wang, Weiqi
abstract_manipulated: This essay delves into the underlying reasons for the comparatively diminished uptake of the COVID-19 vaccine within the adult cohort, notwithstanding its acknowledged advantages and efficacy. Employing a Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes (BLP) demand framework, an estimation is made regarding how localized severity of the pandemic and demographic variables influence the vaccination behavior of individuals across different states. It is disclosed through the analysis that marginalized segments—characterized by lower educational attainment, compromised health status, and unemployment tendencies—are more inclined to defer their vaccination intentions, whereas the younger demographic exhibits greater sensitivity to alterations in perceived risk levels. These findings underscore the necessity for meticulous policy formulation that accommodates the spatial and age-specific variations in vaccine hesitancy.

title: Analysis of Moral Language in Self-Improvement Discourse through Natural Language Processing
abstract: Self-improvement consists of making efforts to improve various traits such as health, appear- ance, efficiency, etc. Previous research has identified that popular culture is increasingly portraying aspects of self-improvement as obligations, as if they were moral requirements. This moralization can create stigmatization or be prejudicial when it fosters self-obsession and perfectionism (Coeckelbergh, 2022; Kraaijeveld & Jamrozik, 2022). Recent social me- dia studies have identified these issues among small samples of users, so the present work aims to contribute to the literature by expanding the scope and assessing if the findings of smaller studies hold true in a broader social media context and considering more self- improvement areas (Bell et al., 2024; Camacho-Mi˜nano & Gray, 2021; Coeckelbergh, 2022). Due to the speed of information flow and norms diffusion on the internet, social media platforms are relevant spots to observe the transmission of self-improvement ideas (Puryear et al., 2025). The guiding research questions for the analyses are: (1) Is moral language present in discussions about self-improvement? (2) Are there specific topics within self- improvement discussions that tend to be framed with a higher moral tone? and (3) How is the use of moral language associated with emotions in the context of self-improvement? To answer these questions, data from the subreddit r/selfimprovement was analyzed us- ing the LIWC software, the Moral Foundations Dictionary, and MoralBERT. Furthermore, LDA topic modeling was utilized for uncovering topics and comparing moralization among them. Findings suggest that the r/selfimprovement subreddit contains higher moral lan- guage, particularly among social-related topics and health & fitness discussions. Moreover, content with higher moral language was found to show higher negative emotion and anger language, supporting previous work on the risks of moralization. However, results also suggest that engagement with moral ideas within self-improvement may not be as negative when approached from a reflective and thoughtful perspective.
Author: Carpio Castellanos, Natasha
abstract_manipulated: Self-enhancement involves exerting efforts to ameliorate diverse facets such as well-being, aesthetics, efficacy, among others. It has been ascertained in antecedent scholarship that the realm of popular culture is progressively depicting elements of self-enhancement as imperatives, akin to ethical mandates. This process of moralization has the potential to engender stigmatization or bias when it cultivates self-absorption and pursuit of flawlessness (Coeckelbergh, 2022; Kraaijeveld & Jamrozik, 2022). Recent investigations within the domain of social media have detected these concerns within limited participant pools, hence the current endeavor seeks to enrich the scholarly discourse by broadening the purview and scrutinizing the validity of smaller-scale investigations within a more expansive social media milieu, encompassing a wider array of self-enhancement domains (Bell et al., 2024; Camacho-Miñano & Gray, 2021; Coeckelbergh, 2022). Owing to the rapid dissemination of information and cultural norms across the online sphere, social media platforms emerge as pertinent arenas for observing the dissemination of self-enhancement ideologies (Puryear et al., 2025). The analytical framework guiding this inquiry poses the following questions: (1) To what extent is moral discourse evident in deliberations pertaining to self-enhancement? (2) Are there particular themes within discussions on self-enhancement that tend to be imbued with a heightened moral tenor? and (3) What is the nature of the correlation between employment of moral language and emotional responses in the context of self-enhancement? In order to address these inquiries, data sourced from the subreddit r/selfimprovement was subjected to analysis employing the LIWC software, the Moral Foundations Dictionary, and MoralBERT. Additionally, LDA topic modeling was harnessed to unveil thematic clusters and juxtapose levels of moralization across them. The outcomes suggest an elevated prevalence of moral discourse within the r/selfimprovement subreddit, especially observable in dialogues concerning social dynamics and health & fitness. Furthermore, content characterized by heightened moral language exhibited elevated levels of negative affect and expressions of anger, corroborating extant scholarship on the perils of moralization. Nonetheless, the results also hint at the prospect that engagement with moral constructs within the realm of self-enhancement may not invariably yield adverse outcomes when undertaken from a contemplative and nuanced standpoint.

title: A Review of Relationships of Cannabis Legalization With Alcohol Use and Consumption
abstract: The legalization of marijuana, especially recreational cannabis legalization(RCL) has become prevalent in the States. The causation effect of RCL on alcohol use or consumption has aroused public attention widely, and the relevance of the two substances is essential to all aspects of society including economic development, public health issues, and taxation. Therefore, the review summarizes the recent studies’ results and conclusions to reveal the causation effect of recreational marijuana legalization on alcohol use.Online literature Research was performed for articles published in the last five years of English in main datasets. After abstract reviewing and full-text reviewing, 12 studies are included to compare the results in research on the effect.
Author: Tang, Ziheng
abstract_manipulated: The prevalence of recreational cannabis legalization (RCL), particularly in the United States, has led to heightened interest in the potential causal impact of RCL on alcohol consumption, a matter of considerable public concern. The interplay between these two substances holds significant implications for various societal domains, encompassing economic growth, public health considerations, and fiscal policy. Consequently, this meta-analytical review synthesizes recent empirical investigations to elucidate the possible causal relationship between recreational marijuana legalization and patterns of alcohol use. A systematic search of online literature repositories was undertaken to identify relevant articles published within the past five years in English across primary databases. Following a meticulous process of abstract screening and full-text evaluation, a total of 12 studies were selected for comparative analysis, shedding light on the nuanced dynamics at play within this domain.

title: Challenging Dual-Coding Theory: Picture Superiority Effect Persists in Aphantasia
abstract: Dual-coding theory proposes that superior memory for pictures compared to words (the ‘picture superiority effect’) and concrete words compared to abstract words (the ‘concrete- ness effect’) both stem from the ability to store information as verbal and image codes in memory. According to the theory, having two distinct memory codes increases recall prob- ability, as either code can independently lead to successful retrieval, and one code can serve to cue the other. The current study tests this theoretical explanation by examining recall performance in individuals with aphantasia—the inability to voluntarily generate mental imagery. Based on dual-coding theory, aphantasic individuals should show neither picture superiority nor concreteness effects due to these individuals’ inability to generate mental images and retrieve image-based memory codes. We recruited 62 aphantasic individuals and 69 typical imagers who completed a memory task with free recall of studied stimuli. Data were analyzed using mixed ANOVA and planned comparison t-tests. We compared recall performance between aphantasic and typical imagers across four stimulus types: pictures, symbols, concrete words, and abstract words. Despite their mental imagery deficit, aphan- tasic individuals demonstrated a robust picture superiority effect. Moreover, while typical populations show similar recall for pictures and symbols, aphantasic individuals displayed superior recall for symbols compared to pictures, perhaps because symbols’ simpler visual properties enable more efficient encoding through alternative strategies like motor imagery. In addition, both aphantasics and controls showed better performance with abstract relative to concrete words—contrary to typical concreteness effects—possibly because our abstract words had corresponding everyday symbols that made them more readily processed despite imagery limitations. These recall patterns across different stimuli in aphantasics challenge dual-coding theory’s explanation that superior recall for visual stimuli stems primarily from the formation and retrieval of supplementary image codes, indicating that either dual-coding is incorrect and some other non-dual-coding mechanism is responsible, or that, at the very least, dual-coding theory requires substantial modification to account for our present find- ings. This study provides novel insights into the relation between mental imagery and memory, challenging traditional theoretical frameworks and suggesting the need for alter- native explanations of these well-established memory phenomena.
Author: Yan, Muhan
abstract_manipulated: Dual-coding theory postulates that the memory advantage observed for images over words (referred to as the ‘picture superiority effect’) and for concrete over abstract words (termed the ‘concreteness effect’) stems from the capacity to encode information using verbal and visual codes in memory. It is suggested by this theory that the utilization of two distinct memory codes heightens the likelihood of successful recall, as each code has the potential to independently facilitate retrieval, with one code serving as a cue for the other. The theoretical framework was put to the test in the current investigation through an analysis of recall performance in individuals afflicted with aphantasia—the inability to consciously conjure mental imagery. In accordance with dual-coding theory, aphantasic individuals were expected to exhibit neither the picture superiority effect nor the concreteness effect due to their incapacity to generate mental images and access image-based memory codes. A total of 62 aphantasic individuals and 69 typical imagers were enlisted to participate in a memory task involving the free recall of previously studied stimuli. The data obtained were subjected to analysis utilizing mixed ANOVA and planned comparison t-tests. The examination compared the recall performance of aphantasic individuals and typical imagers across four categories of stimuli: images, symbols, concrete words, and abstract words. Despite their deficiency in mental imagery, aphantasic individuals displayed a pronounced picture superiority effect. Furthermore, unlike the general population that exhibits similar recall rates for pictures and symbols, aphantasic individuals exhibited superior recall for symbols over pictures, potentially attributable to the simpler visual characteristics of symbols facilitating more effective encoding through alternative strategies such as motor imagery. Additionally, both aphantasics and controls demonstrated enhanced performance with abstract as opposed to concrete words—contrary to the usual concreteness effects—potentially because the abstract words used in our study were accompanied by everyday symbols, rendering them more easily processed despite limitations in mental imagery. The recall patterns observed in aphantasics when presented with diverse stimuli challenge the dual-coding theory's assertion that the enhanced recall for visual stimuli primarily arises from the generation and retrieval of supplementary image codes, suggesting either the inadequacy of dual-coding or the requirement for significant revisions to the theory to accommodate our findings. This study offers fresh perspectives on the interplay between mental imagery and memory, prompting a reevaluation of established theoretical frameworks and advocating for alternative interpretations of these well-documented memory phenomena.

title: Predicting Clinical Trial Completion and Success using Machine Learning and NLP
abstract: This study introduces a dual-task machine learning framework for predicting both the opera- tional completion and scientific success of clinical trials using data from ClinicalTrials.gov. Leverag- ing structured trial metadata and unstructured textual descriptions, we develop predictive models that assess whether trials are likely to complete and whether they meet their primary endpoints. For the first task, ensemble models like XGBoost significantly outperform traditional baselines, partic- ularly when enriched with contextual embeddings derived from BioLinkBERT. For the second task, we propose a novel large language model (LLM)-driven annotation pipeline using GPT-4o-mini to label trial success based on publication content. Human evaluation confirms its high accuracy. Across both tasks, our framework demonstrates the value of combining structured features, natural language processing, and scalable LLM-based labeling to improve the understanding and forecast- ing of clinical trial performance. This approach not only enhances predictive accuracy but also contributes to better utilization of large-scale biomedical data.
Author: Li, Jiazheng
abstract_manipulated: This investigation introduces a dual-task machine learning framework aimed at predicting both the operationalization and scientific achievement of clinical trials by utilizing data sourced from ClinicalTrials.gov. By leveraging structured trial metadata alongside unstructured textual descriptions, predictive models are developed to evaluate the likelihood of trial completion and the fulfillment of primary endpoints. In the initial task, ensemble models such as XGBoost exhibit superior performance compared to conventional baselines, especially when enriched with contextually derived embeddings from BioLinkBERT. Concerning the second task, a novel annotation pipeline driven by a large language model (LLM) utilizing GPT-4o-mini is proposed to annotate trial success based on publication content. Evaluation by human assessors confirms its commendable accuracy. The utilization of a combination of structured features, natural language processing, and scalable LLM-based labeling within our framework showcases the efficacy in enhancing comprehension and prediction of clinical trial performance. This method not only enhances predictive precision but also contributes to the enhanced utilization of extensive biomedical data resources.

title: Between ‘Hukou’ and ‘I Do’: How the 2014 Hukou Reform Reshapes China’s Marriage Market
abstract: n this paper, I study how the 2014 relaxation of China’s household registration (Hukou) restrictions reshaped the marriage market for internal migrants. The reform significantly eased the requirements for obtaining local Hukou in non-megacities, while megacities re- tained strict controls. Exploiting this institutional change, I use a difference-in-differences framework to compare marriage outcomes between treatment cities (cities with population below 5 million) and control cities (cities with population above 5 million). I find that, after the reform, migrants in treatment cities became less likely to marry local Hukou holders, and the educational gap between spouses narrowed. These results suggest that the value of Hukou status as a marriage-matching advantage declined when local registration became easier to obtain. As a result, individuals placed more emphasis on non-institutional traits, such as education, when selecting partners.
Author: Zhang, Weiran
abstract_manipulated: In the present investigation, an analysis is undertaken on how the relaxation in 2014 of China's household registration (Hukou) restrictions has led to a reconfiguration of the marriage market dynamics for internal migrants. The aforementioned reform brought about a considerable alleviation in the prerequisites for acquiring local Hukou in non-megacities, while megacities maintained stringent controls. Leveraging this transformative institutional modification, a difference-in-differences framework is employed to conduct a comparative assessment of marriage outcomes between treatment cities (i.e., cities with a populace below 5 million) and control cities (i.e., cities with a populace exceeding 5 million). It is ascertained that subsequent to the reform, migrants residing in treatment cities exhibited a diminished propensity to form marital unions with local Hukou holders, concurrently witnessing a convergence in the educational disparity between spouses. These findings tentatively propose that the significance of Hukou status as a determiner in mate selection attenuated with the increased accessibility of local registration. Consequently, individuals seemingly accorded greater weight to non-institutional attributes, notably educational attainment, in the process of selecting partners.

title: Seasonal Influences on Regional Consumer Satisfaction: A Computational Analysis of Yelp Reviews
abstract: This study investigates how consumer satisfaction in the restaurant sector varies across seasonal periods and geographic contexts, using over one million Yelp reviews from Philadelphia and Tampa—two cities with distinct climatic and economic profiles. By integrating structured data (star ratings) with unstructured data (text-based sentiment scores), the analysis applies sentiment analysis, Ordinary Least Squares regression, ordered logistic models, and Random Forests to detect spatiotemporal patterns in consumer evaluations. Results show that satisfaction is generally higher in warmer seasons, but the strength of seasonal effects differs across cities, with Tampa exhibiting greater seasonal variation than Philadelphia. Sentiment scores are more sensitive to seasonal shifts than star ratings, and systematic divergence between the two emerges across cities and seasons. These findings suggest that economic structures, such as tourism reliance, may shape satisfaction more than climate alone, and highlight how textual analysis captures emotional nuance often masked by numeric ratings. The study also demonstrates how combining structured and unstructured data can reveal nonlinear and context-dependent satisfaction dynamics that conventional models may overlook. This contributes to consumer research by offering an integrated framework for modeling seasonal and geographic variation in satisfaction and by validating the utility of sentiment analysis in large-scale behavioral studies. Practical implications for service management and design are discussed, and future research directions are proposed to extend this work across diverse service contexts and environmental conditions.
Author: Zhang, Kexin
abstract_manipulated: This investigation explores the variability of consumer satisfaction within the restaurant industry across seasonal cycles and geographical landscapes, leveraging a dataset of over one million Yelp reviews from Philadelphia and Tampa—two municipalities characterized by unique climatic and economic profiles. By fusing structured data (star ratings) with unstructured data (textual sentiment scores), the analysis employs sentiment analysis, Ordinary Least Squares regression, ordered logistic models, and Random Forests to identify spatiotemporal patterns in consumer appraisals. It is revealed that satisfaction levels generally exhibit an upward trend during warmer periods, albeit with varying degrees of seasonal influence observed between the two cities, with Tampa demonstrating more pronounced seasonal fluctuations compared to Philadelphia. Notably, sentiment scores demonstrate a higher sensitivity to seasonal transitions than star ratings, with a discernible systematic divergence between the two metrics materializing across different urban contexts and temporal phases. These outcomes imply that economic configurations, such as reliance on tourism, may exert a more substantial impact on satisfaction levels than climatic factors alone, underscoring the capacity of textual analysis to uncover emotional subtleties often obscured by numerical ratings. Furthermore, the investigation showcases how amalgamating structured and unstructured data can unveil nonlinear and contingent dynamics in satisfaction that traditional models may fail to capture. This contributes to the field of consumer research by presenting an integrated framework for modeling variations in satisfaction across seasons and locations, as well as by validating the efficacy of sentiment analysis in large-scale studies of consumer behavior. The practical implications for service management and design are elucidated, and avenues for future research are posited to extend these insights to diverse service environments and ecological circumstances.

title: Investigating the Pathways from World Trade Center Exposures to COVID-19 Severity and Long-Term Sequelae
abstract: Our study examined the associations between WTC exposures and COVID outcomes using two analyses: one focused on COVID severity and the other on post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), which is known as long COVID. In the severity analysis, our aggregate exposure measure revealed that a significant portion of the effect on COVID severity is mediated by obstructive airway disease (OAD), indicating that impaired respiratory health is a key pathway linking WTC exposures to more severe acute illness. In the PASC analysis, we found that the overall effect of WTC exposures on long COVID is largely mediated by impaired respiratory health. These findings suggest that while OAD is a critical mediator for both COVID severity and PASC, additional mechanisms likely contribute to long-term outcomes.
Author: Yu, Bin
abstract_manipulated: The associations between World Trade Center (WTC) exposures and outcomes of COVID-19 were investigated in our research through two distinct analyses. One analysis was dedicated to exploring the severity of COVID-19, while the other delved into the post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), commonly referred to as long COVID. In the severity analysis, it was ascertained that a considerable portion of the impact on COVID-19 severity is potentially channeled through obstructive airway disease (OAD), indicating that compromised respiratory well-being constitutes a pivotal pathway linking WTC exposures to heightened acute illness severity. The analysis of PASC revealed that the overarching impact of WTC exposures on long COVID is predominantly channeled through compromised respiratory health. These findings tentatively suggest that although OAD plays a pivotal mediating role in both the severity of COVID-19 and PASC, there are likely additional mechanisms contributing to long-term outcomes.

title: Emotional and Cultural Patterns Expressed in Chinese and Japanese Cuisines in U.S. Consumer Reviews
abstract: Ethnic restaurants in the United States hold significant cultural and social meanings, being widely embraced by diverse communities. As these restaurants adapt to American dietary habits, they not only reflect but also influence culinary trends. Chinese cuisine has long been a popular choice among American diners, while Japanese cuisine has increasingly captured the attention of consumers. This growing interest can be traced back to the 1980s and 1990s, when chefs began incorporating Japanese ingredients and culinary techniques into their menus, a phenomenon known as the “Japanese turn.” By the twenty-first century's second decade, concepts like umami and shun had become familiar to many American chefs (yamashita, 2020). This thesis investigates how U.S. consumer reviews on Google reflect differing perceptions of Chinese and Japanese cuisines, focusing on emotional tone, word choice, and cultural framing. Using a dataset of over 690,000 reviews, the study applies TF-IDF, log-odds ratio, and Valence-Arousal-Dominance (VAD) analysis to identify distinct linguistic and emotional patterns associated with each cuisine. The findings reveal that consumer language around Chinese cuisine tends to reflect habitual, everyday experiences, suggesting symbolic assimilation, whereas Japanese cuisine is more frequently framed through cultural markers and positive emotional descriptors—indicating a retained sense of ethnic distinctiveness. This study contributes to broader discussions of authenticity, emotional evaluation, and cultural stratification in consumer discourse, showing how digital reviews both mirror and construct cultural narratives around ethnic food in the U.S.
Author: Yin, Yue
abstract_manipulated: The cultural and social significance of ethnic dining establishments in the United States is a topic of considerable interest, as they are widely embraced by diverse communities. It is suggested that these establishments, as they adapt to American dietary norms, not only serve as reflections but also exert influence on culinary trends. Chinese cuisine has maintained enduring popularity among American patrons, whereas Japanese cuisine has garnered increasing attention from consumers. The burgeoning fascination with Japanese culinary tradition can be traced back to the 1980s and 1990s, a period marked by the integration of Japanese ingredients and culinary methodologies into menus, commonly referred to as the "Japanese turn." By the second decade of the twenty-first century, concepts such as umami and shun had permeated the culinary lexicon of numerous American chefs. This thesis undertakes an exploration of how U.S. consumer reviews posted on Google may potentially encapsulate distinct perceptions of Chinese and Japanese gastronomy, with a particular emphasis on emotional tonality, lexical selection, and cultural contextualization. Leveraging a vast dataset comprising in excess of 690,000 reviews, the investigation employs TF-IDF, log-odds ratio, and Valence-Arousal-Dominance (VAD) analyses to discern unique linguistic and affective configurations associated with each culinary tradition. The outcomes suggest that expressions employed by consumers in appraising Chinese cuisine predominantly connote routine, everyday encounters, hinting at a form of symbolic assimilation, whereas discussions surrounding Japanese cuisine are more frequently imbued with cultural signifiers and positive emotional descriptors—signifying the preservation of ethnic distinctiveness. This inquiry contributes to broader dialogues on the themes of authenticity, emotional assessment, and cultural differentiation within consumer discourse, illustrating how digital reviews serve as both mirrors and architects of cultural narratives pertaining to ethnic sustenance in the American context.

title: Understanding Bullet Chat Moderation on Bilibili: Patterns, Effects, and Implications
abstract: On the popular Chinese video-sharing platform Bilibili, users can post live comments that float across video streams or broadcasts—a unique interaction known as ''bullet chat''. This real-time commenting system is actively moderated by an automated scoring mechanism known as the ''censor score'', which assigns each comment a value from 1 to 10, reflecting its likelihood of being censored based on content appropriateness. This study investigates how moderation on Bilibili varies according to user identity, emotional sentiment of comments, and topic sensitivity. By analyzing publicly available moderation data and employing large-scale sentiment classification methods, we discovered distinct moderation patterns. We found that severity of penalties differ substantially depending on the type of violation and the imposer of the penalty (System detect versus Human detect). Moreover, accounts with different certifications—such as government entities versus individual influencers—exhibit significantly different censor scoring trends even within the same discussion topics. Institutional accounts, generally received higher censor scores, suggesting variations in moderation standards tied to user identity and content context.
Author: Shen, Yang
abstract_manipulated: On the prevalent Chinese video-sharing platform Bilibili, the phenomenon of "bullet chat" emerges, wherein users engage in real-time commenting that traverses video streams or broadcasts. This distinctive interaction is under the purview of an automated scoring mechanism dubbed the "censor score", which assigns a numerical value from 1 to 10 to each comment, indicative of its susceptibility to censorship based on content appropriateness. Variations in moderation practices on Bilibili are explored with respect to user identity, emotional sentiment conveyed in comments, and sensitivity regarding discussion topics. Through the examination of publicly accessible moderation data and the application of extensive sentiment classification techniques, discernible moderation patterns have been unearthed. Noteworthy disparities in the severity of punitive measures are observed based on the nature of the transgression and the entity imposing the penalty (System detect versus Human detect). Furthermore, accounts with diverse certifications—such as governmental entities vis-à-vis individual influencers—manifest conspicuously distinctive trends in censor scoring even within identical discourse themes. Notably, institutional accounts tend to garner elevated censor scores, indicative of nuanced variations in moderation criteria contingent upon user identity and contextual content.

title: Papaya Privacy: Empowering Web Users through AI-enabled Cookie Consent and Data Monetization Tools
abstract: The current online advertising data ecosystem is broken. Web-users are repeatedly deceived by dark design patterns into clicking “Accept All” on cookie consent banners, unwittingly opting in to a myriad of unintended data flows. These data flows can have drastic consequences, ranging from targeted advertisement all the way to social oppression. To combat these problems, we present Papaya Privacy, a new way to empower web-users using AI-powered cookie consent and data monetization mechanisms. We designed and implemented two interventions, Agent Papaya and Papaya Payback. With Agent Papaya, our Agentic AI-powered chrome extension, we give users the ability to set their cookie preferences once and execute this seamlessly across websites, mitigating the effects of dark design patterns. With users now able to effectively opt-out of data collection, we conceptualize an alternative data market, Papaya Payback, based on the theories of Data Unions and Contextual Integrity. Through simulation, we show that by allowing users fine-grained control of who gets to access what data of theirs and for what purposes, the Papaya Payback market creates greater social welfare than the current online advertising data market. Together, Papaya Privacy’s two interventions demonstrate a practical path toward a consent-enforcing, user-centered, and economically viable data economy for online advertising.
Author: Kripa, Ram M.
abstract_manipulated: The prevailing ecosystem of online advertising data is deemed to be in a state of disrepair. Web-users are frequently ensnared by obscure design patterns, leading them to select the “Accept All” option on cookie consent banners, inadvertently subscribing to a plethora of unintended data streams. The ramifications of these data streams can be profound, spanning from tailored promotional content to societal subjugation. In order to address these issues, we introduce Papaya Privacy, a novel approach aimed at empowering web-users through the utilization of AI-enhanced cookie consent and data monetization mechanisms. Two interventions, namely Agent Papaya and Papaya Payback, were devised and put into operation. Through the implementation of Agent Papaya, an Agentic AI-infused chrome extension, users are endowed with the capacity to establish their cookie preferences once and have them seamlessly executed across various websites, thereby mitigating the impacts of obscure design patterns. With users now possessing the ability to opt-out of data collection effectively, we conceptualize an alternative data marketplace, Papaya Payback, founded upon the tenets of Data Unions and Contextual Integrity. Through the employment of simulation techniques, we demonstrate that by affording users intricate control over which parties are granted access to their data and for what purposes, the Papaya Payback market yields superior social welfare outcomes compared to the existing online advertising data marketplace. Collectively, the dual interventions offered by Papaya Privacy delineate a pragmatic trajectory toward a consent-centric, user-oriented, and economically sustainable data ecosystem for online advertising.

title: From Pixels to Preferences: Mixed Logit Demand Estimation Using Product Image Embeddings for E-Commerce
abstract: Accurately estimating substitution patterns in e-commerce is difficult because most demand models rely on hand-coded product attributes that are often missing or incomplete. I propose a multimodal-embedding approach that replaces those attributes with low-dimensional features extracted from product images and texts by pre-trained deep-learning models. Embedding principal components are entered—alongside price—into a mixed logit, allowing visual and textual similarity to discipline cross-price elasticities. Applied to 3,478 Amazon purchases in a 25-item Headsets category, adding just two image principal components from a ResNet-50 encoder lowers the Akaike Information Criterion by 296 points relative to a price-only logit and reduces the out-of-sample mean absolute error of market-share forecasts by 22%. Diversion ratios become more concentrated, raising the category-level Herfindahl–Hirschman Index from 0.073 to 0.088 (+21%), which reveals tighter competition within visually defined sub-segments such as mid-range gaming headsets. These results demonstrate that information already present in product pages can materially improve demand estimation, even when no structured attributes are available.
Author: Dong, Kaiwen
abstract_manipulated: The estimation of substitution patterns in the realm of e-commerce presents a daunting challenge due to the prevalent reliance of demand models on manually coded product attributes, which are frequently absent or incomplete. A novel approach is introduced in the form of a multimodal-embedding strategy that seeks to supplant these attributes with low-dimensional features extracted from product images and texts via pre-trained deep-learning models. The embedding principal components, in conjunction with price information, are incorporated into a mixed logit framework, thereby enabling the alignment of visual and textual similarities to govern cross-price elasticities. By applying this method to an assortment of 3,478 Amazon transactions within the domain of 25-item Headsets, the integration of merely two image principal components derived from a ResNet-50 encoder results in a noteworthy reduction of 296 points in the Akaike Information Criterion as compared to a price-centric logit model. Furthermore, this integration leads to a 22% decrease in the out-of-sample mean absolute error pertaining to market-share predictions. Notably, the diversion ratios exhibit a heightened concentration, culminating in an escalation of the category-level Herfindahl–Hirschman Index from 0.073 to 0.088, denoting a 21% increase and underscoring intensified rivalry within visually delineated sub-categories such as mid-range gaming headsets. These outcomes underscore the profound impact of leveraging existing data present on product pages to enhance the precision of demand estimation, even in scenarios where structured attributes are conspicuously absent.

title: Do Cryptocurrency Valuations Reflect Their Technological Innovation? Insights from Marketing, Security, and Code Levels
abstract: This thesis investigates how technological innovation influences the valuation of cryptocurrencies, focusing on the top 50 DeFi tokens by market capitalization. To capture the multifaceted nature of blockchain innovation, I construct three distinct indicators: a Whitepaper Innovation Index based on word embedding and clustering techniques, a standardized Audit Security Score derived from rubric-guided evaluation of audit reports, and a Code Maturity Proxy based on GitHub fork counts. These metrics are combined with financial data from CoinMarketCap and project-level metadata including blockchain architecture classification, academic involvement, historical volatility, and token age. Cross-sectional regression analysis shows that the proposed innovation indicators—while theoretically meaningful—do not exhibit statistically significant relationships with either market capitalization or trading volume. Instead, token age emerges as the most robust and consistent predictor across specifications, indicating that investor behavior is more responsive to project longevity than to technical complexity. Historical volatility is also negatively associated with market capitalization, suggesting that market participants tend to penalize assets with unstable pricing histories. The results suggest that, within the current market landscape, signals of maturity and stability outweigh detailed technical disclosures in shaping investor perception. This study contributes to the empirical literature by introducing a structured, multi-dimensional framework for evaluating technological innovation in crypto assets and by shedding light on the behavioral cues that dominate pricing dynamics in decentralized finance.
Author: Cui, Xiaotong
abstract_manipulated: This dissertation endeavors to explore the impact of technological innovation on the valuation of cryptocurrencies, with a specific focus on the top 50 DeFi tokens based on market capitalization. In order to capture the nuanced facets of blockchain advancement, three distinct indicators are formulated: a Whitepaper Innovation Index utilizing word embedding and clustering methodologies, a standardized Audit Security Score derived from rubric-guided assessment of audit reports, and a Code Maturity Proxy based on GitHub fork counts. These metrics are integrated with financial data sourced from CoinMarketCap and project-level metadata encompassing blockchain architecture categorization, academic engagement, historical volatility, and token age. Through cross-sectional regression analysis, it is revealed that the proposed innovation indicators, while conceptually significant, do not manifest statistically significant associations with either market capitalization or trading volume. Instead, token age emerges as the most robust and consistent predictor across various specifications, suggesting that investor behavior is more reactive to project longevity than to technical intricacies. Furthermore, historical volatility demonstrates a negative correlation with market capitalization, indicating a tendency among market participants to devalue assets with erratic pricing histories. The findings imply that, in the prevailing market environment, signals of maturity and stability take precedence over detailed technical disclosures in shaping investor perspectives. This investigation contributes to the empirical body of literature by introducing a structured, multi-dimensional framework for assessing technological innovation in crypto assets and by illuminating the behavioral signals that dictate pricing dynamics within decentralized finance.

title: The Effect of Monetary Policy Shocks on Stock Returns and Firm Characteristics in the US
abstract: This thesis studies the effect of monetary policy shocks on stock price change before and after FOMC meetings. Results show that contractionary monetary policy surprise cause negative asset returns on average, vice versus. I also found that reactions of individual stocks to monetary shocks are not fully explained by Fama French five-factor model. Then, results show that size, profitability, book leverage, current ratio, and current liabilities ratio are related to stock prices change induced by monetary policy surprise.
Author: Zou, Yang
abstract_manipulated: The impact of monetary policy shocks on fluctuations in stock prices surrounding Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) gatherings is investigated in this scholarly inquiry. The outcomes indicate that unexpected contractions in monetary policy tend to yield, on average, adverse asset returns, in contrast to the converse scenario. Moreover, it is revealed that the responses of individual stocks to monetary perturbations elude full explication by the Fama French five-factor model. Subsequently, the findings suggest that dimensions such as size, profitability, book leverage, current ratio, and current liabilities ratio are intricately linked to the variability in stock valuations induced by unanticipated monetary policy adjustments.

title: Displacement of Online Communities by Artificial Intelligence: The Impact of ChatGPT on Stack Exchange
abstract: This study explores the impact of ChatGPT on Stack Exchange's sustainability and community health by analyzing data from 40 communities from September 2021 to September 2023. In the community-level analysis, we used the difference-in-differences method, utilizing the previous year's data to construct the control group. We found the launch of ChatGPT significantly decreased the number of new posts on the platform, with the rollout of GPT-4 intensifying this effect. Additionally, our findings indicate notable heterogeneity in the impacts of GPT across various communities. Technology communities experienced the most significant negative effects. In the user-level analysis, we found the reduction in post numbers primarily resulted from decreased activity among users registered in the last two years. Subsequently, we used the Temporal Causal Inference (Turjeman and Feinberg, 2023) method using data from users who registered 1-6 months earlier to construct control groups and conducted regressions using the Difference-in-Differences and Generalized Synthetic Control Method. We identified the effect of GPT by comparing the number of posts from users with different registration times during the same registered duration. We found the negative GPT effect consistent with previous results, and it is more pronounced among users with higher community status and more social interaction.
Author: Xie, Hongzhang
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the implications of ChatGPT on Stack Exchange's sustainability and community well-being through an examination of data from 40 communities spanning the period from September 2021 to September 2023. Employing the difference-in-differences technique in our community-level scrutiny, we leveraged data from the preceding year to formulate the control group. Our analysis unveils a substantial reduction in the volume of new posts on the platform subsequent to the introduction of ChatGPT, with the deployment of GPT-4 exacerbating this phenomenon. Furthermore, our results suggest a discernible variability in the repercussions of GPT across diverse communities, with technology-oriented communities experiencing the most pronounced adverse consequences. In our user-level assessment, we observed that the decline in post frequency primarily emanated from diminished engagement among users who had registered within the preceding two years. Subsequently, we employed the Temporal Causal Inference method (Turjeman and Feinberg, 2023) utilizing data from users who had registered 1-6 months earlier to establish control groups and performed regressions employing the Difference-in-Differences and Generalized Synthetic Control Method. By juxtaposing the post volumes of users with varying registration durations during the same period of registration, we discerned the impact of GPT. Our analysis aligns with prior findings, indicating a negative effect of GPT that is particularly conspicuous among users with elevated community standing and enhanced social interaction.

title: Familiarity Breeds Complexity: The Impact of Brand Loyalty on E-Commerce Advertising and Promotion Decisions
abstract: This study investigates the impact of consumers' brand loyalty on sellers' promotional and advertising strategies on China's leading e-commerce platform, Taobao. Utilizing a unique dataset in 2013, the research examines how sellers adjust their behaviors in response to the proportion of familiar consumers, a direct measure of loyalty. The study extends Agrawal's (1996) game-theoretical framework, testing the hypothesis that seller with smaller proportion of loyal consumers increase promotional frequency and advertising spending as the loyalty size increase. The research innovatively addresses the challenge of reverse causality by using the platform algorithm as an instrumental variable. Empirical findings support the hypotheses, providing new insights into the relationship between brand loyalty and seller behavior in e-commerce. The paper contributes to the literature on firm responses to consumer loyalty, offering practical implications for marketing strategies in digital marketplaces.
Author: Hu, Yunshu
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the influence of consumers' brand allegiance on sellers' promotional and advertising tactics on Taobao, China's preeminent e-commerce platform. Drawing upon an exclusive dataset from the year 2013, the analysis scrutinizes how sellers adapt their actions in reaction to the ratio of familiar consumers, a direct gauge of loyalty. Extending the game-theoretical framework proposed by Agrawal (1996), the hypothesis is tested that sellers with a lower percentage of loyal consumers heighten promotional frequency and augment advertising expenditure with enlarging loyalty dimensions. The challenge of reverse causality is ingeniously tackled through the utilization of the platform algorithm as an instrumental variable. The empirical results lend support to the hypotheses, unveiling fresh perspectives on the interplay between brand loyalty and seller conduct in the realm of e-commerce. This paper enriches the existing literature on firm reactions to consumer loyalty, proffering pragmatic implications for marketing strategies within digital marketplaces.
