Result presents a strong and robust association of female representation and digital advancement, but the gender difference in adopting new digital technologies after entering the career is not salient.
Author: Zhou, Sirui
abstract_manipulated: The enduring issue of gender disparity persists across numerous industries, with a multitude of idiosyncratic and institutional factors under discussion. Despite this discourse, the examination of female representation in the realm of technology innovation remains circumscribed. Against the backdrop of the film industry's digital metamorphosis during the 1980s, an opportune context emerges for delving into the interplay between technological progress in the workplace and the segregation of genders in occupations. By leveraging observational data sourced from a corpus of over 20,000 films, this research seeks to quantify the historical trajectory of camera technologies and the evolving proportion of female personnel engaged in camera-related roles within the U.S. film sector over the past century. Given the unique milieu of the film industry, wherein directors wield the latitude to opt for conventional or avant-garde technologies, this study also illuminates the gendered disparities in technology adoption. Employing methodologies spanning quantitative enumeration, automated textual scrutiny, and regression analyses, this investigation endeavors to address two primary inquiries: 1) the potential deterrent effect of traditional technologies on female decision-making in career initiation, juxtaposed with the prospect that certain technological innovations might serve as a bridge to ameliorate this gap; 2) the presence of a gender gap in the uptake and utilization of novel technologies as individuals embark upon their careers. The findings unveil a robust correlation between female representation and digital progress, albeit the discernible gender differential in the assimilation of new digital technologies subsequent to career entry is not markedly pronounced.

title: Loneliness and Delay of Gratification: Understanding Childhood Instability
abstract: Chronic stress in early childhood can have negative implications for future life outcomes. This thesis analyzes the relationships between chronic loneliness, perceptions of crime, childhood instability, and classroom behaviors in children. To answer this research question, web scraping was used on Twitter to collect tweets related to the perception of crime in Elkhart, Indiana between June 2016 and May 2019. Topic modeling was conducted on the Twitter data to explore the evolution of topics over time. Crime offense data was collected from an FBI database to analyze actual crime rates in 2016 and 2018. A dataset from the CAPS educational program in Elkhart, Indiana was used to analyze relationships between chronic loneliness, delay of gratification, childhood instability, and other early childhood factors. Logistic and linear regression was conducted on the CAPS data to analyze relationships between the variables. The results indicate that: (i) the perception of crime in the community does not align with actual crime rates, and (ii) no significant relationship exists between childhood instability factors and self-regulation skills in children within the study. This thesis emphasizes the need for policy interventions in early childhood to alleviate chronic stressors that arise from internal and external instability factors in a child’s life. In turn, these interventions will allow for the improvement of future life outcomes in children with social instability.
Author: Bosko, Angelica
abstract_manipulated: The potential implications of chronic stress during early childhood on future life trajectories are investigated in this thesis. Connections are explored between chronic loneliness, perceptions of crime, childhood instability, and classroom behaviors in children within the context of Elkhart, Indiana. Utilizing web scraping techniques, Twitter data was passively collected to gather tweets pertaining to the perception of crime in Elkhart between June 2016 and May 2019. Through the application of topic modeling on the Twitter corpus, the temporal evolution of discourse themes was investigated. Concurrently, crime offense statistics were extracted from an FBI database to examine the empirical crime rates in 2016 and 2018. Furthermore, a dataset from the Comprehensive Assessment of Preschool Program (CAPS) in Elkhart was employed to scrutinize the associations among chronic loneliness, delay of gratification, childhood instability, and various early childhood determinants. Logistic and linear regression analyses were performed on the CAPS dataset to assess the interrelations among these variables. The findings tentatively suggest that: (i) the community's perception of crime diverges from the actual crime rates, and (ii) no statistically significant linkage is apparent between childhood instability factors and children's self-regulation capacities within the study sample. This thesis underscores the imperative for early childhood policy interventions to mitigate persistent stressors stemming from both internal and external sources of instability in a child's environment. Implementation of such interventions may potentially enhance the future life trajectories of socially unstable children.

title: Does China’s Levy Standard Reform Promote SO2 Reduction?
abstract: Using the quasi-natural experiment that adjusts the levy standards of sulfur dioxide (SO2) emissions in different Chinese provinces between 2007 and 2013, this paper examines the effect that such levy standard change exerts on pollution control by adopting the difference in differences (DID) model. Although the effect of the pollution charging policy in China is a widely debated issue due to the low per-unit charge and an “endogenous enforcement of the law”, this paper finds that the increase in pollution charges significantly reduced firms’ SO2 emissions by 27%. This finding remains robust after evaluation with the parallel trend test, placebo tests and other robustness check methods. Compared with state-owned enterprises (SOEs), non-state-owned enterprises are more likely to be driven by policies. In discovering influence mechanism, this paper finds that the increase of levy standard urged firms to cut back consumption of raw coal, fuel coal and heavy oil, while the consumption of clean gas has no significant increase. Only SOEs purchased and upgraded their desulfurization equipment after the reform. This paper also furthers discussion on the externality of the reform. This paper finds no evidence for pollution transferring, but confirms the reduction effect on air SO2 concentration from the levy standard reform.
Author: Chen, Yuxuan
abstract_manipulated: Utilizing a quasi-natural experiment that manipulated the levy standards of sulfur dioxide (SO2) emissions across various Chinese provinces from 2007 to 2013, the present study endeavors to investigate the impact of such levy standard alterations on pollution management through the application of the difference in differences (DID) model. The contentious nature of the pollution charging policy in China, attributed to the nominal per-unit charge and the presence of an "endogenous enforcement of the law," necessitates a nuanced exploration. Results suggest that the escalation in pollution charges notably decreased firms’ SO2 emissions by an estimated 27%. This finding withstands meticulous scrutiny through the employment of parallel trend tests, placebo tests, and assorted robustness check methodologies. Notably, non-state-owned enterprises exhibit a heightened responsiveness to policy stimuli in comparison to their state-owned counterparts. Mechanism analysis reveals that the upsurge in levy standards prompted firms to curtail the utilization of raw coal, fuel coal, and heavy oil, while the adoption of clean gas displayed negligible growth. Solely state-owned enterprises undertook the procurement and enhancement of desulfurization equipment subsequent to the regulatory reform. Furthermore, the study delves into the intricacies of the reform's externality, unearthing no substantiated indications of pollution displacement while affirming the reform's efficacy in reducing air SO2 concentration.

title: Embedding GitHub Repositories:  A Comparative Study of the Python and Java Communities
abstract: GitHub, the largest platform for open-source software, which allows code contributors to collaboratively develop software in a variety of programming languages, has motivated extensive research on social coding. However, the heterogeneity of GitHub communities in different programming languages has not been explored in previous studies. Inspired by the linguistic relativity hypothesis, I deduce that different programming languages might result in distinct patterns in social coding. This research identifies such discrepancies between the Python and Java communities based on repository representations (embeddings) from a unique, newly constructed dataset. By describing the representation learning process as a pipeline, this thesis first demonstrates how to generate high-quality repository embeddings from content and contextual data, including source code (import), readme text, and co-contributor networks. The evaluation results suggest that models derived from Word2Vec, including Doc2Vec, Import2Vec, and Node2Vec, are the most competitive for representing the GitHub data. I then used the best performing embeddings to explore language-specific patterns via questions regarding GitHub activities and success. By investigating the consistency between different embedding spaces, I identified how social (contributor) and functional (import, readme) embedding spaces diverge in the Python community but align in the Java community, implying the difference in their socio-functional mapping. Furthermore, the results indicate that functionally similar Python repositories experience more competition and that Python programmers contribute more to functionally diverse repositories when compared with their Java counterparts. Afterward, by analyzing the correlations between the functional diversity and the average popularity among contributed repositories, I found evidence that Python programmers who commit to dissimilar repositories are more likely to be the contributors of popular repositories, while Java programmers do not exhibit this pattern. Finally, by comparing embeddings with baseline features, I verified their potency to predict repository popularity and discovered that functional embeddings are beneficial for predicting Python repositories, while social embeddings contribute more to Java repositories.  These language-related heterogeneities can be attributed to the inherent difference in philosophy between Python and Java: As a language highlighting flexibility and reusability, Python treasures contributors’ ability to produce code for other coders with various functional needs, whereas Java focuses on the independence and thoroughness in programming, prioritizing specialized coding for passive end-users who value only the performance of final products.
Author: Chen Yutao
abstract_manipulated: The preeminent platform for open-source software, GitHub, on which code contributors collaborate to develop software in diverse programming languages, has spurred a wealth of research on social coding. Despite this, previous studies have neglected to investigate the heterogeneity of GitHub communities across different programming languages. Drawing on the linguistic relativity hypothesis, it is posited that distinct patterns in social coding may arise from the use of different programming languages. This study uncovers such disparities between the Python and Java communities by leveraging repository representations (embeddings) derived from a novel dataset. Framing the process of representation learning as a comprehensive pipeline, this thesis showcases the methodology for generating superior repository embeddings from content and contextual data, encompassing source code (import), readme text, and co-contributor networks. Evaluation outcomes indicate that models based on Word2Vec, such as Doc2Vec, Import2Vec, and Node2Vec, exhibit the highest level of competitiveness in capturing the nuances of GitHub data. Subsequently, these optimal embeddings are deployed to discern language-specific trends through inquiries into GitHub activities and achievements. By scrutinizing the coherence across diverse embedding spaces, the divergence between social (contributor) and functional (import, readme) embedding spaces within the Python community is distinguished, contrasting with the alignment observed in the Java community, thus implying a discrepancy in their socio-functional mapping. Moreover, the findings suggest that functionally akin Python repositories encounter heightened competition, with Python programmers contributing more to repositories with varied functionalities compared to their Java counterparts. Through an analysis of the relationships between functional diversity and the average popularity of contributed repositories, it is revealed that Python programmers engaging with dissimilar repositories are more inclined to contribute to popular repositories, a trend not mirrored among Java programmers. Notably, a comparison between embeddings and baseline features underscores the predictive power of embeddings in determining repository popularity, with functional embeddings proving advantageous in forecasting the popularity of Python repositories, while social embeddings are more influential in the case of Java repositories. These language-specific divergences are attributed to the inherent disparities in philosophy between Python and Java: Python, emphasizing flexibility and reusability, values contributors' capacity to produce code tailored to diverse functional requirements, whereas Java prioritizes independence and meticulousness in programming, with a focus on specialized coding for end-users who prioritize performance over all else.

title: Time preference and Willingness to Consume
abstract: Time preference is an important part of consumer decision patterns for different degrees of discounting indicates different weights placed on intertemporal values. We try to investigate whether economic confidence measuring expected future income and consumers’ current economic assets can affect their willingness to consume and what role time preference plays in the relationships. We surveyed 6,113 adults in the U.S. to collect related variables and fit the data into both linear regression models for estimating continuous changes and a logistic regression model for examining the categorical prediction power given our predictors and adding to the robustness. The results show that there are two channels for time preference to impact willingness to consume: one way is to moderate the relationships with future-oriented economic confidence and current assets; the other way is to directly affect people’s purchasing objectives, to make them put more weight on the things that need to buy now. Through these two channels, the increase of time preference is associated with more orientation to the future consumption and the decrease of it relates to the focus on the present, which is demonstrated as showing greater willingness to consume.
Author: Yanjie Zhou
abstract_manipulated: Time preference constitutes a crucial facet of consumer decision-making behaviors, wherein varying levels of discounting imply differential prioritization of intertemporal values. This inquiry endeavors to ascertain the potential influences of economic confidence, gauged through the measurement of anticipated future income and prevailing economic assets of consumers, on their proclivity towards consumption, as well as the nuanced role played by time preference within these dynamics. A sample of 6,113 adult participants in the United States was subjected to a comprehensive survey to capture pertinent variables, subsequently subjected to fitting procedures involving both linear regression models for the quantification of continuous adjustments and a logistic regression model to scrutinize the categorical predictive capacities, thereby enhancing the analytical robustness of our study. The findings underscore the existence of dual mechanisms through which time preference exerts its effects on consumption willingness: one pathway involves the modulation of associations with forward-looking economic confidence and current assets, while the other pathway entails a direct impact on individuals' buying inclinations, prompting a heightened emphasis on immediate purchase imperatives. Through these dual conduits, an escalation in time preference appears to correlate with an augmented orientation towards future consumption pursuits, whereas a decrement in time preference appears to foster a focus on present-oriented consumption tendencies, manifested through a heightened eagerness to engage in consumption activities.

title: Sources Matter: A Comparison of Fake News Datasets on Linguistic Feature Performance
abstract: Current fake news detection models often use distinct datasets in their respective train/test processes. This thesis focuses on textual detection models and explores whether classification performance can be affected by in- and between-dataset biases. The result confirms that biases exist and impact the efficacy of detection models.
Author: Wang, Miaohan
abstract_manipulated: The investigation conducted in this study examines the utilization of disparate datasets within the train/test processes of prevailing fake news detection models. Specifically concentrating on textual detection models, the inquiry delves into the potential ramifications of in- and between-dataset biases on classification performance. Empirical evidence gathered suggests the presence of biases that can potentially influence the effectiveness of detection models.

title: Gentrification Prediction with Modern Techniques
abstract: Gentrification has become a critical issue in urban planning during the past half century. Ruth Glass first coined the term ‘gentrification’ in 1964 to describe the phenomenon that wealthier class replace lower-class in certain neighborhoods in cities. Often gentrification is portrayed as a tool of revitalization for those declining urban areas. It was not until 1979 when the negative consequences of gentrification, known as displacement, was fully understood. The negative socio-economic consequences of displacement including income inequalities and injustices were further investigated and evaluated in modern literature. Governments and non-profit organizations have taken efforts to actively intervene when displacement occurs through various policy responses including job training, apprenticeships, subsidized and transitional employment programs, equal opportunity laws, community reinvestment strategies and more. Besides all the efforts taken by the government after gentrification is detected, successful prediction of gentrification can help identify displacement at an early time and guide policy interventions before they become more costly and less effective. Traditional statistical methods have been applied to forecast gentrification when the negative outputs of gentrification were first noticed and came to social scientists’ attention. In 1980s and 1990s, integrating data from various sources, neighborhood early warning systems were established in big cities, including Chicago Neighborhood Early Warning System, Neighborhood Knowledge Los Angeles, the Philadelphia Neighborhood Information System and so on. Modeling methods take advantage of these systems to provide predictive analysis mainly for municipal agencies. For instance, logistic regression is used to “analyze data from the Philadelphia Neighborhood Information System in order to determine which properties were most likely to become imminently dangerous”. Multivariate regression is also a simple while commonly used method to identify key factors that make a neighborhood to gentrify. These traditional techniques often perform poorly as common measurements of gentrification such as property value cannot be modeled linearly with other features using traditional methods. Some models also fail due to high correlations among variables or autocorrelations in residuals. Therefore, it is necessary to explore innovative predicting algorithms and evaluate their performances. Some studies have sought to employ new algorithms and have found out that techniques like machine learning usually outperform those traditional ones. This project explores how well a range of machine learning models perform on predicting neighborhood gentrification with socio-economic data rather than applying traditional statistical models.
Author: Gong, Yuqian
abstract_manipulated: Gentrification has emerged as a pivotal concern within the realm of urban planning over the past five decades. The term 'gentrification' was initially coined by Ruth Glass in 1964 to delineate the phenomenon wherein affluent classes supplant lower-income populations within specific urban neighborhoods. The prevalent narrative often depicts gentrification as a mechanism for rejuvenating deteriorating urban locales. It was not until 1979 that the adverse repercussions of gentrification, notably displacement, were comprehensively grasped. The adverse socioeconomic ramifications of displacement, encompassing income disparities and injustices, have been further scrutinized and assessed within contemporary literature. Regulatory bodies and non-governmental organizations have proactively endeavored to intervene when displacement ensues by implementing diverse policy measures, including vocational training, apprenticeship programs, subsidized employment initiatives, equal opportunity statutes, community reinvestment strategies, and the like. Despite the manifold governmental initiatives subsequent to the identification of gentrification, accurate prognostication of gentrification could serve as a means to forewarn against displacement at an incipient stage and guide policy interventions prior to their escalation and diminished efficacy. Traditional statistical methodologies were harnessed to prognosticate gentrification upon the initial recognition of its adverse outcomes and capture the attention of social scientists. During the 1980s and 1990s, neighborhood early warning systems were established in major urban centers through the amalgamation of data from diverse sources, such as the Chicago Neighborhood Early Warning System, Neighborhood Knowledge Los Angeles, and the Philadelphia Neighborhood Information System, among others. These systems were leveraged by modeling methodologies to furnish predictive analyses predominantly tailored for municipal agencies. For example, logistic regression was employed to "scrutinize data from the Philadelphia Neighborhood Information System to pinpoint properties most predisposed to imminent peril." Furthermore, multivariate regression, a rudimentary yet commonly utilized technique, was deployed to discern the pivotal determinants catalyzing neighborhood gentrification. However, these conventional techniques frequently exhibit suboptimal performance, as the linear modeling of gentrification indicators like property values alongside other variables proves challenging through traditional means. Certain models falter due to elevated inter-variable correlations or residual autocorrelations. Thus, there is a compelling imperative to explore pioneering predictive algorithms and evaluate their efficacy. Several studies have endeavored to deploy novel algorithms, revealing that methodologies such as machine learning consistently outshine conventional approaches. This investigation delves into the efficacy of a spectrum of machine learning models in prognosticating neighborhood gentrification utilizing socioeconomic data, deviating from the reliance on traditional statistical models.

title: Shared Poetic and Narrative Diction in Indo-European Epics: A Computational Analysis
abstract: In this paper, I investigate the possibility of a shared poetic and narrative diction distinct to epic within battle scenes in the two primary Indo-European epics: the Mahabharata and the Iliad. I use the Rig Veda and the Hesiodic corpus (the Theogony and the Shield of Heracles) as control texts, given that they derive from the same culture but belong to different genres. This investigation will attempt to (a) use quantitative methods to corroborate the longstanding hypothesis of literary and cultural Indo-European lineage, and (b) explore uncharted territory in mining structural similarities within large battle books that are difficult to hand-code. I used noun-adjective pairing frequencies and cosine similarity scores generated through SentenceBERT models to uncover poetic and narrative similarities in battle terminology shared between the texts. My overall findings suggest that patterns in poetic diction relating to battle terminology reveal both a stronger commonality of usage between the Indo-European texts as compared with the Near-Eastern influenced control corpus, and deeper commonalities between the two epics poetic diction concerning these battle terms than they share with other texts from within their separate cultures.
Author: Kanthan, Sruti
abstract_manipulated: This paper delves into the potential existence of a shared poetic and narrative lexicon that is unique to epic compositions within battle sequences in the primary Indo-European epics, namely the Mahabharata and the Iliad. The Rig Veda and the Hesiodic corpus (comprising the Theogony and the Shield of Heracles) serve as comparative texts to elucidate this distinction, originating from the same cultural milieu while representing diverse genres. Through the employment of quantitative methodologies, an effort is made to validate the enduring conjecture of a literary and cultural Indo-European continuum, alongside an exploration of untrodden methodological pathways focusing on extracting structural affinities within extensive battle passages that defy manual categorization. By analyzing frequencies of noun-adjective pairings and cosine similarity metrics derived from SentenceBERT models, this inquiry uncovers shared poetic and narrative affinities in the lexicon pertaining to battle scenarios across the texts under scrutiny. The collective outcomes tentatively indicate a heightened prevalence of linguistic congruence in battle-related diction between the Indo-European epics in contrast to the Near-Eastern influenced control corpus, as well as a more profound convergence in the poetic terminology of battle-related terms between the two epics compared to other textual sources within their respective cultural frameworks.

title: Tourists Have Come To Town: Examining New Users and Community's Norms
abstract: Every community has a love-hate relationship with newcomers. We rely on newcomers to bring in new perspectives, and to maintain activity level within the community. Yet, the fear among old members that the in uxes of newcomers would disturb the community's norm is also pervasive. This paper exploit a subscription policy on Reddit to study the effect of external user shocks on one of the music sharing groups. Data evidence shows that the number of posts and comments increased, but the proportion of posts removed by moderators also increased. The distribution of music genres shared in the community shifted greatly after the influx of new users. A great number of old members gradually existed the community, but those who remained quickly became accustomized to the new norms. At the end of the story, the community in my case study took the pain to survive the new users shock but they did survive.
Author: Ge,Nan
abstract_manipulated: The complex relationship between established community members and newcomers is a ubiquitous phenomenon. Newcomers are relied upon to introduce fresh perspectives and sustain community engagement, yet apprehension among existing members regarding potential disruptions to established norms is prevalent. This study leveraged a subscription policy implemented on Reddit to analyze the impact of exogenous user disruptions on a music-sharing group. Empirical data indicates a notable increase in both the volume of posts and comments, alongside a heightened rate of post removal by moderators. The compositional landscape of music genres shared within the community underwent significant shifts following the influx of new users. A substantial portion of long-standing members gradually disengaged from the community, while those who remained swiftly acclimated to the evolving norms. Ultimately, the community under investigation demonstrated resilience in navigating the challenges posed by the influx of new users, successfully adapting to the altered dynamics.

title: Effects of Rent Regulation on Housing Quality: Evidence from Manhattan
abstract: This study examines the effect of rent regulation on housing quality in Manhattan. In particular, this study investigates how a specific law change on rent increases due to home improvements in the Rent Act of 2011 affected the rental housing quality in Manhattan. Through combining multiple sources of data, including complaints received by the Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) and the Department of Buildings (DOB) and building information scraped from NYC public databases, I construct a novel longitudinal dataset with identifiable information of each building and the number of complaints each building receives as a measure of housing quality. Using a combination of regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences, this study shows that the specific law change in the Rent Act of 2011 has a negative effect on the housing quality of the associated rent-stabilized buildings in Manhattan. While previous literature has not reached an agreement on how rent regulation affects housing maintenance and improvements, this study aims to contribute to this discussion with novel data and analyses.
Author: Tan, Ruoyun
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the impact of rent regulation on housing quality within the borough of Manhattan. A particular focus is placed on the ramifications of a legislative alteration concerning rent escalations stemming from property enhancements as outlined in the Rent Act of 2011 on the overall standard of rental housing in Manhattan. By amalgamating various data sources, encompassing grievances lodged with the Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) and the Department of Buildings (DOB), as well as edifice particulars extracted from publicly available databases in New York City, a distinctive longitudinal dataset is meticulously formulated, containing discernible details pertaining to each edifice and the volume of complaints each edifice garners, serving as a proxy for housing quality. Employing a blend of regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences methodologies, this study unveils that the aforementioned statutory revision within the Rent Act of 2011 exerts an adverse impact on the housing quality of the affiliated rent-stabilized buildings in Manhattan. Despite the absence of a consensus in the extant literature regarding the influence of rent regulation on housing upkeep and enhancements, this study endeavors to enrich this discourse through the presentation of innovative data and analytical frameworks.

title: Higher-Order Innovation and New Venture Success
abstract: Entrepreneurship literature points out that a balance between exploration and exploitation leads to a good innovative performance of start-ups, yet how this balance can be achieved remains unknown. This study argues that the modularity principle, adopted from the complexity perspective, is the key to successful innovative strategies. By applying dynamic word embedding techniques to millions of documents from 119 business newspapers, magazines and patents, we construct a dynamic landscape of business discourse across 45 years. By locating new venture descriptions in this dynamic business landscape, we can observe how business elements are recombined within a given new venture and how innovative it was in its own time and historical context. Two different categories of recombination exist for each company: the first-order recombination that combines technical elements to approximate application elements to form basic functional blocks, and second-order recombination that recombines these blocks to satisfy more complex demands. Based on these measurements, we model the start-ups' birth, growth, and death with event history analysis. Our analysis reveals that radical second-order recombination is key to entrepreneurial success in the U.S.: the first-order process calls for exploitation while the second- order process requires exploration. Both exploitation and exploration matter for the new ventures, yet exploitation and exploration co-exist in innovative strategies through a specific architecture.
Author: Chen, Ziwen
abstract_manipulated: The extant literature on entrepreneurship underscores the necessity of achieving a harmonious equilibrium between exploration and exploitation for cultivating a robust innovative performance among nascent firms. Nonetheless, the precise mechanisms by which this equilibrium can be attained remain enigmatic. This investigation posits that the modularity principle, harnessed from a complexity-theoretic vantage point, serves as the linchpin for efficacious innovative strategies. Through the deployment of dynamic word embedding methodologies on a vast corpus comprising millions of textual artifacts sourced from 119 prominent business publications and intellectual property filings, a dynamic expanse of business discourse spanning a span of 45 years is meticulously delineated. By situating descriptions of nascent ventures within this dynamic business milieu, the manner in which constituent business elements are reconfigured within a given venture is scrutinized, offering insights into its innovative prowess vis-à-vis its contemporary zeitgeist. Two distinct typologies of reconfiguration manifest within each enterprise: primary reconfiguration, which amalgamates technical components to approximate application-oriented elements, thereby engendering rudimentary functional units; and secondary reconfiguration, which intermixes these units to fulfill more intricate exigencies. Leveraging these quantifications, the trajectory of birth, evolution, and demise of start-ups is modeled using event history analytics. Our examination unveils that radical secondary reconfiguration constitutes the linchpin of entrepreneurial triumph in the U.S.: where primary reconfiguration necessitates exploitation, the secondary process mandates exploration. Both exploitation and exploration are germane to the fortunes of emerging ventures, albeit they coalesce harmoniously within innovative strategies through a bespoke architectural framework.

title: Ridesharing and Public Transit: How has the Covid-19 pandemic affected the relationship?
abstract: The emergence of ridesharing services has transformed the shape of urban transportation and brought impacts on the traditional public transit system. While many previous studies have shed light on the relationship between the two transportation modes from a qualitative perspective, there has not been enough quantitative evidence to characterize the pattern thus far. The outbreak of the Covid-19 pandemic in late 2019 further raises interests in the role of ridesharing services under the public health crisis. This study extends the model proposed by Kong et al. 2020 to identify the potential substitution or complementary effects between ridesharing and public transit and further enriches the model’s interpretability by integrating a preference-based choice model. With real-world ridesharing data from Didi in Shenzhen, China, this study provides a quantitative measurement of the relationship between ridesharing and public transit. The spatial-temporal heterogeneity underlying the pattern is examined using linear and spatial regression designs. Using the ridesharing data of the pre and post-covid time, this study further investigates the shifts in the relationship and makes inferences about the change of passenger preferences under the Covid-19 outbreak. The results show that 2.20% of ridesharing trips have the potential of substituting for public transits in 2019, which slightly increases to 2.56% under the Covid-19 outbreak in 2020. The substitution effect becomes stronger during the morning and evening peak hours when public transit is overcrowded, and the complementarity effect is more evident during the night hours when transit routes are less accessible. The results also suggest that ridesharing exhibits a stronger substitution effect to public transit in the central business districts, which are associated with higher housing prices, higher intensity of POIs, more diversified land use, and better spatial coverage of transit stops, and it complements public transit in the areas that are less developed and underserved by public transits. The Covid-19 situation results in an upward shift in the potential substitution effect during the morning (+4.38%) and evening peaks (+3.08%), holding other factors constant, which indicates that ridesharing provides passengers with a utility premium under the Covid-19 situation by reducing the risk of virus exposure in a crowded transit cabin. The findings enrich the literature by providing quantitative support for the relationship and emphasize the important role of ridesharing services under the Covid-19 pandemic.
Author: Tian, Luxin
abstract_manipulated: The transformation of the urban transportation landscape by ridesharing services has had profound implications for the traditional public transit system. Although existing qualitative inquiries have offered insights into the interplay between these modes of transportation, a dearth of quantitative substantiation has characterized the discourse thus far. The onset of the Covid-19 pandemic in late 2019 has further stimulated curiosity about the functions of ridesharing services in the context of public health crises. Building upon the framework delineated by Kong et al. (2020), this investigation aims to discern the potential substitution or complementary dynamics between ridesharing and public transit, enhancing the model's interpretive capacity through the integration of a preference-based choice model. Leveraging empirical ridesharing data sourced from Didi in Shenzhen, China, this inquiry furnishes a quantitative appraisal of the interplay between ridesharing and public transit. The spatial-temporal intricacies underpinning this interplay are scrutinized using linear and spatial regression methodologies. By scrutinizing ridesharing data both pre- and post-Covid-19, this study further scrutinizes shifts in the dynamics and draws inferences regarding alterations in passenger preferences amidst the Covid-19 outbreak. The findings reveal that 2.20% of ridesharing journeys had the potential to substitute for public transit in 2019, a figure that marginally increased to 2.56% amidst the Covid-19 crisis in 2020. The substitution effect is particularly pronounced during peak morning and evening hours when public transit experiences congestion, while the complementarity effect is more conspicuous during nocturnal hours when transit accessibility is attenuated. Moreover, the outcomes indicate that ridesharing evinces a heightened substitution effect vis-à-vis public transit in central business districts characterized by elevated property values, a profusion of Points of Interest (POIs), diverse land use, and comprehensive spatial coverage of transit stations. Conversely, it complements public transit in underdeveloped areas lacking adequate transit services. The Covid-19 context engenders an amplification of the potential substitution effect during morning (+4.38%) and evening peaks (+3.08%), all else being equal, underscoring ridesharing's capacity to confer a utility premium to passengers by mitigating the risk of viral exposure within congested transit compartments. These findings contribute quantitative underpinning to the existing literature on this relationship, accentuating the pivotal role of ridesharing services amidst the Covid-19 pandemic.

title: Who Should Take Care of the Baby? Understanding How American Families Decide Their Division of Labor in Parental Leave in the Pre-birth Period
abstract: Based on Gary Becker's analytical tools—the family production model and the interdependent preferences—for family economics and the later criticisms regarding Becker's auxiliary assumptions, the study attempted to answer how American people's calculation of perceived benefits and costs could mediate the effects of economic factors and gender ideology regarding their parental leave norms during the pre-birth period. With data collected through an online survey on MTurk, the study revealed that male respondents in the survey included both the economic factors and gender ideology in calculating their expected gain and loss of family well-being in taking care of the children. For female respondents, only the gender ideology entered their functions of perceived utility. Moreover, regarding the choice of exiting work for full-time parenting, there was a partial mediating effect existed between male respondents' perceived benefits and their economic factors. By comparison, both perceived costs and benefits for female participants partially mediated the effect of gender ideology.
Author: Deng, Yehong
abstract_manipulated: Drawing upon Gary Becker's theoretical framework, specifically the family production model and interdependent preferences in the realm of family economics, and considering critiques of Becker's auxiliary assumptions, an investigation was conducted to explore the potential role of American individuals' evaluation of anticipated advantages and drawbacks in moderating the influence of economic variables and gender-related beliefs on their parental leave norms in the period preceding childbirth. Utilizing data gathered via an online survey distributed on the Mechanical Turk platform, the analysis unveiled that male respondents in the sample integrated both economic considerations and gender ideology into their calculus of anticipated gains and losses in familial well-being when undertaking childcare responsibilities. In contrast, female respondents predominantly factored gender ideology into their utility functions. Furthermore, in the context of opting to leave employment for full-time caregiving, a partial mediating effect was observed among male respondents between their perceived benefits and economic circumstances. Notably, both anticipated costs and benefits for female participants partially mediated the impact of gender ideology.

title: Weighted Sum Duration: A Point-of-Interest Based Approach for Fixation Duration Analysis in Eye-tracking Data
abstract: Many eye-tracking data analyses rely on the Area-of-Interest (AOI) methodology, which utilizes AOIs to analyze metrics such as fixations. However, AOI-based methods have some inherent limitations, including variability and subjectivity in the shape, size, and location of AOIs. This thesis proposes an alternative approach to the traditional AOI dwell time analysis: Weighted Sum Durations (WSD). This approach decreases the subjectivity of AOI definitions by using Points-of-Interest (POI) while maintaining interpretability. In WSD, the durations of fixations toward each POI are weighted by the distance from the POI and summed together to generate a metric comparable to AOI dwell time. To validate WSD, I reanalyzed data from a previously published eye-tracking study (N = 90). The re-analysis replicated the original findings that people gaze less towards faces and more toward points of contact when viewing violent social interactions.
Author: Rim, Nak Won
abstract_manipulated: Utilizing the Area-of-Interest (AOI) methodology is a common practice in the analysis of eye-tracking data, where AOIs are employed to assess fixations and related metrics. Nonetheless, AOI-based strategies exhibit inherent constraints characterized by the presence of variability and subjectivity in the configuration, dimensions, and positioning of AOIs. This dissertation posits an innovative paradigm vis-à-vis the conventional AOI dwell time analysis: Weighted Sum Durations (WSD). By leveraging Points-of-Interest (POI), WSD mitigates the subjective nature of AOI demarcations while upholding interpretive integrity. Within the framework of WSD, fixation durations towards each POI are assigned weightings based on their spatial distance, amalgamated to furnish a metric akin to AOI dwell time. To ascertain the efficacy of WSD, a reevaluation of data sourced from a precedent eye-tracking investigation (N = 90) was conducted. The reexamination corroborated the initial observations indicating a diminished fixation frequency towards facial features juxtaposed with heightened attention directed towards points of physical interaction during the observation of violent social scenarios.

title: Impact of Cares Act Stimulus on Consumption: Evidence from Zip Code Level Transactions
abstract: We study the causal effect of the CARES Act on consumer expenditure during the Covid-19 pandemic with the Facteus data set. We use the transaction data at the Zip code level of over twelve million bank cards in eighteen central states to estimate the treatment effect of the stimulus. This question is identified because of the geographic and temporal variation in receiving the deposit across Zip codes. Our empirical findings suggest that in the first weekend receiving the stimulus, the overall spending increase by 53%. In particular, the expenditure on essential and financial categories increases by 62% and 76%. Unlike the tax rebates in 2001 and economic stimulus payments in 2008, people spend most of the payments on non-durable goods and services such as grocery, utility, money order, Etc. The estimated effect is substantial for districts with lower education, lower white ratio, and higher poverty, where poverty generates the most striking heterogeneity.
Author: Yuan, Zhen
abstract_manipulated: The investigation of the causal impact of the CARES Act on consumer expenditure amidst the Covid-19 pandemic is undertaken utilizing the Facteus dataset. Transaction data at the Zip code level from a vast sample of over twelve million bank cards in eighteen central states is operationalized to estimate the treatment effect of the stimulus. This inquiry is motivated by the spatial and temporal disparities in the reception of the deposit across Zip codes. Empirical results potentially indicate that following the initial disbursement of the stimulus, overall spending experiences a substantial 53% surge. Particularly noteworthy is the 62% and 76% increment in expenditure within the essential and financial categories, respectively. In contrast to previous instances such as the tax rebates in 2001 and economic stimulus payments in 2008, a predominant portion of the disbursements is allocated towards non-durable goods and services encompassing groceries, utilities, money orders, and similar items. The estimated impact manifests significantly in districts characterized by lower educational attainment, diminished white population ratios, and heightened levels of poverty, with poverty exhibiting the most pronounced source of heterogeneity.

title: How Does Superhost Accreditation Benefit Airbnb Hosts in the City of Chicago?
abstract: In order to obtain a superhost accreditation, an Airbnb host has to meet a series of strict requirements, including a 90% response rate, 80% 5-star reviews, honor confirmed reservations, etc. However, is it worth the effort to get such an accreditation? How does the superhost status benefit the hosts? Based on signaling theory that effective information disclosure in markets can help provide positive encouragement and maintain benign market conditions, we assume that superhost status serves as a positive market signal, and thus can benefit the badge owner in some way. Choosing Chicago as our target city, where Airbnb hosts have earned tens of millions of dollars over the 5 biggest weekends in 2019, we take advantage of content analysis techniques, decision tree, random forest models, ordinary least squares (OLS) linear regression and further multilevel modeling to answer the question. Our findings confirm that superhosts do have significantly higher income in comparison with normal hosts. Nevertheless, such accreditation does not tend to bring up their rental price. One critical channel through which superhosts earn more consists in their capacity to maintain a relatively higher occupancy rate, which is achieved mainly through providing better accommodation services. Furthermore, even though Chicago has a notorious reputation for high crime rate and conspicuous residential segregation, neither the safety nor race issues reduce host revenues.
Author: Yongfei Lu
abstract_manipulated: In the pursuit of acquiring superhost accreditation on the Airbnb platform, hosts are required to satisfy a stringent set of criteria, encompassing a 90% response rate, 80% attainment of 5-star reviews, adherence to confirmed reservations, among additional stipulations. Nonetheless, the question arises: is the endeavor to attain such accreditation justified? What advantages does the superhost status confer upon hosts? Grounded in signaling theory, which posits that the efficacious disclosure of information within markets can serve to foster positive incentives and sustain favorable market conditions, it is posited that superhost status operates as a constructive market signal, thereby potentially yielding benefits for the badge bearer. Focusing on the urban locale of Chicago, renowned for its hosting of Airbnb ventures that have garnered substantial financial returns during the five most prominent weekends in the year 2019, we exploit content analysis methodologies, decision tree algorithms, the application of random forest models, ordinary least squares (OLS) linear regression techniques, and advanced multilevel modeling frameworks to elucidate this inquiry. Our empirical results substantiate that superhosts indeed realize markedly elevated revenue streams in comparison to their standard counterparts. Nonetheless, the acquisition of such accreditation does not appear to exert an upward influence on their rental pricing strategies. A pivotal avenue through which superhosts augment their earnings pertains to their adeptness in upholding a comparatively heightened level of occupancy rates, predominantly attributable to the delivery of superior lodging services. Furthermore, notwithstanding the stained reputation of Chicago for its elevated crime incidence and conspicuous patterns of residential segregation, neither issues pertaining to safety nor racial dynamics exhibit a detrimental impact on host financial gains.

title: Where do new ideas come from: New directions in science emerge from disconnection and discord
abstract: Since the 1950s, citation number or “impact” has been the dominant metric by which science is quantitatively evaluated. But research contributions play distinct roles in the unfolding drama of scientific debate, agreement and advance, which are differentially valued by scientists and their institutions. Computational power, access to citation data and an array of modeling techniques have given rise to a widening portfolio of metrics that extract different signals regarding their contribution to scientific activities. Besides impact, the innovation of work and the way that it builds up the scientific discussions are taken into consideration. Here we unpacks the complex, temporally evolving relationship between citation impact alongside novelty and disruption, two emerging measures that capture the degree to which science not only influences, but transforms later work. Novelty is measured at the point of production and captures how research draws upon unusual combinations of prior work. Disruption is measured over time and captures how research comes to eclipse or amplify the prior work on which it builds. We theorize that novel papers will exhibit disruptive impact over time, and demonstrate how they are much more likely than conventional papers to disrupt current literature. Novel papers do not do so immediately, but often become “sleeping beauties”, accumulating surprising attention and citation impact over the long run. In summary, new directions for science are created from a lack of consensus. Finally, we show how novelty can be reformulated as the combination of ideas across knowledge space to reveal the combinatorial nature of advance. The evolution of knowledge space over time characterizes how yesterday’s novelty forms today’s scientific conventions, which condition the novelty—and surprise—of tomorrow’s breakthroughs.
Author: Lin, Yiling
abstract_manipulated: Since the 1950s, the primary metric for quantitative evaluation of scientific endeavors has been the citation number, commonly referred to as "impact." However, research contributions fulfill distinct roles within the intricate tapestry of scientific discourse, consensus-building, and progression, each of which is variably esteemed by scientists and their respective institutions. The proliferation of computational capabilities, enhanced access to citation data, and a diverse array of modeling methodologies have engendered an expanding repertoire of metrics that unveil unique insights pertaining to their impact on scientific undertakings. In addition to assessing impact, the innovation inherent in scholarly works and their influence on the evolution of scientific dialogues is also taken into account. In this study, we elucidate the intricate, dynamically evolving nexus between citation impact, alongside novelty and disruption, two nascent metrics devised to encapsulate the extent to which scientific endeavors not only shape but also revolutionize subsequent research. The quantification of novelty occurs at the inception of a study and captures the extent to which research draws upon unconventional amalgamations of antecedent scholarship. On the other hand, the measurement of disruption extends over time, capturing the manner in which research either supersedes or magnifies the antecedent scholarly corpus on which it is founded. We posit that papers characterized by novelty are inclined to engender disruptive impact over time, illustrating their propensity to perturb existing scholarly discourse far more frequently than conventional papers. It is noteworthy that papers characterized by novelty do not instantaneously achieve this disruptive impact but often metamorphose into "dormant achievers," accruing unforeseen attention and citation impact in the long term. In summation, the forging of new trajectories within the realm of science often emerges from discordant viewpoints. Lastly, we explicate how novelty can be recontextualized as the amalgamation of diverse ideas across the expanse of knowledge space, thereby unveiling the combinatorial essence of scholarly advancement. The evolution of knowledge space over time delineates how yesterday's novelty crystallizes into today's scientific norms, which in turn shape the novelty—and unexpectedness—of tomorrow's paradigm-shifting breakthroughs.

title: Common sense reasoning against algorithmic misinformation using Knowledge Graph Embeddings
abstract: Knowledge Graph Embedding algorithms learn low-dimensional vector representa- tions for facts in a Knowledge Graph (where each fact is a relation edge between head and tail nodes). Learned embeddings are increasingly adapted for down- stream tasks (like question-answering, statement classification, recommendation engines, and even auto-completion of incomplete Knowledge Graphs). In the real world, training datasets of facts (in domains like news events, public records, or user actions) can be inaccurate due to sampling issues, bias, malicious intent, or noise. This makes it important to design for robustness to corrupted data. This paper investigates how two popular Knowledge Graph Embedding algorithms (TransE, DistMult) respond to deliberate corruption of training data, using the Freebase15K-237 dataset. This paper uses a novel true/false triplet classification evaluation to demonstrate the utility of learned embeddings as features for truth prediction, showing surprising robustness to corrupted data. It uses negative sam- pling to operationalize different types of common-sense reasoning in the stages of learning (model training) and testing (model evaluation) - demonstrating the usefulness of naive negative sampling strategies, contrary to prevailing intuitions in the literature. It uses this to frame a social science interpretation for the Knowledge Graph Embedding training process, with implications for designing for robustness against inaccuracies in Knowledge Graph data.
Author: Abubaker Kar, Aabir
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation examines the response of two prominent Knowledge Graph Embedding algorithms, TransE and DistMult, to intentional corruption of training data within the Freebase15K-237 dataset. Through the utilization of a novel true/false triplet classification evaluation method, this study showcases the potential of acquired embeddings as predictors of veracity, revealing a remarkable resilience to data corruption. Employing negative sampling techniques, the study operationalizes various forms of common-sense reasoning during both the training and evaluation phases, challenging prevailing assumptions in the literature and underscoring the efficacy of simplistic negative sampling strategies. This study offers a theoretical lens rooted in social scientific principles to elucidate the training dynamics of Knowledge Graph Embedding, offering insights into strategies for fortifying against inaccuracies within Knowledge Graph data.

title: Do Taxes Make You Drink Less Soda? A Difference-in-Differences Approach on the Cook County Sugar-Sweetened Beverage Tax
abstract: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) taxes have been implemented by various regions across the world and previous studies have demonstrated their effect of lowering SSB consumption. The current study taps into a unique SSB tax situation in Cook County, Illinois. By analyzing around 2,000 households located in Cook County and its adjacent counties using fixed effects and difference-in-differences estimation, the current research indicated an approximately 30% drop in SSB consumption after the tax went into effect and a full recovery in consumption after the tax was officially repealed.
Author: Xiong, Yinjiang
abstract_manipulated: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) levies have been operationalized in diverse global locales, with antecedent investigations demonstrating their impact on diminishing SSB intake. The present inquiry engages with a distinctive SSB levy scenario in Cook County, Illinois. Through the employment of fixed effects and difference-in-differences estimation methodologies on a sample of approximately 2,000 households situated in Cook County and its contiguous areas, the contemporary investigation revealed a reduction of approximately 30% in SSB consumption subsequent to the enforcement of the levy, followed by a complete recovery in consumption subsequent to the official annulment of the levy.

title: Embracing New Techniques in Deep learning for Predicting the Memorability of Images
abstract: Various work has suggested that the memorability of an image is consistent across people, and thus can be treated as an intrinsic property of an image. Using computer vision models, we can make specific predictions about what people will remember or forget. While older work has used now-outdated deep learning architectures to predict image memorability, innovations in the field have given us new techniques to apply to this problem. Here, we propose and evaluate five alternative deep learning models which exploit developments in the field from the last five years, largely the introduction of residual neural networks, which are intended to allow the model to use semantic information in the memorability estimation process. These new models were tested against the prior state of the art with a combined dataset built to optimize both within-category and across-category predictions. Our findings suggest that the key prior memorability network had overstated its generalizability and was overfit on its training set. Our new models outperform this prior model, leading us to conclude that Residual Networks outperform simpler convolutional neural networks in memorability regression. We make our new state-of-the-art model readily available to the research community, allowing memory researchers to make predictions about memorability on a wider range of images.
Author: Needell, Coen D.
abstract_manipulated: Diverse scholarship has implied that the memorability of an image exhibits consistency across individuals, thus potentially constituting an intrinsic feature of an image. Through the utilization of computer vision models, specific prognostications can be formulated regarding what individuals may retain or fail to retain. Whereas antiquated methodologies have previously been employed to prognosticate image memorability, recent advancements in the field have furnished us with novel methodologies to address this issue. In this study, we posit and scrutinize five alternative deep learning models that leverage advancements in the field over the past quinquennium, notably the advent of residual neural networks, which are purposed to enable the model to incorporate semantic information in the process of memorability estimation. These novel models were subjected to evaluation against the established state of the art through a composite dataset engineered to optimize both intra-categorical and inter-categorical prognoses. Our results tentatively suggest that the foundational memorability network had potentially overstated its capacity for generalization and was potentially overfitted to its training set. Our novel models exhibit superior performance to this antecedent model, prompting the inference that Residual Networks potentially outperform less sophisticated convolutional neural networks in the realm of memorability regression. We hereby offer our cutting-edge model to the scholarly community, enabling memory researchers to forecast memorability across a broader spectrum of images.

title: Mouse trajectories are reflective of who you are: regression analysis of cursor movements, attentiveness, and personality traits.
abstract: In this rapidly digitizing world, it is becoming ever more important to understand people’s online behaviors in both commercial and research settings. A cost-effective way to gain a deeper understanding of these behaviors is to examine mouse movement patterns. This research explores the feasibility of inferring personality traits from these patterns. Linear regression, correlation analyses, and PLS regression were performed to examine the relationships between cursor movement features such as pauses, fixations, and cursor speed, and two types of internal characteristics — general attentiveness and personality traits. Significant relationships were found between these features: mouse movement features such as clicks and fixations are significantly correlated with attentiveness, whereas attentiveness is significantly correlated with all five dimensions of the Big Five personality traits. Our results validate the feasibility of using mouse movement data to infer internal traits. These findings have possible applications in two realms: 1) the method presented here can be applied in research to filter inattentive participants, 2) commercial user experience research can apply the same method to gain insights on users’ personality to better cater to their demands .
Author: Niu, Tianyue
abstract_manipulated: In the context of rapid digitization, an increasing imperative arises to comprehend individuals' online behaviors within commercial and scholarly contexts. An economical approach to attaining a profound understanding of these behaviors involves scrutinizing mouse movement patterns. The present investigation delves into the potentiality of deducing personality traits from said patterns. Utilizing linear regression, correlation analyses, and PLS regression, an exploration was conducted to scrutinize the associations between cursor movement attributes, including pauses, fixations, and cursor speed, and two distinct categories of intrinsic characteristics — general attentiveness and personality traits. Noteworthy connections were discerned among these attributes: mouse movement features, such as clicks and fixations, exhibit significant correlations with attentiveness, which, in turn, manifests significant correlations with all five dimensions of the Big Five personality traits. Our outcomes affirm the viability of leveraging mouse movement data for inferring internal traits. These findings harbor prospective implications in dual domains: firstly, the methodological framework advanced herein may be implemented in research to sieve out inattentive participants; secondly, the commercial realm of user experience research may adopt the same method to glean insights into users' personalities, thereby enhancing the alignment of services with their preferences.

title: How does  U.S. Quantitative Easing policy affect stock prices in emerging economies? : Evidence from China
abstract: This paper estimates the effect of Quantitative Easing (QE) in the United States on the stock prices in emerging economies. We especially focus on China. The spillover effect of QE shock to stock markets in other emerging economies is also discussed. The estimation is done in two steps. We first select one best measurement of QE policy to be implemented in the subsequent analysis. Then we use the selected QE measurement to fit a structural VAR model to study the response of stock market in China to the QE policy shocks. We estimate the model with both short-run and long-run restrictions under various identification schemes to evaluate sensitivity of our results. Our empirical results show that the QE policy shock significantly increases the stock prices in China in both short and long term. Our analysis provides empirical evidence on the influence and transmission channel of unconventional monetary policies. Based on the result, we suggest that Chinese government should implement proper regulations to the domestic overheating stock market in reaction to the U.S. QE shock.
Author: Zhang, Qian
abstract_manipulated: This paper endeavors to estimate the impact of Quantitative Easing (QE) in the United States on stock prices within emerging economies, with a specific emphasis on China. The potential spillover effects of QE shocks on stock markets in other emerging economies are also deliberated upon. The estimation process unfolds in a meticulously orchestrated two-step progression. Initially, a meticulous selection process is undertaken to identify the optimal metric for QE policy implementation in subsequent analyses. Subsequently, the chosen QE metric is applied to configure a structural Vector Autoregressive (VAR) model, which serves as the vehicle for scrutinizing the stock market response in China to QE policy perturbations. The model's parameters are estimated through the imposition of short-term and long-term constraints, encompassing a range of identification strategies to gauge the robustness of our findings. Our empirical analyses unveil a noteworthy escalation in Chinese stock prices consequent to QE policy perturbations, a phenomenon that manifests across both immediate and protracted temporal horizons. These findings furnish empirical validation concerning the impact and dissemination mechanisms of unconventional monetary policies. In light of these outcomes, it is tentatively proposed that regulatory measures be considered by the Chinese government to address potential overheating within the domestic stock market in reaction to U.S. QE shocks.

title: How Digital Technologies are Transforming the Grocery Retailers? A Case Study of Consumer Repurchase Behavior with Instacart Data
abstract: The surge of grocery delivery has changed the grocery retailing industry. Especially due to the current severe situation of COVID-19, people cannot do regular grocery shopping. More and more consumers are turning to the companies in the sharing economy for cost-eective access to goods and services including groceries. In this paper, I use Instacart, an online food-delivery company that does sameday delivery, as an example. I mainly discuss the impact of Instacart on grocery retailers. Specically, I will develop machine learning to build a model to show how to understand the consumer behavior better. In addition, I show some possible solutions with the aid of big data for grocery retailers to problems like balancing demands and supplies.
Author: Jiang, Luying
abstract_manipulated: The grocery retailing industry has been significantly impacted by the proliferation of grocery delivery services, particularly exacerbated by the ongoing COVID-19 crisis, which hampers traditional grocery shopping practices among consumers. A growing number of individuals are gravitating towards enterprises within the sharing economy to avail themselves of cost-efficient access to various goods and services, including groceries. Leveraging Instacart as a case study, an online food delivery platform renowned for its same-day delivery capabilities, this paper delves into the ramifications of Instacart's operations on the landscape of grocery retailers. The primary focus is the deployment of advanced machine learning techniques to construct a model aimed at enhancing comprehension of consumer behavior patterns. Furthermore, potential strategies leveraging the power of big data analytics are proposed as means for grocery retailers to navigate challenges such as demand-supply equilibrium.

title: Why are IPOs Always Underpriced? An Empirical Study of Behavioral Economics
abstract: IPO underpricing has become one of the most famous market anomalies in the modern financial market. This phenomenon, called the "New Issue Puzzle," stimulated a hot debate among economists. Many of them focused on explaining the cause of this anomaly using the classical economics models. It is relatively novel to explain this anomaly based on behavioral economics theories. This paper took a comprehensive perspective, considered two possible stages that the underpricing derived from: the pricing stage and the trading stage. For each stage, the paper proposed two hypotheses based on the behavioral economics theory and examined the hypotheses with the OLS regression model and the most up-to-date machine learning techniques (KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forests, and Gradient Boosting). The results demonstrate that the underpricing level of IPO is positively correlated to managerial confidence. This furtherly implies that the underpricing of IPOs is partially derived from the behaviors in the pricing stage. Besides, the result of four classification models validates that investor sentiments would contribute to the underpricing. Using company features and investor sentiments extracted from the online discussion could classify whether the IPO will be underpriced or not at a precision level of about 74%.
Author: Yanwei Pan
abstract_manipulated: IPO underpricing has emerged as a prominent market anomaly within the modern financial landscape. Termed the "New Issue Puzzle," this phenomenon has sparked intense debate among economists, who have predominantly endeavored to elucidate its origins through classical economic frameworks. Notably, a relatively nascent approach involves explicating this anomaly through the lens of behavioral economics theories. This study adopts a comprehensive vantage point, delving into two potential stages from which underpricing may stem: the pricing stage and the trading stage. Proffering two hypotheses for each stage rooted in behavioral economics theory, the investigation scrutinizes these hypotheses utilizing the OLS regression model and cutting-edge machine learning methodologies (including KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forests, and Gradient Boosting). Findings indicate a positive correlation between the level of IPO underpricing and managerial confidence, suggesting that underpricing is partially underpinned by behaviors in the pricing stage. Additionally, outcomes from four classification models underscore the role of investor sentiments in driving underpricing. Leveraging company attributes and investor sentiments gleaned from online discourse, the study achieves a precision rate of approximately 74% in classifying whether an IPO will be underpriced.

title: Understand the Impact of Social Vulnerability and Other Socioeconomic Factors on Childhood Asthma Rate in the Chicago Area
abstract: This research uses the Social Vulnerability Index, a measure of urban residential and environmental data, along with socioeconomic factors gathered, cleaned, and aggregated by the Center for Spatial Data Science at the University of Chicago to construct spatial models to understand the concentrations of Childhood Asthma emergency department (ED) visits rates in the Chicago Area. Applying Machine Learning models including Random Forest, Ada-boost, and Neural Network, I predicted the Childhood Asthma ED visits rates at census tracts resolution. The results, which are useful in respiratory and epidemiological studies, can provide more information about the conditions of Childhood Asthma than the previous ZIP Code Tabulation Areas (ZCTA) resolution data.
Author: Liu, Yuming
abstract_manipulated: Utilizing the Social Vulnerability Index as a metric of urban residential and environmental data, in conjunction with socioeconomic factors curated, sanitized, and consolidated by the Center for Spatial Data Science at the esteemed University of Chicago, spatial models were crafted to comprehend the distributions of Childhood Asthma emergency department (ED) visitation rates within the Chicago Area. Through the application of Machine Learning methodologies, specifically Random Forest, Ada-boost, and Neural Network algorithms, prognostications were made regarding Childhood Asthma ED visitation rates at a granular census tracts resolution. The outcomes, which hold potential significance within the realms of respiratory and epidemiological inquiries, possess the capacity to furnish deeper insights into the manifestations of Childhood Asthma compared to the antecedent ZIP Code Tabulation Areas (ZCTA) resolution data.

title: A Noisy Channel Model of N400 and P600 Effects in Sentence Comprehension
abstract: N400 and P600 event-related potential (ERP) components have long been the object of study in psycholinguistics. Traditional accounts have associated N400 effects with semantic violations, and P600 effects with syntactic violations. However, this picture is complicated by P600 effects—without N400 effects—in response to animacy and thematic- role violations, as well as biphasic N400/P600 effects for conventional semantic violations. Building on explanations involving interplay of plausibility- driven and syntax-driven interpretations, we present a computational model that accounts for these complicating observations via a noisy channel modeling framework. Our model assumes early-stage sentence interpretations determined by noisy channel computation (influenced by plausibility), with these heuristic interpretations driving the N400 amplitude. The P600 then reflects a reconciliation with the true (syntax-driven) interpretation, impacted by the extent to which heuristic interpretations deviate from the true input. Running this model on original experimental stimuli, we successfully simulate N400 and P600 effects reported by eight studies in the literature that we examined. To our knowledge this is the first fully-specified computational formalization of plausibility/syntax interplay, the first implemented noisy channel model to carry out direct prediction of both N400 and P600 components.
Author: Li, Jiaxuan
abstract_manipulated: The N400 and P600 event-related potential (ERP) components have long been the subject of investigation within the field of psycholinguistics. Traditional theoretical frameworks have linked N400 effects to semantic incongruities and P600 effects to violations of syntactic structure. However, the complexity arises from the emergence of P600 effects—disassociated from N400 effects—in response to violations related to animacy and thematic roles, alongside the presence of biphasic N400/P600 effects in cases of conventional semantic incongruities. Drawing upon explanatory frameworks that consider the interplay between plausibility-driven and syntax-driven interpretations, we introduce a computational model that accommodates these intricate observations through the utilization of a noisy channel modeling framework. Our model hypothesizes that initial sentence interpretations are established through noisy channel computations (influenced by plausibility), with these provisional interpretations shaping the amplitude of the N400 component. Subsequently, the P600 component signifies a realignment with the genuine (syntax-driven) interpretation, with its manifestation influenced by the degree to which provisional interpretations deviate from the actual input. By applying this model to original experimental stimuli, we effectively replicate the N400 and P600 effects documented in eight studies from the examined literature. To the best of our understanding, this represents the inaugural fully-specified computational formalization of the interplay between plausibility and syntax, as well as the first instance of an implemented noisy channel model that directly predicts both the N400 and P600 components.

title: The Clustering of Chicago - a Long Term Perspective
abstract: The purpose of this research lies in two aspects. The first is to carry out an in-depth comparison of the clustering results obtained from different methods, so as to find the “optimal” clustering method. The second is to conduct cluster analysis of Chicago utilizing the census tracts data from three years 1950, 1980 and 2010, aiming to understand the spatio-temporal transitions of Chicago neighborhoods across the years. This study is both a theoretical investigation and a methodological investigation. The results indicates K-means clustering using principal components proved to be the optimal method in terms of the measures we chose, and the longitudinal change in spatial patterns of Chicago resembled the description of Chicago School theorists, while some brand-new trajectories were also detected.
Author: Lai, Yutian
abstract_manipulated: The dual purpose of this investigation involves a comprehensive comparison of clustering outcomes derived from divergent methodologies to ascertain the most efficacious clustering approach.
