Additionally, cluster analysis of Chicago is undertaken utilizing census tracts data from the years 1950, 1980, and 2010, with the goal of comprehending the spatio-temporal evolutions of Chicago neighborhoods over the temporal continuum. This inquiry serves as both a theoretical exploration and a methodological inquiry. The outcomes suggest that the application of K-means clustering with principal components emerged as the optimal technique based on the selected metrics, and the longitudinal shifts in spatial configurations of Chicago bore semblance to the postulations of Chicago School theorists, albeit with the identification of novel trajectories.

title: Risk Transmission Mechanism across Industries in China
abstract: Learning how the financial risk has been transmitted between industries in China before and after some big events is a crucial topic in risk manage- ment. Due to the large number of industries in China, the market can be regarded as a high-dimension network. To conduct the empirical research on risk transmission mechanism in China based on daily data of the SWS (Shen Wan Security) secondary industry index, I utilize the LASSO algorithm to compress, select and estimate variables and build a high dimensional VAR model and calculate the pairwise risk connectedness between different in- dustries. With the help of network analysis, I visualize the outcome of the VAR-Lasso model. Then, both full sample estimation and rolling window estimation are applied to provide static analysis and dynamic analysis on the risk transmission network. Based on the analysis, clustering character- istics can be easily found in risk transmission of the market, especially be- tween industries in the same industrial chain or between industries that are closely connected. Particularly, Oil exploitation, Insurance, Banking, and Railway Transportation are functioning as an efficient intermediary nodes in the whole transmission process. As for dynamic analysis, the overall risk connectedness reaches the summit during the great stock crisis in 2015 and the shock of COVID-19. Comparisons are made on the risk transmission network before and after those big events.
Author: Zhaoyang, Chen
abstract_manipulated: The investigation of how financial risk transmission occurs between industries in China, both pre- and post-major events, is a critical aspect of risk management. Given the vast array of industries in China, the market is conceptualized as a high-dimensional network. Empirical examination of the risk transmission mechanism in China is undertaken utilizing daily data from the SWS (Shen Wan Security) secondary industry index. The LASSO algorithm is employed to compress, select, and estimate variables, culminating in the construction of a high-dimensional VAR model that facilitates the calculation of pairwise risk connectedness among industries. The outcomes of the VAR-Lasso model are visually represented through network analysis. Subsequently, analyses encompassing full sample estimation and rolling window estimation are executed to furnish both static and dynamic examinations of the risk transmission network. The analysis reveals discernible clustering patterns in risk transmission within the market, particularly evident among industries within the same industrial chain or those closely interconnected. Notably, Oil exploitation, Insurance, Banking, and Railway Transportation emerge as efficacious intermediary nodes in the overall transmission process. In terms of dynamic analysis, the overall risk connectedness peaks during the significant stock crisis in 2015 and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. Comparative assessments are made between the risk transmission network pre- and post-occurrence of these major events.

title: The  Effects of Health Insurance Reimbursement Methods on Health Care Expenditures: Evidence from China
abstract: For health insurance policies, immediate reimbursement and later reimbursement can have different effects on people's total health care expenditures. Utilizing the establishment of the provincial online systems for settlement of intra-provincial medical bills, this paper constructs two instrument variables and implements a triple difference-in-difference model to identify the effect of reimbursement methods on health care expenditures. With a cross-sectional data set of migrants' inpatient expenditures and a panel data set of outpatient expenditures, the estimates indicate that immediate reimbursement significantly increases total inpatient expenditures by 78% and increases total outpatient expenditures by 128% on average.
Author: Zheng, Yiqing
abstract_manipulated: In the realm of healthcare insurance policies, the mode of instant reimbursement versus delayed reimbursement may yield disparate impacts on individuals' overall healthcare outlays. By leveraging the advent of provincial online systems for the settlement of intra-provincial medical invoices, this study formulates two instrumental variables and operationalizes a triple difference-in-difference model to discern the influence of reimbursement modalities on healthcare expenditures. Drawing upon a cross-sectional dataset of migrants' inpatient spending and a panel dataset of outpatient outlays, the findings suggest that immediate reimbursement is associated with a notable 78% average escalation in total inpatient expenditures and a substantial 128% average rise in total outpatient expenditures.

title: Does crisis turn into opportunity?
abstract: This study estimates the effect of manager challenge in Major League Baseball to test whether momentum exists in sports. Despite much research over the last 30 years, the existence of momentum in sports remains controversial. Productivity of teams, measured by the number of runs in the inning, is analyzed by using 1,193 replay reviews during 2015-2019 regular season American League home games. Using a difference-in-differences methodology, this study fails to find statistically significant causal effect of winning a manager challenge on the team’s productivity on subsequent innings. However, there could still be a shorter- or longer-term momentum as this study analyzes the existence of a medium-term momentum that lasts for subsequent two innings. The finding could be generalized to other zero-sum settings where agents have option to challenge.
Author: Kim, Hyun Ki
abstract_manipulated: This investigation seeks to estimate the impact of managerial challenges in Major League Baseball with the aim of examining the potential presence of momentum dynamics within the realm of sports. Despite the extensive body of research spanning the past three decades, the contentious debate surrounding the manifestation of momentum in sports persists. The operationalization of team productivity, as quantified by the magnitude of runs scored in a single inning, is meticulously scrutinized through the analysis of 1,193 replay reviews conducted during American League home games throughout the regular seasons of 2015 to 2019. Employing a rigorous difference-in-differences approach, this study does not yield empirical evidence supporting the presence of a statistically significant causal relationship between the successful outcome of a managerial challenge and the subsequent productivity of the team in subsequent innings. Nonetheless, it is plausible that an underlying momentum effect, whether of a truncated or protracted nature, may potentially be discerned, given the examination of a medium-term momentum that spans over the subsequent two innings. The implications of these findings may be transferrable to analogous zero-sum contexts wherein decision-makers are presented with the option to initiate challenges.

title: Assessing Personality Traits Through Social Media Language
abstract: With the emergence of online social media, people increasingly leverage these platforms to build connections and express themselves. In particular, our language reflects the increasing emphasis on personal agency and expression. In this paper, we explored whether language can be a strong predictor of Big Five personality traits on Yelp, a site in which there is no expectation of building a socially desirable profile as do other social media. The linear models of lexical correlates suggest that users that produce high-quality reviews show higher Extraversion, Agreeableness, Openness, Conscientiousness, and lower Neuroticism. Feature importance suggests that low-quality reviews show a higher degree of egocentricity while high-quality reviews are more socially oriented.
Author: Hu, Chun
abstract_manipulated: The emergence of online social media has led individuals to increasingly utilize these platforms for the purpose of establishing connections and articulating their thoughts and feelings. Of particular interest is the manner in which our linguistic expressions manifest a growing emphasis on individual agency and self-expression. This paper delves into the inquiry of whether language possesses the capacity to serve as a robust predictor of the Big Five personality traits within the context of Yelp, a platform where the expectation of constructing a socially desirable persona is not prevalent as seen in other forms of social media. Through the application of linear models analyzing lexical correlations, it is posited that individuals who generate reviews of exceptional quality exhibit heightened levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Openness, Conscientiousness, and diminished levels of Neuroticism. Furthermore, the analysis of feature importance suggests that reviews of substandard quality tend to exhibit a greater degree of egocentricity, while those of superior quality are characterized by a more socially-oriented approach.

title: Is There a Predictability Hierarchy in Reference Resolution?
abstract: The concept of accessibility has often been evoked to explain reference resolution. According to the Givenness Hierarchy theory, a referent’s accessibility in the mental state of a comprehender is encoded in the form of the referent as part of its lexical semantic representation. However, the current literature has not reached a consensus on what accessibility exactly means and how to best quantify it. The factors that modulate accessibility show a great extent of overlap with another independently motivated concept—predictability—raising the possibility of a “Predictability Hierarchy” that mirrors Givenness Hierarchy. Using a self-paced reading experimental design, the current study examines whether there is such a “Predictability Hierarchy,” by manipulating the predictability and the form of the referent presented to the participants. Our results indicate that although there is no strong evidence for approximating the Givenness Hierarchy with a “Predictability Hierarchy,” there is some preliminary evidence for a partial correlation between the form and the predictability of a referent.
Author: Xu, Weijie
abstract_manipulated: The evocation of the concept of accessibility has frequently been undertaken in the context of reference resolution. In accordance with the Givenness Hierarchy theory, the encoding of a referent's accessibility in the mental state of a comprehender is manifested in the form of the referent as an integral component of its lexical semantic representation. Nevertheless, a consensus on the precise definition of accessibility and the optimal method for its quantification has not been definitively reached within the existing body of literature. The factors that govern accessibility exhibit substantial overlap with another concept that is independently motivated, namely predictability, thereby eliciting the potential emergence of a "Predictability Hierarchy" that parallels the Givenness Hierarchy. Employing a self-paced reading experimental paradigm, the present investigation aims to assess the existence of such a "Predictability Hierarchy" by manipulating both the predictability and the form of the referent provided to the participants. The findings of our study suggest that while compelling evidence supporting the approximation of the Givenness Hierarchy with a "Predictability Hierarchy" is lacking, there is initial support for a tentative association between the form and predictability of a referent.

title: Identifying the relationship between US monetary policy and the VIX futures: An empirical characterization using VAR methodology
abstract: New Keynesian theory believes the central bank control over the real interest rate both in the short run and the long run due to the price stickiness. The expectation on the rental rate of capital is reflected by the real interest rate. Therefore, the return on the assets, determined by the expected future dividends, can be heavily influenced by the real interest rate. Vector autoregressive model (VAR) has been widely used to address issues regarding both identification and the impact of monetary policy. This paper aims to investigate the interdependence between asset prices and monetary policy in the VAR model, combining both traditional short-run and long-run restrictions. Instead of using stock prices as indicators of monetary policy stance, this paper introduced VIX futures price which may provide additional forecasting information in either the determinants of target variables or the target variables. The principal component analysis is implemented to reduce the dimensions of VIX futures data. According to the outcome, a contractionary monetary policy shock raises the real interest rate and lowers the output. In addition, it also reduces asset prices. In response to the changes in the real interest rate, there is likely a positive financial market shock.
Author: Zheng, Zijun
abstract_manipulated: The control exerted by the central bank over the real interest rate, as posited by New Keynesian theory, is believed to operate both in the short run and the long run, owing to the presence of price stickiness. The anticipation of the rental rate of capital manifests in the real interest rate, thereby potentially exerting a significant impact on asset returns, which are contingent upon expected future dividends. The application of the Vector Autoregressive (VAR) model has been prevalent in addressing issues pertaining to the identification and implications of monetary policy. This research endeavors to explore the interconnectedness between asset prices and monetary policy within the VAR framework, amalgamating traditional short-run and long-run constraints. Departing from the conventional use of stock prices as proxies for the stance of monetary policy, this study introduces VIX futures prices, which hold promise in furnishing supplementary predictive insights in relation to either the determinants or the target variables. Principal component analysis is harnessed to streamline the VIX futures data dimensions. The findings indicate that a contractionary monetary policy shock heightens the real interest rate, dampens output, and diminishes asset valuations. Moreover, it is anticipated that adjustments in the real interest rate could potentially trigger a favorable financial market shock.

title: The Analysis of Gender-Bias towards Children in Amazon Customer Reviews
abstract: Girls have been biased against throughout history. Although progress in gender equality has been made, such biases still exist all over the world. But, unlike in the past, when gender biases towards kids were explicitly shown, nowadays, people may unconsciously or implicitly hold gender biases towards children. These biases are embedded in their words and are not easily revealed. Utilizing Customer data from Amazon customer reviews and a word embedding algorithm, I analyze whether there are gender biases and  stereotypes towards children within a close relationship and within the general society explicitly and implicitly. I also examine how such biases occur, whether it is because of the positive bias against boys or negative bias against girls. Finally, I show that if gender biases and stereotypes towards children do exist, how they differ between the US and the UK.
Author: Wu, Yuxin
abstract_manipulated: Throughout historical chronicles, females have encountered substantial prejudice. Despite advancements in gender parity, such biases persist globally. In contrast to previous eras characterized by overt manifestations of partiality towards juveniles based on gender, contemporary manifestations may potentially operate at a subconscious or implicit level. These manifestations are deeply ingrained in verbal expressions, eluding facile detection. Leveraging consumer data gleaned from Amazon reviews and employing a word embedding algorithm, an investigation is conducted to ascertain the presence of gender biases and stereotypes towards minors within intimate relationships and society at large, both overtly and covertly. The genesis of such biases is scrutinized, whether stemming from a favorable predisposition towards boys or a detrimental inclination towards girls. Ultimately, an analysis is presented highlighting potential disparities in the prevalence of gender biases and stereotypes towards children between the United States and the United Kingdom.

title: The Effect of Asian Ethnic Community Size on the Asian-White Achievement Gap in the U.S.
abstract: This study seeks to examine the effect of the size of Asian ethnic communities on the Asian-White achievement gap between 2013 and 2017 in U.S. school districts. Ordinary least squares regressions were used to assess this relationship, while controlling for other district characteristics that have been empirically shown to have an association with the gap. It was found that inconsistent with the model minority theory, Asian Americans do not always have an academic advantage over time. The present study also does not directly support the selective assimilation theory, as larger Asian ethnic communities in a district are not associated with a greater Asian-White gap.  However, a negative and significant relationship was found between the size of Asian ethnic communities and the academic achievement of White students.
Author: Hu, Anqi
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation endeavors to operationalize the impact of Asian ethnic community magnitudes on the Asian-White educational disparity within U.S. educational jurisdictions spanning the years 2013 to 2017. Utilizing ordinary least squares regressions, this inquiry aimed to scrutinize this interplay, while controlling for additional district attributes that have been previously substantiated to possess a correlation with the aforementioned gap. Contrary to the premises of the model minority hypothesis, it was discerned that the academic advantage of Asian Americans is not consistently maintained across temporal iterations. Furthermore, the current inquiry does not unequivocally buttress the selective assimilation theory, given that augmented Asian ethnic enclaves within a district do not manifest a commensurate exacerbation of the Asian-White scholastic discrepancy. Nonetheless, a statistically significant inverse relationship was ascertained between the proportions of Asian ethnic communities and the academic performance of White students.

title: Company Announcements and Stock Returns
abstract: Employing a novel text mining method proposed by Ke et al. (2019), I carry out a comprehensive textual analysis of Chinese company announcements for stock return prediction. The long-short portfolio based on article sentiment scores yields 78% cumulative returns with a Sharpe ratio of 0.86 from 2016 to 2019. The model well captures the contemporaneous association between announcement sentiment and stock returns. There is also evidence for the information leakage before the announcements are made public. Careful examination of the traded articles finds that they are longer than average, suggesting complex information may not be fully digested by the investors as they are released. Company annual reports and financing activities reports play important roles in explaining the portfolio's performance.
Author: He, Songrun
abstract_manipulated: Utilizing an innovative text mining technique introduced by Ke et al. (2019), a comprehensive textual analysis is conducted on Chinese company announcements with the aim of predicting stock returns. The construction of a long-short portfolio, predicated upon sentiment scores extracted from the articles, results in the generation of 78% cumulative returns, accompanied by a Sharpe ratio of 0.86 during the period spanning from 2016 to 2019. The model effectively captures the concurrent relationship between announcement sentiment and stock returns. Furthermore, indications exist of potential information seepage prior to the public announcement dissemination. A meticulous scrutiny of the articles traded reveals their above-average length, intimating that intricate information possibly remains incompletely assimilated by investors upon release. Company annual reports and financial activities documentation emerge as pivotal factors in elucidating the performance of the portfolio.

title: Diversity and Cohesion: Exploring Employment as Mediator
abstract: Motivation: As the United States becomes increasingly diverse through immigration, understanding how demographic change affects community solidarity is a critical policy concern. While research documents associations between ethnic diversity and social cohesion, the mechanisms driving these relationships remain unclear. Problem Statement: This study examines whether employment mediates the relationship between immigration-driven demographic change and social cohesion. Specifically, it tests if increases in foreign-born population shares reduce natives’ employment opportunities, thereby lowering social cohesion. Approach: We combine data from the American Community Survey and the CPS-CEV supplement (2017–2023), analyzing 43,548 respondents across 12 metropolitan areas. Linear mediation models decompose total associations into direct and indirect (via employment) effects, with bootstrap confidence intervals and robustness checks (simulation-based GLMs, IPW, regression imputation). Results: Metros with significant foreign-born growth exhibit a binary cohesion effect of β = 0.002 (95% CI: –0.009, 0.012; p=0.917) and a continuous effect of β = 0.032 (95% CI: –0.001, 0.068; p=0.071). Indirect effects via employment are negligible (0.0001; 95% CI: –0.0003, 0.0005), representing <1% mediation. Conclusions: Employment does not meaningfully mediate demographic-change effects on social cohesion, challenging economic-competition explanations. Very small effect sizes suggest minimal practical significance; policies should consider alternative pathways like intergroup contact, cultural-bridge initiatives, or institutional responses.
Author: Kozlowski, Ethan
abstract_manipulated: Motivational impetus arises from the escalating diversity of the United States due to the influx of immigrants, prompting a pressing policy concern regarding the impact of demographic shifts on communal solidarity. Despite extant literature demonstrating linkages between ethnic heterogeneity and societal integration, the underlying mechanisms propelling these connections remain shrouded in ambiguity. The inquiry at hand delves into the potential mediating role of employment in the nexus between immigration-induced demographic transformations and social cohesion. Specifically, an exploration is undertaken to ascertain whether escalations in the proportion of foreign-born individuals curtail opportunities for native employment, consequently impeding social cohesion. Leveraging a fusion of data from the American Community Survey and the CPS-CEV supplement spanning the years 2017 to 2023, a cohort of 43,548 respondents drawn from 12 metropolitan regions undergoes scrutiny. Employing linear mediation models, the dissection of overall associations into direct and indirect pathways (via employment) is executed, underpinned by the utilization of bootstrap confidence intervals and a suite of robustness checks (comprising simulation-based Generalized Linear Models, Inverse Probability Weighting, and regression imputation techniques). The empirical evidence unravels that metropolitan areas characterized by substantial growth in foreign-born populations manifest a dichotomous impact on cohesion, with a beta coefficient of 0.002 (95% CI: –0.009, 0.012; p=0.917) and a continuous effect of 0.032 (95% CI: –0.001, 0.068; p=0.071). Inconsequential indirect effects through employment are discerned at a minute magnitude of 0.0001 (95% CI: –0.0003, 0.0005), translating to less than 1% of total mediation. The deductions drawn indicate that employment exerts negligible mediating influence on the relationship between demographic shifts and social cohesion, thereby challenging the explanatory power of economic-competition paradigms. The minute effect sizes underscore a paucity of practical import, thereby advocating for policy interventions to contemplate alternate avenues such as fostering intergroup interactions, promoting cultural-bridging initiatives, or instituting organizational responses.

title: Job Diversity and Political Selection in China
abstract: Political elites rarely advance by moving up a single, well defined ladder; instead they accumulate experience across a variety of posts, creating defacto career tracks. Yet we still know surprisingly little about how those tracks pay off and why. We offer a systematic assessment of career pathways in the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) from 1982 to 2012. We assemble an annual panel of every full and alternate Central-Committee member, we map unique jobs into a directed transition network, and apply a SpringRank algorithm to derive a continuous leadership–status scale that spans all bureaucratic domains. This study takes a novel perspective on the selection of leaders in the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). We measure career tracks of full and alternate CCP Central Committee members investigate their role in political selection. We find that the career tracks corresponding to frequent rotations are associated with significantly higher probabilities of obtaining top leadership positions in the CCP despite the influence of patronage networks. By comparing the role of career tracks with that of personal connections, our findings speak to the research on institutional versus personalistic rules in non-democracies.
Author: Rondo, Roberto
abstract_manipulated: Political elites are seldom propelled upwards along a singular, precisely delineated ascent; rather, they amass expertise through diverse roles, thereby shaping de facto career trajectories. Nonetheless, gaps persist in our understanding of the outcomes and rationales underlying these trajectories. We present a methodical evaluation of career pathways within the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) spanning the period from 1982 to 2012. Employing an annual panel encompassing every incumbent and substitute Central Committee member, we construct a directed transition network delineating distinct occupational roles. Subsequently, we apply a SpringRank algorithm to deduce a continuous scale of leadership status spanning the entire bureaucratic spectrum. This inquiry adopts an innovative vantage point on the leadership selection processes within the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). The trajectories of full and substitute CCP Central Committee members are scrutinized in terms of their impact on political appointment processes. Our analysis reveals that trajectories characterized by frequent rotations exhibit notably heightened probabilities of attaining premier leadership roles within the CCP, notwithstanding the sway of patronage networks. By contrasting the significance of career trajectories against that of personal affiliations, our findings contribute to the discourse on institutional versus personalistic norms in autocratic regimes.

title: Explaining Gendered Language Through Embedding Approximation: How “Stupid” Became a Man’s Word
abstract: Recent advances in word embedding and digitized text have reinvigorated macro-historical cultural analysis based on empirical data. However, static word2vec embedding, a distance-based measurement of latent semantic structures, becomes less interpretable when moved beyond survey-validated categories. Often, there is a one-to-many mapping between low-level semantic distance and high-level meanings, obscuring the pragmatic language use that drive latent semantic shifts in the embedding space. Focusing on “unintelligence” words in 20th-century American discourse, I critique the stereotype-based interpretation of their changing gender alignment in historical word2vec embeddings of American literature. While the gender axis reliably measures word associations, low-level associations do not neatly map onto well-defined gender stereotypes. Applying an adapted A La Carte approximation of individual context words and sentences, I suggest another possible interpretation: that the masculinization of “unintelligence” words could have stemmed from the increasing harshness in tone (a spurious variable), not judgments of male intelligence or stupidity. This study highlights the interpretive gap in embedding-based methods and calls for more rigorous approaches to making intersubjectively valid interpretations on cultural change.
Author: Zhu, Yutong
abstract_manipulated: Recent developments in the field of word embedding and digitized textual data have led to a resurgence in the application of macro-historical cultural analysis grounded in empirical evidence. Nonetheless, the utilization of static word2vec embedding, which serves as a distance-based metric of latent semantic structures, encounters challenges in terms of interpretability when extended beyond categories validated through survey methodologies. Frequently, there exists a situation where low-level semantic distances exhibit a one-to-many correspondence with high-level connotations, thereby obfuscating the pragmatic linguistic practices that underlie shifts in latent semantic dimensions within the embedding framework. Through a focused examination of "unintelligence" terms within the discourse of 20th-century American society, a critical interrogation is undertaken regarding the stereotype-driven construal of alterations in gender alignment as evidenced in historical word2vec embeddings of American literary texts. While the gender dimension consistently captures word associations, the alignment of low-level associations with clearly defined gender stereotypes is not straightforward. By employing a modified A La Carte approximation of individual contextual words and sentences, an alternative interpretation is posited: specifically, that the masculinization of "unintelligence" terms may potentially be attributed to a heightened degree of harshness in linguistic tone (a potentially confounding variable), rather than assessments of male intelligence or lack thereof. This investigation brings to the fore the presence of a hermeneutic lacuna in embedding-centric methodologies and underscores the necessity for the adoption of more stringent approaches aimed at facilitating intersubjectively valid interpretations of cultural transformations.

title: Tracing the Evolution of Public and Media Perceptions of LLMs: A Longitudinal Analysis of Sentiment, Topics, and Trends
abstract: This thesis conducted a longitudinal analysis examining the evolution of public and media perceptions towards Large Language Models (LLMs) from December 2022 to October 2024. Utilizing more than 300,000 Reddit submissions and approximately 10,000 news articles, it applied sentiment analysis and topic modelling to explore the trends of sentiment and topics in the context of LLMs. Public discussions on Reddit exhibited gradual increases and fluctuations tied to the releases of new models and contained a wide range of sentiments, such as approval, gratitude, disappointment, and annoyance. The amount of public discussions on Reddit steadily increased over time, with noticeable spikes in activity following the release of major LLMs. The dominant sentiment also varied across topics, for instance, computer science-related discussions showed relatively higher proportions of positive attitudes toward LLMs compared to other themes such as health or education. In contrast, media coverage sharply increased after significant events and maintained a neutral emotion in general. Topic analyses highlighted common discussion areas between the public and media, such as healthcare, education, and career. However, the public emphasized technical details and daily-life applications, whereas media reports centred around corporate events, ethical concerns, and broader societal implications.
Author: Ertong, Wei
abstract_manipulated: This thesis undertook a longitudinal examination to investigate the trajectory of public and media perceptions regarding Large Language Models (LLMs) from December 2022 to October 2024. Leveraging a corpus exceeding 300,000 Reddit submissions and around 10,000 news articles, sentiment analysis and topic modeling were employed to operationalize the exploration of sentiment and topics within the domain of LLMs. Discussions within the public sphere of Reddit were observed to manifest gradual escalations and fluctuations contingent upon the introductions of novel models, encapsulating a diverse spectrum of sentiments encompassing approval, gratitude, disappointment, and annoyance. The volume of public discourse on Reddit exhibited a consistent upward trend over time, punctuated by discernible peaks in engagement subsequent to the debuts of prominent LLMs. The prevailing sentiment further displayed variances across different thematic domains; for instance, discussions pertaining to computer science evinced comparatively elevated proportions of favorable dispositions toward LLMs vis-à-vis other domains such as health or education. Conversely, media coverage experienced abrupt surges subsequent to notable occurrences and predominantly maintained a neutral affective tenor. Topic analyses underscored common conversational domains shared between the public and media, including healthcare, education, and career trajectories. Nonetheless, the public discourse accentuated technical minutiae and practical implementations in daily life, while media narratives centered on corporate developments, ethical quandaries, and broader sociocultural ramifications.

title: Topic Modeling and Analysis of Misinformation in India
abstract: The rapid proliferation of misinformation in the digital age poses significant challenges to democratic processes, particularly in multilingual and multicultural societies like India. This thesis investigates the thematic structure, temporal patterns, and user engagement dynamics of misinformation during two general election cycles (2019 and 2024) and major socio-political events. By leveraging computational methods such as BERTopic for topic  modeling and sentiment analysis using large language models (LLMs), this research analyzes data from BoomLive, a fact-checking organization, and Twitter activity from Mohammed Zubair, co-founder of Alt News. The findings reveal distinct patterns in misinformation spread, with peaks aligning closely with electoral milestones and thematic shifts reflecting political and communal nar ratives. Health-related misinformation dominated during the COVID-19 pandemic, while communal and political falsehoods surged during elections. User engagement varied across themes, with politically charged misinformation eliciting polarized reactions and communal narratives driving higher engagement metrics. Sentiment analysis highlighted users’ tendency to engage selectively with fact-checks that aligned with their ideological beliefs, underscoring the role of motivated reasoning in shaping public discourse.
Author: Gajbhiye, Pritam Rashtrapal
abstract_manipulated: The proliferation of misinformation in the digital era is presenting formidable challenges to democratic processes, particularly in diverse and multilingual societies such as India. An exploration is undertaken in this thesis regarding the thematic architecture, temporal trends, and dynamics of user engagement with misinformation during two successive general election cycles (2019 and 2024) and significant socio-political occurrences. By employing computational techniques including BERTopic for topic elucidation and sentiment examination utilizing expansive language models (LLMs), this investigation scrutinizes data from BoomLive, a fact-checking entity, and Twitter activity from Mohammed Zubair, the co-originator of Alt News. The outcomes disclose distinctive dissemination patterns of misinformation, with peaks closely synchronized with electoral milestones and thematic transitions mirroring political and communal discourses. Amid the COVID-19 pandemic, misinformation pertaining to health predominated, while falsehoods of a communal and political nature experienced a surge during electoral periods. User engagement exhibited variations across themes, with politically charged misinformation provoking polarized responses and communal discourses propelling heightened engagement indices. The sentiment analysis underscored the proclivity of users to selectively engage with fact-checks that harmonized with their ideological predispositions, thus accentuating the influence of motivated reasoning in shaping public dialogue.

title: Assessing the Predictive Power of Urban Green Spaces in Machine Learning Models for Chicago Housing Prices
abstract: Urban Green Space (UGS) plays an important role in the urban environment. This study compares the predictive power of two types of UGS measurements — park proximity and NDVI — in the machine learning models to predict housing prices in Chicago. By incorporating real estate big data, GIS analysis, and Machine Learning techniques, the result indicates NDVI is a strong predictor for single-family residential properties on the fringe of the urban core.
Author: Sheng, Kuang
abstract_manipulated: Urban green space (UGS) is posited to exert a crucial influence within the urban milieu. The investigation herein delves into a comparative analysis of the prognostic efficacy of two distinct categories of UGS metrics — namely, spatial proximity to parks and Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) — within the paradigm of predictive modeling vis-à-vis residential property valuations in the city of Chicago. Through the integration of voluminous real estate data, geospatial analytical frameworks, and state-of-the-art Machine Learning methodologies, the findings proffer a semblance of evidence suggesting that NDVI harbors substantial predictive capacity specifically in relation to detached single-family dwellings situated at the urban periphery.

title: From Binary to Multiplicity: Rethinking Women’s Portrayals in Chinese State-owned Media
abstract: This study investigates the evolving portrayals of women in Chinese state-owned media through a computational analysis of the People’s Daily corpus from 1940s to 2000s. Chal- lenging traditional binary frameworks that simplify portrayals into either masculinized or feminized images, this research applies computational social science methods such as node embedding and topic modeling to identify the dynamic shifts in the oﬀicial gender discourse system. The empirical findings reveal that, rather than shifting between two idealized states, women’s representations strategically adapted to the state’s changing ideological and political objectives. Three distinct historical periods are identified: the early Mao’s era characterized by rather diverse representations aligning with revolutionary mobiliza- tion; the Cultural Revolution marked by singular, intensified political portrayals; and the post-reform era that witnessed a revival of traditional familial roles along with new represen- tations emphasizing individual economic success and national pride in sports. By reframing women’s portrayals through the lens of gender fluidity and performativity, this research challenges oversimplified masculinization narratives about the Mao’s era and provides new insights into how state power strategically adapt its construction of gender norms.
Author: Cao, Jie
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the evolving depictions of women in Chinese state-owned media via a computational analysis of the People’s Daily corpus spanning from the 1940s to the 2000s. Confronting conventional dichotomous frameworks that overly simplify portrayals into either masculinized or feminized archetypes, this research employs computational social science methodologies such as node embedding and topic modeling to discern the dynamic shifts in the official gender discourse system. The empirical results indicate that, rather than oscillating between two idealized states, the representations of women strategically adjusted to the evolving ideological and political objectives of the state. Three distinct historical epochs are discerned: the early Maoist era characterized by a spectrum of representations aligned with revolutionary mobilization; the Cultural Revolution distinguished by singular, heightened political depictions; and the post-reform era that observed a resurgence of traditional familial roles alongside novel depictions accentuating individual economic achievement and national pride in athletics. By reconceptualizing women's portrayals through the prism of gender fluidity and performativity, this research contests the overly simplistic masculinization narratives concerning the Maoist era and offers fresh perspectives on how state power strategically adapts its construction of gender norms.

title: Traffic and the Racialized Built Environment: Applying a New Lens on HOLC "Redlining" Maps to Understand Traffic Safety Outcomes
abstract: Traffic fatalities are understood to be a pressing cause of fatality in the United States, but the fundamental causes of inequities in their distribution are poorly understood. In this study, we aimed to understand the influences of one such fundamental cause: the implemen- tation of antiquated neighborhood lifecycle theories through racially discriminatory policies. Through a propensity matching design within five American cities, we found evidence that traffic health disparities are tied to the perceptions of race and space held by governmental entities such as the HOLC, but are not necessarily tied to redlining or other policies derived from neighborhood lifecycle theories. Our discovered relationships also vary by city, leading to potential implications for future studies using HOLC maps to understand modern health inequities.
Author: Wimer, Ashlynn
abstract_manipulated: Traffic fatalities are recognized as a pressing contributor to mortality rates in the United States, yet the underlying factors contributing to disparities in their distribution remain inadequately elucidated. The present investigation sought to elucidate the impacts of one such underlying factor: the operationalization of outdated neighborhood lifecycle paradigms through racially discriminatory measures. Employing a propensity matching approach across five urban centers in the United States, it was discerned that disparities in traffic-related health outcomes are intricately linked to the racialized spatial narratives propagated by governmental bodies such as the HOLC, albeit not invariably associated with redlining or other policy manifestations rooted in neighborhood lifecycle frameworks. Furthermore, the identified associations exhibit city-specific nuances, thus suggesting potential implications for forthcoming research endeavors employing HOLC maps to unpack contemporary health disparities.

title: Spatial Spillovers and Technical Efficiency in U.S. Local Banks
abstract: This study examines whether technical efficiency in U.S. community banks is shaped by spatial spillovers and how these effects are conditioned by financial and institutional factors. Employing a two-stage empirical strategy, we first estimate bank-level efficiency scores using Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA), followed by bias-corrected truncated regressions within a spatial Simar and Wilson (2007) framework. As a robustness check, we replicate the main findings using a spatial Stochastic Frontier Analysis (SFA) approach. The analysis draws on panel data from 4,936 community banks spanning 2015–2019. The results provide robust evidence of spatial interdependence: banks’ efficiency levels are significantly influenced by the characteristics of neighboring institutions, particularly in terms of capital structure, income diversification, and liquidity positions. Two primary mechanisms underpin these spillovers. First, relationship lending promotes the diffusion of soft information, amplifying local interbank linkages through social and informational embeddedness. Second, the magnitude and direction of spatial effects are heterogeneous, varying systematically with institutional contexts, especially across Federal Reserve Dis- tricts and data-driven spatial regimes. The overlap between regulatory jurisdictions and identified spatial clusters points to the relevance of localized supervision in shaping efficiency dynamics. By integrating spatial heterogeneity into the analysis of bank performance, the findings underscore the impor- tance of accounting for geographic structure in both academic modeling and policy design concerning community banking.
Author: Li, Zhuyin
abstract_manipulated: The investigation examines the potential influence of spatial spillovers on technical efficiency within U.S. community banks, as well as how these effects may be moderated by financial and institutional factors. Utilizing a two-stage empirical method, efficiency scores at the bank level are initially estimated through Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA), followed by bias-corrected truncated regressions within a spatial framework proposed by Simar and Wilson (2007). As a measure of robustness, the principal findings are replicated using a spatial Stochastic Frontier Analysis (SFA) technique. The analysis is based on panel data from 4,936 community banks across the years 2015–2019. The outcomes yield strong indications of spatial interdependence: the efficiency levels of banks are notably influenced by the attributes of neighboring institutions, particularly in relation to capital structure, income diversification, and liquidity positions. These spillovers are underpinned by two primary mechanisms. Initially, the dissemination of soft information through relationship lending fosters local interbank connections by means of social and informational embeddedness. Secondly, the intensity and direction of spatial impacts exhibit diversity, displaying systematic variations across institutional contexts, notably within Federal Reserve Districts and data-derived spatial regimes. The convergence between regulatory jurisdictions and identified spatial clusters highlights the significance of localized oversight in molding efficiency dynamics. By incorporating spatial diversity into the scrutiny of bank performance, the findings emphasize the necessity of considering geographical structures in both scholarly modeling and policy formulation concerning community banking.

title: Cultural Legacies of Agriculture: The Impact of Rice Farming on Innovation Success in China
abstract: This paper examines how historical rice-farming traditions shape modern innovation outcomes in China. Using county-level data and a spatial regression discontinuity design centered on the Qinling–Huaihe Line—the geographic boundary separating traditional rice and wheat farming regions—we investigate whether deep-rooted cultural legacies constrain the quality of innovation, as measured by invention patent success rates. The results show that regions with a rice-farming legacy exhibit significantly lower innovation success, despite no observable effect on the number of enterprises. This pattern suggests that collectivist cultural traits, shaped by historical agricultural systems, continue to suppress risk-taking and originality, which are essential for high-quality innovation, even within modern institutional environments. Our findings contribute to the literature on cultural economics by demonstrating that the impact of historical norms varies across types of economic activity. While modern institutions may support general entrepreneurship, they appear insufficient to overcome deeper cultural constraints on innovation effectiveness. These insights highlight the need for targeted strategies, such as incentive structures and cultural interventions, to foster breakthrough innovation in culturally risk-averse contexts.
Author: Zhou, Charlotte
abstract_manipulated: This investigation explores the influence of longstanding rice-farming traditions on contemporary innovation outcomes in China. Leveraging county-level data and employing a spatial regression discontinuity design centered on the Qinling–Huaihe Line—a geographic demarcation separating traditional rice and wheat cultivation regions—we endeavor to ascertain whether entrenched cultural legacies impede the efficacy of innovation, as gauged by rates of success in patenting inventions. The findings indicate that regions with a historical legacy of rice farming evince notably diminished success in innovation, notwithstanding the absence of any discernible impact on the proliferation of enterprises. This observed trend intimates that communal cultural attributes, molded by bygone agricultural frameworks, persist in stifling propensities for risk-taking and originality, both of which are indispensable for the cultivation of high-caliber innovation, even within contemporary institutional milieus. Our discoveries contribute to the body of knowledge within the realm of cultural economics by illustrating the nuanced effects of historical norms on various forms of economic activity. While contemporary institutions may foster general entrepreneurial endeavors, they seem to fall short in surmounting the profound cultural barriers that hamper the efficacy of innovation. These revelations underscore the imperative of tailored strategies, such as the restructuring of incentives and interventions targeting cultural dynamics, to cultivate breakthrough innovations in settings characterized by a prevailing aversion to risk.

title: Perceived Usefulness of eWOM in Movie Reviews: A Multi-Level Analysis of Content Features, User Traits, and Interactive Dynamics
abstract: Electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) significantly influences consumer decision-making because prospective viewers rely on peers’ first-hand opinions to reduce uncertainty and anticipate their own enjoyment—an effect that is especially pronounced for narrative prod- ucts such as movies. This influence is amplified by Web 2.0 technologies, which make peer reviews highly searchable at critical decision points, and by the credibility conferred through identity-based social networks. However, despite this growing social reliance, most existing studies overlook the broader context in which reviews circulate, implicitly treating each review as an independent unit. This study investigates how content features, reviewer characteristics, social network position, spoiler labels, and herd behavior collectively shape the perceived usefulness of movie eWOM on Douban Movies. By analyzing data from 185,516 movie reviews and their associated user networks using zero-inflated negative bi- nomial regression models, this research demonstrates that content richness and reviewer credibility substantially increase perceived usefulness. Reviews containing spoilers receive higher usefulness evaluations, suggesting spoilers reduce consumer uncertainty. Notably, re- viewers occupying bridging positions (high betweenness centrality) exert greater influence, aligning with social capital theory. In contrast, content similarity driven by herd behavior negatively affects usefulness perceptions, indicating redundancy diminishes perceived infor- mational value. This research extends the eWOM literature by integrating content-level and individual-level characteristics with interaction dynamics, offering practical implications for managing online review platforms and narrative product marketing strategies.
Author: Li, Zhuoqing
abstract_manipulated: The impact of electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) on consumer decision-making is substantial, as prospective viewers rely on peers' firsthand opinions to mitigate uncertainty and anticipate their own enjoyment, particularly for narrative products like movies. This influence is magnified by the utilization of Web 2.0 technologies, which render peer reviews highly accessible at pivotal decision junctures, bolstered by the credibility bestowed by identity-based social networks. Despite the burgeoning social reliance on eWOM, the majority of extant studies neglect the broader contextual framework within which reviews circulate, tacitly treating each review as an autonomous entity. The current investigation delves into how various factors such as content attributes, reviewer characteristics, social network positioning, spoiler labels, and herd behavior collectively mold the perceived utility of movie eWOM on Douban Movies. By scrutinizing data from 185,516 movie critiques and their corresponding user networks through the application of zero-inflated negative binomial regression models, this study illustrates that content richness and reviewer credibility play a pivotal role in heightening perceived utility. Reviews that incorporate spoilers are accorded higher utility ratings, implying that spoilers mitigate consumer uncertainty. Noteworthy is the finding that reviewers in bridging positions (characterized by high betweenness centrality) wield substantial influence, aligning with the tenets of social capital theory. Conversely, content similarity driven by herd behavior detrimentally impacts utility perceptions, indicating that redundancy diminishes perceived informational value. This research contributes to the eWOM literature by amalgamating content-level and individual-level attributes with interaction dynamics, thereby offering practical implications for the management of online review platforms and narrative product marketing strategies.

title: Thematic Analysis on Intersectional Harassment in Online Misogyny
abstract: As feminist activism gains visibility online, women increasingly face gender-based abuse in digital spaces, much of it originating from the ”manosphere”—a constellation of anti- feminist online communities such as incels. While prior research has examined the preva- lence of misogyny in online communities, less attention has been paid to how misogynist disproportionately targets women along intersecting social identities, including race, class, sexuality, etc. Intersectionality describes how overlapping systems of oppression interact to shape the lived experiences of people (Crenshaw, 1991). Specifically, the current study focuses on intersectional misogyny, defined as misogyny that invokes harassment related to one’s race, ability, class, or other identities. This study contributes to current literature in this area through two research questions: (1) How effectively can fine-tuned language models be used to detect intersectional harassment in misogynistic online forums? and (2) How do categories of misogynistic expression cluster within manosphere discourse, and in what ways do these clusters coalesce around particular intersectional identity markers? To answer these questions, I draw on a dataset of over 716k posts from one of the most active and unmoderated platforms in the manosphere: incel.is forum, focusing on those matched to physical and sexual violence lexicons. I manually annotated 7,000 posts based on whether it contains the presence intersectional targeting hostile language. For RQ1, a supervised HateBERT model was fine-tuned to detect intersectional harassment within the corpus. For RQ 2, an unsupervised topic modeling pipeline using BERTopic was used to inductively extract thematic clusters from the entire corpus. Next, the supervised HateBERT classifier was overlaid on these four macro thematic clusters to compute the proportion of posts containing intersectional harassment. This two-step pipeline enable to identify intersectionally harmful post and to analyze how such intersectional harassment cluster within manosphere contexts. The best-performing classifier achieved an F1 score of 0.73, with significant recall im- provements through class weighting, highlighting the potential of NLP models to detect nuanced intersectional hate speech. Regarding RQ2, results show that intersectional ha- rassment is not evenly distributed across four emerging macro themes, including Violence Fantasy, Fatalistic Worldview, Body-Based Devaluation, and Socioeconomic Complaint. Limitations of the study lie in the scope of human annotation and classifier’s deficit ability of detecting implicit harassment that lack overt identity markers. Future research should develop benchmark datasets for implicit intersectionality and expand annotation frame- works to include a broader range of misogynistic expressions. These steps would allow for more robust models capable of uncovering how intersectional hostility evolves across digital subcultures over time.
Author: Zhong, Haoyu
abstract_manipulated: As feminist activism becomes increasingly prominent in online spheres, women are increasingly subjected to gender-based abuse in digital domains, much of which is disseminated by the “manosphere”—an amalgamation of anti-feminist virtual communities, such as incels. While previous investigations have scrutinized the prevalence of misogyny in virtual communities, comparatively less scrutiny has been directed towards the ways in which misogyny disproportionately victimizes women with intersecting social identities, encompassing factors such as race, class, and sexuality. Intersectionality, as conceptualized by Crenshaw (1991), elucidates how intersecting systems of oppression converge to mold individuals' lived realities. This inquiry specifically delves into intersectional misogyny, operationally defined as misogyny that instigates harassment predicated on an individual's race, ability, class, or other social identities. This study adds to the contemporary literature in this domain by addressing two primary research inquiries: (1) To what extent can meticulously calibrated language models be leveraged for the identification of intersectional harassment within misogynistic virtual forums? and (2) How do categories of misogynistic discourse aggregate within manosphere dialogues, and in what manner do these clusters coalesce around specific intersectional identity markers? To address these inquiries, a dataset of more than 716k posts from one of the most active and unmoderated manosphere platforms, incel.is forum, was utilized, with a focus on posts matching physical and sexual violence lexicons. A manual annotation process was undertaken for 7,000 posts to ascertain the presence of intersectional targeting in hostile language. For the first research query, a supervised HateBERT model underwent fine-tuning for the detection of intersectional harassment within the corpus. In addressing the second research query, an unsupervised topic modeling pipeline utilizing BERTopic was employed for the inductive extraction of thematic clusters from the entire corpus. Subsequently, the supervised HateBERT classifier was applied to these four overarching thematic clusters to calculate the proportion of posts containing intersectional harassment. This dual-stage pipeline facilitated the identification of intersectionally harmful posts and the analysis of how such intersectional harassment is clustered within manosphere contexts. The most effective classifier attained an F1 score of 0.73, with notable enhancements in recall achieved through class weighting, underscoring the potential of NLP models in identifying nuanced intersectional hate speech. As for the second research question, findings demonstrate that intersectional harassment is not uniformly distributed across the four emergent macro themes, encompassing Violence Fantasy, Fatalistic Worldview, Body-Based Devaluation, and Socioeconomic Complaint. The study's limitations are contingent upon the extent of human annotation and the classifier's limitations in detecting implicit harassment devoid of explicit identity markers. Future research endeavors should endeavor to construct benchmark datasets for implicit intersectionality and broaden annotation frameworks to encompass a wider spectrum of misogynistic expressions. These endeavors would pave the way for more robust models capable of elucidating the evolution of intersectional hostility across digital subcultures over time.

title: Geopolitical Tensions and the Energy Stock Market: Examining Oil Price Volatility and U.S. Energy Stock Responses
abstract: This study examines how crude oil price volatility affects the price returns of leading energy companies in the U.S. stock market during two major geopolitical events: the 2014–2017 OPEC–Russia oil price war and the ongoing Russia–Ukraine war. Using the Bai-Perron structural break test, the study identifies critical turning points in oil price dynamics, fol- lowed by ARMA-EGARCH modeling to analyze volatility patterns during periods of crisis. The study finds strong asymmetries in oil price volatility, with negative shocks amplifying market uncertainty, particularly during crisis peaks. Furthermore, this study estimates a vector autoregressive (VAR) model to identify the relationship between oil price shocks and firm-level stock returns across upstream, integrated, and downstream oil and gas companies. The findings show that upstream firms exhibit stronger and more persistent reactions to oil price shocks, while integrated and downstream firms display quicker but more transient responses, returning to baseline levels in a shorter time frame. Notably, the Russia–Ukraine war triggered more synchronized and significant stock price reactions across firms, but these effects were shorter-lived compared to the earlier oil price war. These insights deepen our understanding of the oil-stock nexus and the behavior of stock prices in periods of height- ened geopolitical risks.
Author: Luo, Yuqing (Alice)
abstract_manipulated: The impact of crude oil price volatility on the price returns of leading energy companies in the U.S. stock market is explored within the context of two major geopolitical events: the 2014–2017 OPEC–Russia oil price war and the ongoing Russia–Ukraine conflict. Through the application of the Bai-Perron structural break test, crucial inflection points in oil price dynamics are identified, leading to the employment of ARMA-EGARCH modeling to scrutinize volatility patterns amidst periods of crisis. Robust asymmetries in oil price volatility are uncovered, with adverse shocks exacerbating market uncertainty, especially at crisis zeniths. Additionally, a vector autoregressive (VAR) model is utilized to discern the relationship between oil price shocks and firm-level stock returns across upstream, integrated, and downstream oil and gas enterprises. The outcomes reveal that upstream entities manifest more robust and enduring responses to oil price shocks, whereas integrated and downstream counterparts exhibit swifter yet transient reactions, reverting to baseline levels within a condensed timeframe. Noteworthy is the observation that the Russia–Ukraine conflict engendered more synchronized and notable stock price responses across firms, albeit of a fleeting nature compared to the antecedent oil price war. These nuanced findings contribute to the enrichment of our comprehension regarding the oil-stock nexus and the conduct of stock prices amidst heightened geopolitical uncertainties.

title: Converging Discourse, Diverging Audiences: Authoritarian Propaganda with Strategic Differentiation
abstract: This thesis introduces the concept of \textit{strategic differentiation} to explain how authoritarian regimes, rather than issuing uniform propaganda, adapt their messaging across platforms and audiences in a deliberate, calibrated manner. Drawing on Chinese state media texts from 2007 to 2022—including China Central Television (CCTV), People’s Daily, and Government Work Reports—this study examines how language, framing, and interpretive accessibility vary depending on audience sophistication and political context. Using semantic network analysis with FastText word embeddings and entropy-based readability metrics, the study finds systematic evidence of discursive segmentation. Elite-facing platforms employ lexically rich, technical language, while mass-facing outlets rely on simplified, emotionally resonant narratives. Temporal analysis reveals that propaganda coherence intensifies during crises and relaxes during routine periods, suggesting that message alignment is a strategic choice rather than a constant feature. These findings contribute to the literature on authoritarian information control by highlighting how regimes manage interpretation—not only through content, but through audience-specific messaging strategies. While the study is limited in its focus on textual media and does not directly measure audience reception, it provides a structured framework and replicable method for analyzing differentiated propaganda. The concept of strategic differentiation offers a new lens for understanding how authoritarian regimes communicate selectively to maintain control, manage legitimacy, and contain dissent.
Author: Jiang, Duoji
abstract_manipulated: This thesis presents the novel concept of \textit{strategic differentiation} as a framework for elucidating the nuanced ways in which authoritarian regimes tailor their communication strategies to diverse platforms and audiences with careful consideration. By analyzing Chinese state media texts spanning from 2007 to 2022—encompassing China Central Television (CCTV), People’s Daily, and Government Work Reports—this investigation delves into the intricate variations in language use, framing techniques, and interpretive accessibility driven by audience sophistication and the prevailing political milieu. Leveraging semantic network analysis utilizing FastText word embeddings and entropy-based readability metrics, the study uncovers compelling evidence of deliberate discursive segmentation. Platforms geared towards elite audiences exhibit a propensity for employing lexically intricate, specialized terminology, whereas those targeting the masses tend to rely on simplified, emotionally resonant narratives. Temporal scrutiny reveals a pattern where propaganda coherence heightens during periods of crisis and eases during routine phases, hinting at a strategic dimension to message alignment that transcends a static nature. These findings enrich the scholarly discourse on authoritarian information control by shedding light on how regimes orchestrate interpretation not solely through content, but also via tailored messaging strategies tailored to specific audience cohorts. Although the study's scope is delimited to textual media and does not directly gauge audience reception, it furnishes a structured analytical framework and replicable method for unravelling the complexities of differentiated propaganda. The notion of strategic differentiation unveils a novel perspective on how authoritarian regimes selectively communicate to uphold control, safeguard legitimacy, and quell dissent.

title: Instagram Shadowbans: Testing for Algorithmic Changes in Treatment of 'Political' Content
abstract: Social media platforms have turned to AI content moderation in response to increased scrutiny for the kind of content they circulate, especially due to major potential for social and political influence. Algorithmic reduction,  colloquially known as ‘shadowbans,’ are favorable for platforms due to their opaqueness, which allows for evasion of responsibility and creates a Foucauldian relationship of platform power and creator discipline. As such, platforms intentionally manufacture opacity surrounding algorithmic reduction. Instagram in particular has recently announced changes in algorithmic content that has caused outrage about their approach to free expression, in part due to vague definitions. This work seeks to shed light on Instagram’s intentionally opaque policies. To do so, I define a ‘model drift’ test to measure change in the ability of a neural network model to predict post like counts. I compare model drift to drift in data in order to tease out algorithmic drift from behavioral drift. I find confirmation that the February 2024 policy change did likely result in algorithmic reduction of content from politicians. More interestingly, I find that pro-Palestine and Zionist content have similar rates of engagement, but only pro-Palestine content shows signs of algorithmic reduction, starting as soon as one week after October 7, 2023. This research serves as a proof of concept demonstrating that intentionally opaque algorithmic reduction can still be investigated. Future research to bring transparency to social media practices is essential for understanding otherwise opaque and guarded practices of algorithmic moderation.
Author: Shao, Grace
abstract_manipulated: Social media platforms have turned to implementation of AI-based content moderation strategies in response to heightened scrutiny regarding the nature of content dissemination, particularly in light of significant potential for social and political impact. The phenomenon of algorithmic reduction, commonly referred to as ‘shadowbans,’ is favored by platforms due to its inherent opacity, which facilitates evasion of accountability and engenders a Foucauldian dynamic of platform power and creator discipline. Consequently, intentional cultivation of opacity around algorithmic reduction processes by platforms is observed. Notably, Instagram has recently unveiled modifications to its algorithmic content framework, prompting public outcry over its stance on free speech, partly attributable to ambiguous delineations. This study aims to elucidate the intentionally obfuscated policies of Instagram. To this end, a ‘model drift’ examination is formulated to gauge alterations in the predictive capacity of a neural network model with regard to post engagement metrics. By juxtaposing model drift against data drift, the aim is to disentangle algorithmic fluctuations from behavioral shifts. Empirical evidence indicates likely implications of the February 2024 policy amendment in terms of algorithmic content reduction pertaining to political figures. Intriguingly, findings reveal comparable engagement levels between pro-Palestine and Zionist content, yet solely pro-Palestine content exhibits indications of algorithmic reduction, manifesting as early as one week following October 7, 2023. This study serves as a methodological demonstration illustrating the feasibility of investigating deliberately obscured algorithmic reduction practices. Subsequent research endeavors aimed at fostering transparency in social media operational norms are imperative for comprehending the otherwise inscrutable and guarded aspects of algorithmic content moderation.

title: Tracing the Social Construction of Abortion Attitudes in Online Discourse
abstract: Building on research that highlights how elite actors have progressively constructed abortion as a polarized moral issue (Cassese et al., 2025; Ziegler, 2020), this study examines abortion attitudes through the lens of a social constructionist theory (Berger and Luckmann, 1966). It analyzes how polarization and social construction surrounding abortion materialize within bottom-up digital dis- course, and it evaluates the effectiveness of computational methods in capturing these patterns. While previous work has examined the presence of factors like elite polarization and social construc- tion on abortion attitudes, fewer have investigated how these dynamics manifest in digital spaces. This work offers insight into the digital reproduction of ideological structures and the potential and limitations of computational approaches for studying social construction processes. Using a dataset of Reddit comments comprising 2.5 million comments, later refined to a subset of 27,451, this study applies dictionary-based keyword classification and Support Vector Classifi- cation to classify the comments into abortion attitudinal stances (‘Pro-Choice’, ‘Pro-Life’ and ‘No Stance/Neutral’) followed by topic modeling (BERTTopic) to analyze large-scale digital text. Results show that, when informed by domain knowledge and iterative qualitative refinements, computational models can assist in identifying theoretical constructs such as polarization and so- cial meaning-making within digital discourse. Additionally, findings show that Pro-Life discourse has a more rigid, elite-driven structure, evidenced through the tight semantic structure and dis- proportionate topic distribution, with many of the dominant themes closely identifying with elite narratives outlined in previous research. Conversely, Pro-Choice discourse is much more decentral- ized, suggesting that the Pro-Life ideology has been carefully socially constructed over the past century.
Author: Griffin, Emma
abstract_manipulated: Drawing upon existing literature that underscores the progressive construction of abortion as a morally polarized issue by elite actors (Cassese et al., 2025; Ziegler, 2020), an investigation is undertaken in this study through the theoretical lens of social constructionism (Berger and Luckmann, 1966). The manifestation of polarization and social construction in relation to abortion within grassroots digital discourse is scrutinized, with an assessment of the efficacy of computational methodologies in capturing these intricate dynamics. While antecedent scholarship has delved into factors such as elite polarization and social construction in shaping abortion attitudes, fewer inquiries have delved into the manifestation of these phenomena in online spheres. This research contributes insights into the digital replication of ideological frameworks and the potentials, as well as constraints, of computational methodologies in scrutinizing processes of social construction. Leveraging a corpus of Reddit comments encompassing 2.5 million entries, subsequently refined to a subset of 27,451, this investigation employs dictionary-based keyword categorization and Support Vector Classification to categorize the comments based on abortion attitudinal orientations ('Pro-Choice', 'Pro-Life', and 'No Stance/Neutral'), followed by topic modeling (BERTTopic) for the analysis of expansive digital textual data. The outcomes indicate that, when guided by domain expertise and iterative qualitative enhancements, computational models can aid in the identification of theoretical constructs such as polarization and sociosemantic signification within digital dialogues. Moreover, the results demonstrate that Pro-Life rhetoric exhibits a more rigid, elite-directed configuration, as evidenced by the compact semantic organization and lopsided topic dissemination, with numerous prevailing themes closely aligning with elite narratives expounded in prior investigations. Conversely, Pro-Choice discourse displays a notably decentralized nature, hinting at the meticulous social construction of Pro-Life ideology over the course of the past century.

title: From Tool to Companion: An Unsupervised Typology of Human-LLM Interaction
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have rapidly adapted from specialized tools to everyday conversational partners. Rather than following the technical tradition of asking what LLMs can do, this study explores empirically how people actively frame their encounters with them. I drew 440k English-language first-turn prompts from the open-access WildChat corpus on human-ChatGPT interaction, embedded each into a 384-dimensional semantic space  (MiniLM-L6-v2), clustered the landscape with unsupervised learning techniques, and interpreted 60 different topic clusters through qualitative close-reading. The process visually typologized the prompts into a “graded spectrum” — instead of categorizing discrete “use cases,” I depicted an interpretable continuum from highly instrumental queries to deeply expressive or esoteric engagements. Another critical methodological finding is that apparent topical “coherence” often reflects authorship concentration rather than genuine consensus — a small cadre of “power-users” contributed thematically narrow yet voluminous exchanges, inflating the semantic density in repeated or industrialized use. This outsized footprint can skew the usage patterns in open-source chatlogs datasets, leading analysts to underestimate the genuine breadth and interweaving of topics while overestimating the prevalence of anthropomorphism and niche role-playing. The findings complicate the simplistic scope that juxtaposes utilitarian and anthropomorphic use of AI. I showed that many users approach ChatGPT not as a neutral tool but as a mutable social actor, strategically anthropomorphized inside local narrative frames. There is a shifting ecology of motives that is prominent in the dataset yet less discussed in existing studies — from affective self-therapy to workplace automation, and with the hidden labour (“playbor”) of heavy users who are quietly shaping public training data and, by extension, future model behavior. The findings foreground the social and emotional stakes of LLM use, and invite designers to treat usage data with caution while honoring the plurality of ways that humans want to converse with — rather than merely through — their machines.
Author: Zhu, Tianfang
abstract_manipulated: The transformation of Large Language Models (LLMs) from specialized tools to conversational partners in everyday settings has been swift. Rather than adhering to the conventional technical approach of evaluating the capabilities of LLMs, this investigation delves into how individuals actively construct their interactions with them. A dataset of 440k English-language initial prompts from the publicly available WildChat corpus on human-ChatGPT interaction was utilized. These prompts were then embedded into a 384-dimensional semantic space (MiniLM-L6-v2) and subjected to clustering using unsupervised learning techniques. Subsequently, 60 distinct topic clusters were qualitatively examined through a process of close-reading analysis. This method visually categorized the prompts along a "graded spectrum," illustrating a continuum from highly practical inquiries to deeply expressive or esoteric engagements, as opposed to discrete "use cases." An important methodological insight was the observation that the apparent thematic "coherence" often arises from a concentration of contributions by a select group of "power-users," who generate thematically focused yet voluminous exchanges, thereby inflating the semantic density through repetitive or standardized usage. This phenomenon can distort the usage patterns within open-source chatlogs datasets, potentially leading researchers to underestimate the true diversity and interconnectedness of topics while overestimating the prevalence of anthropomorphism and specialized role-playing.
