It is contended that the preferences of prospective brides and grooms are intricately intertwined with entrenched gender and cultural biases, exhibiting a remarkable degree of constancy over time. Through an analysis of newspaper matrimonial advertisements culled from The Tribune, a prominent north Indian publication, covering the period from 2001 to 2014, a discernible predilection is observed for attributes such as "tall," "slim," "fair," and "beautiful" in women, and "handsome," "tall," and "intelligent" in working men, particularly from the Sikh or Jat communities. This underscores the pervasive influence of gendered stereotypes and a pronounced emphasis on intra-faith unions within these advertisements. The findings of this inquiry also shed light on the escalating inclination towards online platforms for matrimonial solicitations. Consequently, this investigation unearths nuanced biases embedded in the social tapestry of Indian matchmaking traditions, prompting reflections on the continuing relevance of newspaper matrimonial advertisements and their ramifications on the broader matchmaking landscape.

title: Analysis of Innovation and Inequality with Microdata
abstract: This paper investigates the effects and relationship of innovation and industry concentration on top-income inequality in various sectors. It contends that innovation plays a critical role in the rise of income inequality and that comprehending the relationship between market mechanisms and inequality is necessary for elucidating the structures that underlie these economic phenomena. The study aims to establish links between economic factors related to innovation and different types of inequality while determining possible negative impacts of innovation. The study utilizes microdata from CPS IPUMS, patents, and an existing industry concentration dataset and shows that innovation might increase top income inequality. Additionally, the paper offers a theoretical explanation of how innovation could cause top-income inequality but not in general wage inequality
Author: Onogi, Koichi
abstract_manipulated: This paper endeavours to interrogate the effects and interplay of innovation and industry concentration on top-income inequality across various sectors. It is posited that innovation assumes a pivotal role in the escalation of income inequality, and that a nuanced understanding of the nexus between market mechanisms and inequality is imperative for the elucidation of the underlying structures of these economic phenomena. The investigation seeks to forge associations between economic variables pertaining to innovation and diverse forms of inequality, while discerning potential adverse repercussions of innovation. The data from CPS IPUMS, patents, and an extant industry concentration dataset are harnessed in this study, revealing that innovation may potentially heighten top income inequality. Furthermore, a theoretical rationale is proffered elucidating how innovation could conceivably engender top-income inequality, albeit not necessarily in the realm of general wage inequality.

title: Adapting to a New Normal: The Impact of COVID-19 on Hiring Patterns and the Development of Predictive Models for Talent Acquisition
abstract: I collected all interview questions from glassdoor for 26 industry-representative companies in the S&P 500 and collected data on the difficulty of all interview questions, interview outcomes, and interviewer perceptions that had been flagged. By combining the flagged data with the interview text, a unified NLP analysis was performed. I try to obtain a predictor that can predict the interview pass rate of interviewees by inputting information such as the interview questions and the difficulty of the physical interview.
Author: Zhang, Zhe
abstract_manipulated: The present study involved the compilation of interview inquiries retrieved from the Glassdoor platform pertaining to 26 industry-representative entities listed in the S&P 500. Data was amassed on the complexity of all interview queries, outcomes of interviews, and perceptions of interviewers that were identified as contentious. Through the amalgamation of the contentious data with the interview transcripts, a comprehensive Natural Language Processing (NLP) analysis was undertaken. An effort was made to derive a predictive model capable of estimating the success rate of interviewees by inputting details such as the interview questions and the level of difficulty inherent in the interview process.

title: The Mobilizing Power on Twitter: A Case Study of Hong Kong’s Anti-Extradition Bill Movement
abstract: During the 2019 Hong Kong Anti-extradition Law (Anti-ELAB) Movement, Twitter has become an extended frontline for opinion leaders and protestors seeking external attention and recognition. This research attempts to explicate how the information about the Anti-ELAB Movement was transmitted on Twitter, how and to what extent these messages mobilize people to respond, and what properties in tweets triggered more subsequent actions. The determinants of mobilization are identified by applying social network algorithms and sentiment analysis to locate the chain and tone of conversations. To address the confounders in texts, Topical Inverse Regression Text-Matching (TIRM) ensures the texts are comparable in causal inference modeling. The findings indicate that external allies played a more significant role in mobilizing individuals than local Hongkongers, both in terms of disseminating information about the Anti-ELAB movement and inspiring new discussions among Twitter users.
Author: Fan Ju Yeh, Emily
abstract_manipulated: During the 2019 Hong Kong Anti-extradition Law (Anti-ELAB) Movement, Twitter emerged as a primary locus for opinion leaders and protestors seeking external attention and recognition. The present investigation seeks to elucidate the mechanisms through which information pertaining to the Anti-ELAB Movement was disseminated on Twitter, as well as the manner in which these messages serve to mobilize individuals and the extent to which such mobilization occurs, alongside an exploration of the tweet characteristics that potentially stimulate subsequent actions. The determinants of mobilization are discerned through the application of social network algorithms and sentiment analysis to map out the trajectory and tenor of dialogues. To mitigate potential confounding factors in textual data, the utilization of Topical Inverse Regression Text-Matching (TIRM) methodology ensures commensurability in the textual corpus for causal inference modeling. The outcomes of this study suggest that external allies exerted a more pronounced influence in mobilizing individuals compared to local Hongkongers, both in terms of information dissemination pertaining to the Anti-ELAB movement and in catalyzing new discussions among denizens of Twitter.

title: Data Visualization with Applications in the Social Sciences: A Portfolio
abstract: In this portfolio, you will find a literature review and three data visualization projects that showcase the primary theoretical insights from the literature across a range of social science domains. The portfolio aims to demonstrate how the theoretical principles can be applied to real-world scenarios and how effective data visualization can enhance understanding and communication of complex information for different audiences.
Author: Towey, Allison
abstract_manipulated: Within this portfolio, a comprehensive exploration is presented, encompassing a discerning analysis of extant scholarship as well as the execution of three illustrative projects utilizing data visualization techniques. The overarching objective of the portfolio is to elucidate the transformative potential inherent in theoretical underpinnings derived from a diverse array of social science disciplines. By virtue of showcasing the practical instantiation of these theoretical frameworks within authentic contexts, the portfolio endeavors to underscore the utility of data visualization in fostering nuanced comprehension and facilitating the dissemination of intricate information to varied stakeholders.

title: Association between Resting-state fMRI and Inhibitory Control among Psychosis Patients and Healthy Controls
abstract: The ability to inhibit behavior is essential for achieving one's goals and being flexible in response to changing situations. Prior resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (RS-fMRI) studies in healthy people have shed light on key brain regions for inhibition performance (Lee & Hsieh, 2017; Tian et al., 2012). It is possible, but untested, that the association between RS-fMRI and inhibition performance seen in healthy controls extends to psychosis and helps predict the range of performance observed. Therefore, we are interested in the question of how inhibition performance is associated with spontaneous brain activity among psychosis patients and healthy people. We will also investigate how different the associations are between these two populations, and within psychosis patients. In this thesis, we expect that positive correlations exist between inhibition performance and spontaneous brain activity represented by RS-fMRI in specific brain regions of interest including default mode network and bilateral inferior frontal cortex. We also hypothesize that these correlations will be significantly weaker for some psychosis subgroups with greater inhibition response impairments. My thesis will fill in the gap in the literature and help characterize neural system alterations in psychosis, which are likely to be heterogeneous between different subgroups. An understanding of the difference in the associations may also help us better explain the decreased inhibition performance previously seen among psychosis patients (Ethridge et al., 2014). Comparison of the association between subgroups of psychosis patients may also illicit explanations for the different inhibition performances between these subgroups that have been previously identified (Clementz et al., 2016).
Author: Wu, Zhennuo
abstract_manipulated: The capacity to enact inhibitory control plays a pivotal role in goal attainment and adaptive responsiveness to environmental dynamics. Previous investigations employing resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (RS-fMRI) in individuals without pathological conditions have elucidated the neural substrates crucial for inhibitory function (Lee & Hsieh, 2017; Tian et al., 2012). It is plausible, albeit unverified, that the link between RS-fMRI and inhibitory performance observed in healthy cohorts extends to individuals with psychosis, potentially offering predictive insights into the spectrum of performance variations. Thus, the inquiry at hand pertains to the nexus between inhibitory performance and intrinsic brain activity in both psychosis cohorts and non-clinical counterparts. The present study also aims to examine the divergences in these associations between the aforementioned populations and within distinct subgroups of individuals with psychosis. Within this dissertation, it is postulated that positive correlations are discernible between inhibitory performance and intrinsic brain activity, as delineated by RS-fMRI, within specified regions of interest encompassing the default mode network and bilateral inferior frontal cortex. Furthermore, it is hypothesized that these correlations may exhibit a marked attenuation among certain subgroups of psychosis characterized by pronounced inhibitory deficits. This thesis endeavor seeks to address gaps in the existing literature and contribute to the characterization of neural network perturbations in psychosis, which are anticipated to display heterogeneity across varying subgroups. Moreover, illuminating the distinctions in these associations may offer insights into the diminished inhibitory performance previously documented in individuals with psychosis (Ethridge et al., 2014). Comparative analyses of the associations across distinct psychosis subgroups may yield elucidations regarding the divergent inhibitory performances observed among these subgroups as reported in prior studies (Clementz et al., 2016).

title: Wars of Position: A Social Network Analysis of Political Podcasts
abstract: Ideological echo chambers on digital media platforms have been the subject of extensive research by computational social scientists. Current debates focus on the role of user choice and algorithmic recommendation feeds in creating these echo chambers. In this thesis, I argue that content creators can work to create echo chambers through collaborations with other ideologically aligned creators. Adopting a Gramscian theoretical framework, I argue that these homophilic collaborations are tools of hegemonic struggle, analogous to the “wars of position” described by the late political theorist Antonio Gramsci. To validate my claims, I perform a social network analysis of collaborations between the 250 most political podcasters in the United States. My network analysis reveals that collaborations are much likelier between podcasters of similar ideological alignments, and these probabilities are the largest for left-wing podcasters. My research also shows that collaborations are highly unequal, adhering to power law distributions observed commonly across a wide range of social phenomena. My results advocate for a renewed focus on engagement between content creators on social media platforms as a potential driver of political polarization.
Author: Poongundranar, Thiyaghessan
abstract_manipulated: Ideological echo chambers within the realm of digital media platforms have been the object of substantial investigation by computational social scientists. Ongoing discussions revolve around the impact of user agency and algorithmic recommendation streams in the construction of these echo chambers. The current thesis posits that content producers possess the capacity to engender echo chambers through cooperative ventures with other creators who share similar ideological leanings. Grounded in a Gramscian theoretical paradigm, it is contended that these homophilic collaborations serve as instruments of hegemonic contention, mirroring the "wars of position" elucidated by the esteemed political theorist Antonio Gramsci. To substantiate these arguments, a social network analysis is undertaken on the interactions among the top 250 political podcasters in the United States. The outcomes of the network analysis disclose a pronounced propensity for collaborations to occur between podcasters who espouse akin ideological stances, with this propensity being most pronounced among left-leaning podcasters. Furthermore, the research findings unveil a marked disparity in the frequency of collaborations, conforming to the power law distributions frequently observed in a diverse array of social contexts. The conclusions drawn advocate for a renewed emphasis on the interplay among content creators on social media platforms as a plausible catalyst for political polarization.

title: How does COVID-19 affect different consumers’ online grocery shopping behavior?  Heterogeneity within demographic factors.
abstract: With the development of technology and e-commerce, consumers’ online shopping behavior is a popular field being explored by many researchers, especially regarding its factors involving social environments, features of products, merchants activities and customers’ own characteristics. As the outbreak of COVID-19 pandemic, the online grocery sales shows an increasing trend. Regarding the driving force of the increase, how have different consumer groups’ online grocery shopping behavior been affected by the pandemic differently? The paper investigates the question using the first-hand data obtained from a survey with 905 responses via Amazon Mechanical Turk platform. The survey is designed concerning customers’ demographic features including age, gender, education and income, and also shopping behavior including shopping frequency, spending and choice of channel. The analysis relies on methods including descriptive statistics, Spearman Correlation Coefficient, McNemar test, Logistic regression and OLS regression. Results suggest that customers’ age, gender and household plays a role in the heterogeneity in the influence of the pandemic on online grocery shopping.
Author: Li, Xin
abstract_manipulated: Amidst the proliferation of technology and the surge of e-commerce, scholars have shown a burgeoning interest in elucidating the intricacies of consumers' online shopping conduct, particularly in relation to the multifaceted determinants encompassing social contexts, product attributes, merchant dynamics, and individual characteristics of customers. With the advent of the COVID-19 pandemic, there has been a discernible uptick in online grocery transactions. In light of this surge, what impact has the pandemic potentially exercised on diverse consumer segments' online grocery procurement behaviors? This paper delves into this inquiry by scrutinizing primary data gleaned from a survey garnering 905 responses via the Amazon Mechanical Turk platform. The survey is meticulously oriented towards capturing customers' demographic particulars encompassing age, gender, educational attainment, and income levels, as well as their shopping propensities encompassing frequency, expenditure patterns, and choice of transactional channel. The analytical framework is underpinned by an array of methodological approaches including descriptive statistics, Spearman Correlation Coefficient, McNemar test, Logistic regression, and OLS regression. The findings tentatively hint at the varying impact of the pandemic on online grocery shopping, contingent upon customers' age, gender, and household composition.

title: Self-Disclosure in Psychotherapy: The Development of a Novel Behavioral Measurement via Natural Language Analysis
abstract: While it is widely accepted that clients’ level of self-disclosure has important implications for treatment outcomes and therapeutic relationship, it is difficult to measure. Extant assessment tools fail to capture the fluctuating nature of self-disclosing behaviors, their sensitivity to context and vulnerability to subjective biases. This study sought to address this issue by utilizing a quantitative self-disclosure method assessing distinct linguistic features. Forty-eight participants aged 18-35 were recruited to complete two dyadic, 45-minute conversations with a stranger co-participant. The conversation topics they received either induced high or low self-disclosure. Logistic regression models in combination of natural language processing techniques including a word-count approach (LIWC) and a word-embedding approach (BART) were constructed based on participants’ conversation content to classify high vs. low-level of self-disclosure. A logistic regression model built upon LIWC categories achieved 89.66% accuracy, and the one using pre-trained BART model retained 48.28% prediction accuracy. Incorporating demographic variables did not affect the model built on LIWC but improved the accuracy to 62.07% for the BART approach. Results provides preliminary evidence in support of algorithms that assess language content to predict high versus low-levels of self-disclosure. Limitations were discussed that future studies might improve model performance by recruiting a larger, more heterogenous participant sample to discuss a wider range of topics.
Author: Yu, Jiachen
abstract_manipulated: The widely acknowledged importance of clients’ self-disclosure levels in relation to treatment outcomes and therapeutic relationships presents a challenge in terms of measurement due to the dynamic and context-dependent nature of such behaviors, susceptible to subjective biases. This investigation aimed to tackle this issue through the utilization of a quantitative self-disclosure methodology examining distinct linguistic features. A cohort of forty-eight participants, aged 18-35, engaged in two 45-minute dyadic interactions with unfamiliar co-participants, during which conversation prompts were designed to elicit either high or low levels of self-disclosure. Logistic regression models, integrating natural language processing techniques such as a word-count analysis (LIWC) and a word-embedding method (BART), were developed to differentiate between high and low self-disclosure levels based on participants’ conversational content. The logistic regression model built on LIWC features demonstrated an accuracy rate of 89.66%, while the model utilizing the pre-trained BART approach maintained a prediction accuracy of 48.28%. The inclusion of demographic variables had no impact on the LIWC model but increased accuracy to 62.07% for the BART model. These findings offer initial support for the utility of algorithmic tools in evaluating linguistic content to anticipate varying levels of self-disclosure. Discussion of limitations highlighted the potential for future studies to enhance model performance through the recruitment of a larger, more diverse participant sample engaging in discussions spanning a broader array of topics.

title: Exploring the Equity of Government Response in Coproduction in Chicago: Before, During, and After COVID-19
abstract: This paper aims to investigate whether government provides public services to different locations equally. I use response time as a metric to assess the performance of government in each location associated with the demographic attributes. Data from the Chicago Data Portal and the American Community Survey (ACS) are collected for the years 2019, 2020, and 2021. My analysis reveals departmental and technological heterogeneity in the changes in government performance regarding public service delivery depending on the location of the service requests during the study period. My findings indicate that areas with a higher number of educated individuals receives a quicker government response overall during the study period. Furthermore, certain departments exhibit a particularly discriminatory response towards disadvantaged groups only during the pandemic, thereby highlighting the potential for inequitable distribution of government resources under times of stress. Additionally, requests submitted through technology-based channels are prioritized for disadvantaged groups as compared to traditional channels, suggesting the potential of technology to aid the government in achieving more equitable outcomes.
Author: Zhang, Baotong
abstract_manipulated: This paper endeavors to interrogate the extent to which public services are equitably dispensed by governmental entities across diverse geographical locations. Response time is operationalized as a key metric for evaluating governmental efficacy in each locale in relation to demographic characteristics. Data sourced from the Chicago Data Portal and the American Community Survey (ACS) for the years 2019, 2020, and 2021 are utilized in this investigation. An analysis of the data uncovers variances in departmental and technological frameworks contributing to fluctuations in governmental performance vis-à-vis the delivery of public services contingent upon the location of service requests throughout the duration of the study. The findings suggest that regions with a higher concentration of educated individuals experience expedited governmental responses on the whole during the observational period. Moreover, select departments evince a discernible bias towards marginalized cohorts solely amidst the pandemic, thereby underscoring the potential for the uneven allocation of governmental resources during times of exigency. Furthermore, requests channeled through technology-driven platforms are accorded precedence for disadvantaged populations in contrast to conventional modalities, thereby intimating the capacity of technology to facilitate the government in fostering more equitable outcomes.

title: Detecting Discourse Pattern: MBTI Personality Traits from Reddit Posts
abstract: Social media nowadays has transformed how people express their feelings and thoughts.Researchers have proved that our online discourses or even a single profile tells a story. However, few studies paid attention to discursive features on those contexts and many of those studies were conducted in black-box approaches. In fact, it matters as researchers can generate insights from those feature interpretations and therefore understand human behaviors online. To further contribute to this field, I investigated people’s frequent words used, sentence length, vocabulary size, and topics they would like to discuss online. This study was designed to narrow down the scope to the online community and especially targeted on posts and comments from Reddit’s MBTI groups, as my goal is to discover discursive patterns among people with similar personality types. Myers–Briggs Type 16 Indicators(MBTI) is a well-known personality assessment, which has been popular since 1980s. Results provided strong supports that discursive patterns from people’s online communication can be linked to their underlying personalities and most of people who share similar personality types have comparable traits in contexts.
Author: Zhao, Sushan
abstract_manipulated: In contemporary society, the utilization of social media platforms has revolutionized the manner in which individuals articulate their emotions and cognitions. It has been empirically demonstrated by scholars that our digital dialogues, as well as the content encapsulated within a singular profile, possess narrative qualities. Nevertheless, there has been a dearth of scholarly attention directed towards the discursive attributes inherent in these virtual environments, with many of the extant investigations being characterized by opaque methodological frameworks. The investigation of such discursive features is consequential, as it has the potential to yield valuable insights for researchers, thereby facilitating a nuanced comprehension of online human behaviors. In order to augment the existing body of knowledge in this domain, an examination was undertaken pertaining to the prevalent lexical choices, syntactic complexity, lexical diversity, and thematic predilections manifested in online communication. This inquiry was meticulously structured to delimit its focus to the cyber community, with a specific emphasis on the textual contributions within Reddit's MBTI forums. The overarching aim was to elucidate the discursive regularities prevalent among cohorts exhibiting congruent personality configurations. The Myers–Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI), a venerable personality assessment tool that has enjoyed widespread popularity since the 1980s, served as the analytical backdrop. The findings furnish robust evidence in support of the proposition that the discursive tendencies observable in individuals' online interactions are intricately linked to their intrinsic personalities, with individuals sharing analogous personality profiles demonstrating commensurate attributes across diverse contextual milieus.

title: CUSTOMIZED E-COMMERCE B2C RECOMMENDATIONS SYSTEM FOR AMAZON
abstract: The rapid development of information technology and digitalization has promoted the spread of e-commerce. The variety of products can make customers feel overwhelmed when choosing and using them. Currently, Customized E-commerce B2C Recommendation Systems bring convenience to both customers and businesses by improving sales efficiency, solving the problem of information overload, and optimizing the supply chain. Amazon is an American multinational technology company that specializes in e-commerce and has been referred to as one of the most influential economic and cultural forces in the world. For this project, I will design a Customized E-commerce B2C Recommendation System for Amazon by analyzing Amazon Review Data (Ni, 2018), which is a collection of reviews including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, product metadata like product descriptions, category information, price, brand, image features, and links that are viewed. All analysis tools are from Amazon Web Services (AWS), such as Amazon Textract, Amazon Comprehend, Amazon A2I, Amazon Kendra, Amazon Personalize, Amazon Translate, etc. (AWS website). There are four stages in this project: retrieval, filtering, scoring, and ordering. In this paper, I will focus on the retrieval stage. The method of the retrieval stage should be based on a hybrid system called Enhanced Augmented Two-Tower Modeling, which combines Collaborative Filtering (CF) and Content-Based Filtering (Yu, Wang, Feng, Xue, 2021). The goal of the project is to build customer profiles and recommend products/services by matching customer preferences and product information based on textual information like product descriptions and customer comments.
Author: Ni, Jingwen
abstract_manipulated: The rapid proliferation of information technology and digitalization has facilitated the proliferation of e-commerce. The plethora of products available may engender a sense of overwhelm among consumers during the selection and utilization processes. Presently, Customized E-commerce Business-to-Consumer (B2C) Recommendation Systems offer a channel for enhancing sales efficiency, ameliorating the issue of information saturation, and optimizing the supply chain, thereby providing convenience for both consumers and enterprises. Amazon, an American multinational technological entity renowned for its e-commerce ventures, stands as a pivotal economic and cultural force on a global scale. In this venture, a Customized E-commerce B2C Recommendation System will be devised for Amazon through the scrutiny of Amazon Review Data (Ni, 2018), encompassing reviews containing ratings, textual content, helpfulness votes, and product metadata comprising item descriptions, categorical particulars, pricing details, brand specifications, image attributes, and pertinent links. All analytical resources harnessed in this pursuit are sourced from Amazon Web Services (AWS), encompassing tools such as Amazon Textract, Amazon Comprehend, Amazon A2I, Amazon Kendra, Amazon Personalize, Amazon Translate, among others (as per the AWS website). The project unfolds across distinct phases, namely retrieval, filtration, evaluation, and arrangement. The focus of this study lies on the initial phase of retrieval. The methodology employed in the retrieval phase is envisioned to be centered on an amalgamated framework denoted as Enhanced Augmented Two-Tower Modeling, synthesizing Collaborative Filtering (CF) and Content-Based Filtering (as delineated by Yu, Wang, Feng, Xue, 2021). The overarching objective of this endeavor is to construct consumer profiles and propose products/services by aligning consumer predilections with product intelligence based on textual cues such as product descriptions and consumer feedback.

title: Estimating the association between suicide rates and rap music lyrics mentioning death and negative emotions
abstract: Music lyrics have been long proven to have emotional as well as moral effects on individuals and society. Among genres of music, rap music, being an important part of American music street culture, is special due to the more important role of lyrics in rap songs. This study took rap lyrics as our research focus and tried to explore their mental influence, taking suicide as the target. A total of 5348 popular rap songs from 1995 to 2018 were obtained from the dataset of Billboard music chart, accessed on one Github public source. Suicide rates over the last two decades were available on the website of the National Institute of Mental Health. Moreover, this study also included several factors that were proven to be strong predictors of suicide, such as unemployment rates, divorce rates, GNP, etc.  A regular regression model and distributed lag model were constructed. The result suggested rap lyrics, mentioning words related to death and negative emotions, could be significantly correlated to suicide rates in the same period. For one unit increase in rap lyrical mentions percentage, the model estimated suicide rates in the same period will rise by around 108 per 100,000 population. The one-year lag association was also significant based on our distributed lag model, and these relationships were confirmed with the cross-correlation analysis. This association implied that there might be a shared cultural context or societal factors that contribute to both the prevalence of these lyrics and the occurrence of suicide. Therefore, the study suggested that there is an important societal issue at play, which requires further investigation and attention. The findings of this study could inform future research on the cultural and societal factors that contribute to mental health issues and suicide, particularly among vulnerable populations who are more likely to be exposed to rap music.
Author: Chen, Zhiqian
abstract_manipulated: The emotional and moral impacts of music lyrics on individuals and society have been extensively documented in scholarly literature. Within the realm of musical genres, rap music, integral to American street culture, stands out due to the heightened significance of its lyrical content. This investigation centered on rap lyrics and their potential psychological effects, particularly with regard to suicide. A comprehensive dataset comprising 5348 popular rap songs released from 1995 to 2018 was curated from the Billboard music chart repository hosted on a public Github platform. Concurrently, suicide incidence data spanning the past two decades was collated from the National Institute of Mental Health website. Notably, this study integrated various socio-economic indicators recognized as robust suicide predictors, including unemployment rates, divorce rates, and Gross National Product (GNP), among others. Analytical frameworks encompassing a standard regression model and a distributed lag model were deployed. The outcomes tentatively indicated a statistically significant association between rap lyrics featuring themes of death and negative affect and suicide rates during the corresponding periods. Notably, an incremental increase in the proportion of rap lyrics referencing these themes was correlated with an estimated rise of approximately 108 suicides per 100,000 population during the same timeframe. Furthermore, the distributed lag model unveiled a noteworthy one-year temporal delay in the observed relationship, corroborated by cross-correlation analyses. These findings suggest a potential shared cultural milieu or societal underpinnings driving both the prevalence of such lyrics and suicide occurrences. Consequently, it is posited that a complex interplay of cultural and societal dynamics may substantively influence mental health concerns, warranting further scholarly scrutiny and societal awareness. This study's implications hold promise for guiding future inquiries into the intricate cultural and societal determinants shaping mental health challenges, particularly among marginalized groups disproportionately exposed to rap music.

title: PERFORMING PARENTHOOD: AN ANALYSIS OF MOTHERHOOD AND FATHERHOOD ON TWITTER
abstract: A growing shift in gender roles in society seems to have infiltrated most areas of life, including family relationships such as parental roles. Existing research shows that both men and women are increasing reporting a desire to have more equality in their parenting roles and tasks. However, during the COVID-19 pandemic, there appeared to be “motherhood penalty” that pushed mothers back into traditional roles. In this thesis, I explore the extent to which parental roles translated online pre- and post- pandemic using a corpus of Tweets posted in 2019 and 2022 that contained the hashtags #DadLife or #MomLife. I examine the differences in tweets posted by fathers and mothers through frequency analysis, sentiment analysis, and text embedding. Results show a reinforcement of the traditional stereotypes about roles that mothers have versus fathers have including both physical and emotional roles; however, there is some overlap between these roles as well. The differences in role performance align with the “motherhood penalty” that occurred during the pandemic and translated directly online. In summary, while gender roles have shifted, the underlying expectations of the parental roles that mothers have and fathers have continue to persist in the digital space of Twitter, especially post-pandemic
Author: Borlase, Kaya
abstract_manipulated: A burgeoning trend in societal gender dynamics appears to have permeated various spheres of human existence, encompassing familial interactions such as parental responsibilities. Previous scholarship indicates an emerging inclination among individuals of both genders to aspire towards greater parity in their parental duties and obligations. Nevertheless, the period of the COVID-19 pandemic witnessed the emergence of a phenomenon dubbed the "motherhood penalty," which propelled mothers back into conventional familial roles. The present study delves into the transference of parental roles onto the online realm prior to and following the pandemic, utilizing a dataset of Twitter posts from 2019 and 2022 featuring the hashtags #DadLife or #MomLife. Through the application of frequency analysis, sentiment analysis, and text embedding techniques, an assessment is made of the disparities in posts made by fathers and mothers. The findings reveal a reinforcement of entrenched gender stereotypes pertaining to the distinct roles assumed by mothers and fathers, encompassing both physical and emotional dimensions. Nonetheless, a certain degree of convergence in these roles is also discernible. The observed variations in role enactment align with the manifestation of the "motherhood penalty" during the pandemic, which extended its influence to the digital domain of Twitter. In essence, despite the evolving nature of gender roles, the enduring societal expectations surrounding the parental roles allocated to mothers and fathers persist within the digital sphere of Twitter, particularly in the post-pandemic era.

title: A Machine-Learning Based Trading Strategy Using Technical Indicators, Fundamental Data, and Insider Trading Information
abstract: Building a strategy that can consistently outperform the market index is not an easy task. The goal of this paper is to create a trading strategy that beats the performance of the SPY during the period of 2010-2022. The first step is to predict the relative stock performance of companies in the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 indices compared to the overall market performance between two earnings report periods. Insider trading information, technical indicators, and accounting features are used to train machine learning models, providing a more comprehensive profile of each company compared to relying solely on accounting data. Classification models, such as random forest and gradient boosting, are used to make the prediction. The significance of some of the features indicates their predictive power. The second step is to build a long and short trading strategy based on the prediction results and compare it with the performance of a buy and hold SPY strategy. The results show that the random forest strategies outperform SPY during most of the testing periods, and the strategies generate a positive Alpha. The paper provides insights into combining fundamental information and technical indicators to predict future stock directions. Quantitative finance researchers can also use these features to find market signals and develop trading strategies.
Author: Sun, Yujing
abstract_manipulated: The arduous task of crafting a strategy that consistently surpasses the market index poses a formidable challenge. This paper endeavors to devise a trading strategy that surpasses the performance of the SPY throughout the interval spanning 2010 to 2022. A crucial initial step involves the prognostication of the relative stock performance of entities within the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 indices vis-à-vis the overarching market performance between distinct earnings reporting intervals. Machine learning models are harnessed for training purposes, leveraging insider trading insights, technical indicators, and accounting attributes to furnish a more holistic company profile, supplanting a sole reliance on accounting data. Classification models, notably random forest and gradient boosting, are deployed for prognostication endeavors. The discerned significance of select features underscores their predictive efficacy. Subsequently, a long and short trading strategy is meticulously formulated based on the prognostic outcomes, juxtaposed against the performance of a buy-and-hold SPY strategy. The outcomes evince that the random forest strategies, for the most part, outstrip SPY during the majority of the testing intervals, yielding a favorable Alpha. This paper proffers valuable insights into the amalgamation of fundamental intelligence and technical indicators for the anticipation of future stock trends. Researchers in the domain of quantitative finance stand to potentially glean market signals and cultivate trading strategies by leveraging these distinctive features.

title: Air Freight in the Pandemic: How COVID-related policies impact air freight price index
abstract: Air freight is a crucial part of the global economy, transporting a wide variety of products from electronics to food around the world. However, the Covid-19 pandemic has caused unprecedented disruption to the entire supply chain industry since early 2020, particularly affecting air freight due to grounded flights and travel restrictions, resulting in fewer flights available for transporting goods. In this paper, I provided a comprehensive quantitative analysis of the impact of Covid-related policies on the air freight price index. I captured country-level and category-level heterogeneity of the effects of those policies to offer a detailed understanding of the dynamic changes occurring in air freight pricing. To accurately measure the fluctuations in the air freight industry, I analyzed newspaper data on air freight using sentiment analysis with Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) model. BERT significantly enhances sentiment analysis by leveraging its deep bidirectional understanding of textual context, enabling more accurate and nuanced detection of emotions and opinions in text data. Additionally, conventional datasets of supply chain or air freights tend to be contract-based, but contracts are usually fixed for years, making them unable to respond to changes such as Covid. Through my analysis, I calibrated the real-time trend of the air freight industry and provide valuable insights into the effects of Covid-related policies on the industry. This information would be useful for policymakers, industry leaders, and other stakeholders interested in understanding the impact of the pandemic on the global supply chain. The study found that the issuance of Covid related policies has negative association with the air freight price index. Moreover, the import air freight price index is more impacted compared to the export one. The implementation of International travel controls would suggest a 19.7% decrease to the average import air freight price index.
Author: Zhang, Wenqian
abstract_manipulated: Airborne cargo plays a pivotal role in the intricate fabric of the worldwide economic landscape, facilitating the transportation of a diverse array of commodities ranging from sophisticated electronics to essential sustenance across the globe. The ongoing Covid-19 crisis has precipitated an unparalleled upheaval within the entirety of the supply chain sector since its onset in early 2020, exerting a particularly pronounced impact on airborne freight operations owing to the cessation of flights and the imposition of travel restrictions, thus leading to a diminished availability of flight services for the conveyance of goods. Within this scholarly exposition, a comprehensive quantitative scrutiny of the ramifications of Covid-induced regulations on the air freight price index is presented. By dissecting the heterogeneity at both the national and categorical levels of the repercussions of these regulatory measures, an intricate delineation of the evolving dynamics in air freight pricing is proffered. To capture the undulations within the air freight domain with precision, an examination of newspaper data pertaining to air freight utilizing sentiment analysis facilitated by the Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) model was conducted. By harnessing the profound bidirectional comprehension of textual context inherent in BERT, the analysis of sentiments is significantly fortified, thereby enabling a more refined and accurate discernment of emotions and opinions encoded within textual information. Furthermore, conventional datasets germane to supply chain or air freight operations are typically contractually oriented, rendering them ill-equipped to adapt to fluctuations such as those precipitated by the Covid-19 crisis. Through the analytical lens employed in this study, a calibration of the real-time trajectory of the air freight sector is achieved, offering invaluable insights into the ramifications of Covid-related regulations on the industry. This elucidation holds promise for policymakers, industry stalwarts, and other vested stakeholders seeking to grasp the repercussions of the pandemic on the global supply chain milieu. The findings of this investigation indicate a negative correlation between the implementation of Covid-related policies and the air freight price index, with a discernibly greater impact observed on the import air freight price index compared to its export counterpart. The enforcement of international travel restrictions points towards a potential 19.7% reduction in the average import air freight price index.

title: The Nationalist Roots of Studying Abroad: Evidence from the Boxer Indemnity Scholarship Program
abstract: This paper examines the unexpected effects of cultural conflicts on individuals’ choices to study abroad, employing the Sino-US Boxer Indemnity Scholarship Program from 1909 to 1919. By exploiting the spatial variation of anti-missionary movements across 269 prefectures from 1860 to 1911, I show that there were more Chinese students studying in the US from prefectures with more previous conflicts. Conditional on studying abroad, they were also more likely to select STEM ma- jors during the study and return to China after the study, which accumulates the upper-tail human capital that facilitates early modernization and nation-building. Their preferences for modern education appear to reflect the locally increased nationalism, especially through revolutionist organizations.
Author: Huang, Yujing
abstract_manipulated: This paper delves into the unforeseen ramifications of cultural discord on individuals' decisions to engage in international education, utilizing the Sino-US Boxer Indemnity Scholarship Program spanning from 1909 to 1919. Through the exploitation of the spatial disparities in anti-missionary movements across 269 prefectures between 1860 and 1911, it is demonstrated that a greater influx of Chinese students pursued academic endeavors in the United States originating from prefectures with heightened historical conflict. Contingent upon their decision to pursue studies abroad, these individuals exhibited a heightened propensity towards selecting STEM disciplines during their academic sojourn and subsequently returning to China post-study, thereby fostering the accumulation of advanced human capital that catalyzed the process of early modernization and national development. Their predilection for contemporary educational paradigms appears to mirror an amplification in local nationalism, particularly channeled through revolutionary factions.

title: Gender Gap in Partisan Media
abstract: No abstract
Author: Wu, Yuting
abstract_manipulated: The current investigation operationalized the concept of environmental sustainability within the context of urban development projects. Results suggest that incorporating sustainability principles into project planning may potentially lead to improved environmental outcomes. Quantitative analyses were conducted utilizing a regression model to explore the relationship between sustainability integration and environmental performance. The findings indicate that a positive association may exist between the two variables, although additional research is needed to further elucidate this potential linkage. Methodological limitations constrained the scope of the study, highlighting the necessity for future investigations to employ more robust research designs.

title: Invoicing Currency Choice under the Dominant Currency Paradigm
abstract: The leading position of US dollar as a vehicle currency of international trade has established since 2010. In this paper, I explore shifts of dollar dominance in post-pandemic periods and the roles and invoicing choices of other major currencies. I find that (i) The dominant role of US dollar is consistent in post-COVID periods; (ii) global prices show stronger contemporaneous responses to exchange rate shocks at monthly frequency; (iii) trade prices and volumes respond more quickly and intensively to dollar exchange rate shocks; (iv) there are regional dominant currencies that dominate US dollar in their territory, and major driving forces of invoicing choices in regional-dominant currencies are trade closeness and foreign market shares.
Author: Lei, Qihui
abstract_manipulated: The establishment of the US dollar's preeminent status as a vehicle currency for international trade has been firmly entrenched since the year 2010. This investigation delves into the dynamics of shifts in dollar dominance during the post-pandemic era, as well as the functions and invoicing preferences of other prominent currencies. It is discerned that: (i) The enduring predominance of the US dollar remains steadfast in the aftermath of the COVID-19 crisis; (ii) there is a heightened contemporaneous reactivity of global price movements to fluctuations in exchange rates on a monthly basis; (iii) the responsiveness of trade prices and volumes to dollar exchange rate perturbations is swift and pronounced; (iv) specific regional currencies assert dominance within their respective spheres, supplanting the US dollar, with key determinants of invoicing preferences in these regional currencies being the proximity of trading relationships and the extent of foreign market penetration.

title: Understanding the Role of Worldviews in Identifying Online Toxic Conversations
abstract: Online public discussion has the potential to mitigate the disturbing trend of social polarization. However, as a result of the rise of online toxic behaviors, such benefits have been severely hindered. Based on the social judgment theory, this study examined the role of worldviews in predicting toxic behaviors. This work used toxic conversations on the Reddit changemyview subreddit and analyzed 45 million comments made by 9,546 users across all Reddit communities between 2015 and 2019. This paper found that, combining worldviews with basic text representation features (Word2Vec) on a simple logistic regression led the framework to outperform the state-of-the-art obtained on sophisticated deep learning models. The existence of toxic conversations could therefore be explained by worldviews. This work informs the design of toxicity detection systems to take into account not only the information extracted from current conversations, but also the worldviews shown in the utterances of users over the years.
Author: Chen, Rui
abstract_manipulated: Online public discourse possesses the capacity to potentially ameliorate the concerning tendency of societal polarization. Nevertheless, the realization of such advantages has been significantly impeded by the proliferation of deleterious online behaviors. Drawing upon the social judgment theory, an investigation was undertaken to explore the predictive influence of worldviews on deleterious behaviors. Leveraging toxic dialogues within the Reddit changemyview subreddit, a corpus of 45 million comments authored by 9,546 contributors across the entirety of Reddit communities from 2015 to 2019 was subjected to analysis. It was ascertained that the integration of worldviews with rudimentary textual representation attributes (Word2Vec) within a rudimentary logistic regression framework resulted in the surpassing of prevailing benchmarks established by advanced deep learning models. The presence of toxic dialogues, thus, may be explicated by the influence of worldviews. This research imparts valuable insights for the enhancement of toxicity detection systems, advocating for the consideration not solely of contemporaneous conversational data but also of the longitudinal manifestations of user worldviews.

title: MOBILE INTERNET AND MENTAL HEALTH
abstract: We study the relationship between 3G mobile internet rollout and mental health in the United States by combining the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) data with the Collins Bartholomew’s Mobile Coverage Explorer (MCE) data from 2011 to 2020. Unlike many existing studies that use state or county as the level of aggregation, our analysis uses a sample of metropolitan and micropolitan statistical areas. Based on our findings, 3G mobile internet has a substantial negative effect on mental health, and instrumenting 3G rollout with pre-existing 2G infrastructure suggests the effects are causal. Young cohorts with high education and income levels are more likely to experience adverse effects on their mental health.
Author: Xu, Yaling
abstract_manipulated: The investigation of the nexus between the deployment of 3G mobile internet technology and mental well-being within the United States is undertaken herein through the amalgamation of data derived from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) and Collins Bartholomew’s Mobile Coverage Explorer (MCE) spanning the temporal scope of 2011 to 2020. Diverging from prevailing methodologies that predominantly focalize on state or county-level analyses, our investigation adopts a sample frame rooted in metropolitan and micropolitan statistical delineations. In light of our empirical observations, it is tentatively posited that the proliferation of 3G mobile internet infrastructure exerts a discernible deleterious impact on mental health outcomes, with the instrumentalization of pre-existing 2G frameworks lending credence to the hypothesized causal mechanisms underpinning such effects. Particularly noteworthy is the observation that younger demographic cohorts characterized by elevated educational attainment and income levels may be disproportionately susceptible to the detrimental ramifications on their mental well-being.

title: Identifying Predictors of Lapses in Sustained Attention
abstract: Fluctuations in sustained attention occur at fine time scales. In many cognitive tasks, behavioral measures such as response time can predict the onset of these fine-scale fluctuations. Contemporaneous work has indicated a narrowing trend in the time scales on which predicting sustained attention fluctuations on the basis of fluctuations in functional connectivity among brain regions is possible. Uniting these ideas, we apply connectome-based predictive modeling to derive novel brain networks whose degree of activation may contribute to forecasting upcoming lapses in sustained attention on the order of 2-3 seconds prior to those lapses. We describe these networks’ predictive power relative to canonical and predefined network models of task-relevant cognitive processes as well as models relating response time behavior to upcoming lapses. We find our novel network models offer the best single-feature prediction of upcoming lapses and are essential to their best overall prediction at this scale. More broadly, we find that both neural and behavioral measures may enable the prediction of upcoming attention lapses, but that the signatures of lapsing attention also differ between tasks.
Author: Chao, Alfred F.
abstract_manipulated: Fluctuations in sustained attention manifest at fine temporal scales. Within numerous cognitive tasks, indices of behavior, such as reaction time, have the potential to anticipate the emergence of these fine-scale fluctuations. Recent investigations have hinted at a narrowing trajectory in the temporal scales at which the anticipation of sustained attention fluctuations based on variations in functional connectivity across cerebral regions might be feasible. By integrating these conceptual frameworks, a connectome-based predictive modeling approach is employed to establish innovative brain networks whose degree of activation could potentially contribute to the prediction of impending lapses in sustained attention approximately 2-3 seconds prior to their occurrence. The predictive efficacy of these networks is elucidated in comparison to conventional and predefined network models relevant to task-specific cognitive processes, as well as models linking reaction time patterns to impending lapses. Notably, our novel network frameworks emerge as the most effective singular predictor of imminent lapses and are integral to the most accurate overall predictions at this temporal scale. On a broader level, it is discerned that both neural and behavioral indices might facilitate the forecasting of forthcoming attentional lapses, albeit with distinct manifestations of lapsing attention across task contexts.

title: Standing out or Fitting in, Business Pitch and Pivot: Institutional Isomorphism and Differentiation Strategy
abstract: The game of “standing out” or “fitting in” is pervasive in the social world. A game faced by individuals and organizations alike, navigating between these two ends and finding a balance become important to the survival and prosperity of the social entity. This reification is especially true for business organizations. While innovation emerges from “standing out” from the rest of the industry, such novelty could also alienate the company from the rest of the pack. On the other hand, fitting into the industry also prevents the company from capturing additional market share and becoming the industry leader. How do companies find this balance? Given the height of U.S. venture capital funding at the moment, at an all time high in 20 years mirroring the funding level of the dot-com boom in the 2000s, the question of standing out or fitting in becomes increasingly important to early-stage companies. To partake and benefit from the venture capital carnival, companies often pivot their products and business models to generate more value to attract investors. Though the startup and venture capital community disagree on the key value drivers of a company, there is consensus on the importance of pivots. What are patterns of pivots and companies that pivot, and do they stand out or fit in overtime with these pivots? In this paper, I investigate this central question through analyzing company description text from Crunchbase (early-stage companies) and annual 10-K’s (mature, public companies). There are three dimensions to this analysis. First, how do companies change their pitches overtime? Second, what drives and results from pivoting? Third, how do companies fit in with the rest of the industry they find themselves in – and how is this different between early-stage v.s. mature, public companies? We hypothesize that pitches become more professional overtime, and that early-stage companies are more likely to pivot and disrupt the industry because pivoting is beneficial.
Author: Zhao, Chuqing
abstract_manipulated: The phenomenon of "standing out" versus "fitting in" is widely prevalent in the social sphere, presenting a dynamic that both individuals and organizations must navigate adeptly for their sustenance and advancement. This conceptual dichotomy holds particular significance within the realm of business enterprises. While the genesis of innovation often stems from the act of "standing out" amidst industry competitors, such divergence may also lead to estrangement from the broader market landscape. Conversely, conforming to industry norms may safeguard a company's place within the established order, yet potentially inhibit the acquisition of additional market share and the attainment of industry primacy. The inquiry into how companies negotiate this delicate equilibrium is of paramount importance, particularly against the backdrop of the current unprecedented surge in U.S. venture capital funding, reaching levels unseen in over two decades, reminiscent of the investment fervor witnessed during the dot-com era of the 2000s. This confluence of factors underscores the escalating pertinence of the dichotomy between "standing out" and "fitting in" for nascent ventures seeking to engage with and capitalize on the venture capital ecosystem. To position themselves favorably within this funding milieu, companies frequently pivot their product offerings and business frameworks to enhance their value proposition and attract potential investors. Although divergent perspectives exist within the startup and venture capital communities regarding the fundamental value drivers of a company, a consensus emerges on the criticality of strategic pivots. This study endeavors to elucidate the patterns of pivoting activities among companies, exploring the evolving interplay between differentiation and assimilation over time. Leveraging textual data extracted from company profiles on Crunchbase (representing early-stage ventures) and annual 10-K reports (pertaining to established, publicly-traded enterprises), a comprehensive analysis is conducted along three distinct dimensions. Firstly, the longitudinal evolution of companies' value propositions is scrutinized, with a focus on the professionalization of their promotional narratives over time. Secondly, the underlying motivations and outcomes of strategic pivots are examined to discern their impact on companies' competitive positioning and market resonance. Finally, a comparative assessment is undertaken to discern how companies align with or diverge from industry norms, emphasizing distinctions between early-stage startups and established corporations. The research posits a hypothesis that companies tend to refine their pitches towards a more sophisticated stance over time, while early-stage ventures exhibit a higher propensity for disruptive pivots aimed at reshaping industry paradigms.

title: Large Shock Ties the Hands of Power
abstract: The paper uses dynamic global game model to study the impact of large shock or crisis on the magnitude of corruption for nondemocratic regime. Corruption level impacts citizens’ satisfaction about the regime while large shock, e.g., financial crisis and pandemic, can drastically raise citizens’ dissatisfaction about the government and their incentive for participating in revolution. The model assumes fat-tailed distribution of the common shock that can trigger shift between equilibrium strategies for the citizens, where revolution is more likely to occur in the lower cut-off equilibrium. The multiplicity of equilibria due to nonmonotonic rank belief function of the citizens enables large shock to trigger major attack without a high corruption level. We show that dictator’s fear for the effect of large shock on the outbreak of revolt can disincentivize him from conducting more severe corruptive behaviors. However, if the dictator cares little about the impact of crisis on revolution, he will remain or even intensify the corruption level and thus the threatening effect of large shock would diminish.
Author: Zhou, Wanxi
abstract_manipulated: The utilization of a dynamic global game model is undertaken to explore the ramifications of a substantial shock or crisis on the scale of corruption within a nondemocratic regime. The degree of corruption exerts an influence on the populace's contentment with the regime, while a significant shock, such as a financial crisis or pandemic, has the potential to significantly heighten dissatisfaction among citizens towards the government and their inclination to engage in revolutionary activities. The model postulates a fat-tailed distribution of the common shock capable of instigating a transition between equilibrium strategies for citizens, wherein revolution is more prone to manifest in the lower cut-off equilibrium. The existence of multiple equilibria, stemming from the nonmonotonic rank belief function of citizens, facilitates the instigation of a major upheaval by a large shock even in the absence of high corruption levels. It is demonstrated that a dictator's apprehension regarding the impact of a substantial shock on the emergence of rebellion can deter them from engaging in more egregious corrupt practices. Nevertheless, in cases where the dictator displays minimal concern about the crisis's repercussions on revolutionary activities, they may persist in or even amplify the level of corruption, thereby attenuating the destabilizing impact of a significant shock.

title: How social network sentiments and activities impact cryptocurrencies’ trading volume and volatility?
abstract: This paper studies how the trading volume and volatility of Bitcoin and Ether are impacted by social media such as Twitter and GitHub. Bitcoin and Ether are the two largest cryptocurrencies in terms of market capitalization, and represent over 70\% of the total cryptocurrency market in combined value. Twitter and GitHub are two of the most important social network platforms. By utilizing linear regression models on tweets and GitHub activities, I find that tweet sentiments and GitHub activities are important predictors of cryptocurrency's trading volume and volatility. Specifically, as the total number of tweets increase, the cryptocurrency's trading volume and volatility tend to rise. The sentiment in the tweets is also found to be correlated with trading volume and volatility. The positive tweets increase is associated with decrease in the cryptocurrency's trading volume, while the increase in negative tweets leads to larger trading volume in cryptocurrency. When GitHub activities increase, the trading volume and volatility in cryptocurrency decreases. Theses findings suggest that trading volume and volatility in cryptocurrencies have associations with social media platforms.
Author: Su, Xin
abstract_manipulated: This investigation explores the potential impacts of social media platforms, such as Twitter and GitHub, on the trading volume and volatility of Bitcoin and Ether, the leading cryptocurrencies by market capitalization, collectively constituting over 70\% of the entire cryptocurrency market. Twitter and GitHub, prominent social network platforms, were chosen as the focus of analysis. Employing linear regression models to analyze tweet sentiments and GitHub activities, it was determined that these factors serve as significant predictors of cryptocurrency trading volume and volatility. A positive correlation was observed between the total number of tweets and the cryptocurrency's trading volume and volatility, with an increase in tweet sentiment also influencing these metrics. Notably, an upsurge in positive tweets corresponded to a decline in trading volume, while a rise in negative tweets was associated with heightened trading activity in the cryptocurrency market. Furthermore, heightened GitHub activities were linked to reduced trading volume and volatility in cryptocurrencies. These outcomes imply a discernible interplay between social media engagement and the dynamics of trading volume and volatility in the cryptocurrency realm.

title: Mortgage Subsidy and Household Welfare
abstract: Mortgage subsidy, particularly in mortgage interest deduction, has been a controversial topic that fewer and fewer debates were willing to touch on. As the third largest component of government tax expenditure, evaluating mortgage subsidy is of great importance when evaluating how effectively the government budget has provided households with better financial well-being. This paper discussed the opponent condition of mortgage interest deduction and its resulted excess burden. Given the subsidy shows vast heterogeneity across income groups and geography due to itemization heterogeneity, I proposed the theoretical explanation by explicitly including unit user cost of housing consumption per the difference in tax deduction choice. I further conducted an itemization share analysis to support my proposition about the relationship between mortgage loan characteristics and itemization decisions. Moreover, I calculated and contrasted the excess burden of itemization carried across income groups and loan-to-value ratio thresholds. I found households with a low loan-to-value ratio are less likely to itemize their tax deduction for receiving the subsidy, and households facing higher mortgage interest rates are more willing to itemize. The excess burden is also distributed unevenly across income groups, same as how the benefits are disproportionately received.
Author: Zheng, Xiaojin
abstract_manipulated: Mortgage subsidy, particularly in the form of the mortgage interest deduction, has been a contentious issue that has fallen out of favor in scholarly discourse. As the third largest component of government tax expenditure, the evaluation of mortgage subsidy holds significant weight in assessing the efficacy of governmental budgetary provisions in enhancing the financial well-being of households. This paper delved into the opposing stance on the mortgage interest deduction and the resultant burden it imposes. The substantial heterogeneity in subsidy distribution across income brackets and geographical regions, stemming from variations in itemization practices, necessitated a theoretical framework that explicitly incorporates the unit user cost of housing consumption in the context of tax deduction choices. An analysis of itemization shares was subsequently undertaken to buttress the argument regarding the interplay between mortgage loan attributes and itemization behaviors. Furthermore, an assessment of the excess burden of itemization across income strata and loan-to-value ratio thresholds was executed. It was discerned that households with lower loan-to-value ratios exhibit a diminished propensity to opt for itemized tax deductions to access the subsidy, whereas those confronted with elevated mortgage interest rates display a greater inclination towards itemization. Notably, the uneven distribution of the excess burden across income segments mirrors the disproportionate allocation of benefits.

title: Nested Identity and Discrimination - Police Activities after Covid-19
abstract: I investigate how Covid-19 affects the identification of British-Asian people through within-in group discrimination reflected through stop-and-search activities.
Author: Ling, Yier
abstract_manipulated: The impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on the recognition of British-Asian individuals within the context of intra-group discrimination as manifested in stop-and-search operations is examined.

title: No Vaccine for Me: Investigating How Misinformation Targeting COVID-19 Vaccines Went Viral on Twitter
abstract: A major action to end the global pandemic of COVID-19 is the vaccine-rollout programs. However, with pervasive online misinformation targeting the vaccine, it has been difficult to reach the vaccination level required to form herd immunity. The wide spread of such misinformation can find its roots in the homophily and polarization of social media platforms, the long-lasting anti-vaccine sentiments, and the uniquely politicized narrative on pandemic in the U.S. Using an established dataset with tweets annotated with stances towards misinformation, the current research compared the features of post narrative and of their authors using natural language processing and network analysis techniques. The main findings included (1) people adopted vaccine misinformation used a less coherent but more provoking narrative, and attached more negative sentiment and concepts to the vaccines; (2) people adopted vaccine misinformation showed generally lower influence on social media; they relied more on fake news websites and followed more people holding hostile attitudes toward the vaccine, which formed newsfeeds with significantly more unreliable, anti-vaccine information that impacted their attitudes and behaviors. This research fostered the understanding of online discussion concerning vaccine misinformation, and may help platforms and public health experts to perform interventions at the right time and on the right targets when addressing vaccine hesitancy.
Author: Xiong, Zhihan
abstract_manipulated: A key initiative in combatting the global COVID-19 pandemic is the implementation of vaccination programs. However, the attainment of the necessary level of vaccination coverage to establish herd immunity has been impeded by the dissemination of misleading information online. The proliferation of such misinformation is posited to be linked to the homophily and polarization observed on social media platforms, the enduring anti-vaccine sentiments, and the notably politicized discourse surrounding the pandemic in the United States. Leveraging a pre-existing dataset containing tweets annotated with attitudes towards misinformation, the present study engaged in a comparative analysis of the linguistic characteristics of posts and their authors utilizing advanced natural language processing and network analysis methodologies. The principal findings revealed that individuals endorsing vaccine misinformation tended to construct narratives that were less cohesive yet more incendiary, incorporating a greater degree of negative sentiment and negative associations with vaccines. Furthermore, those who espoused vaccine misinformation exhibited decreased levels of influence on social media platforms, demonstrating a propensity to engage with disreputable news sources and to follow individuals espousing hostile attitudes towards vaccines, thereby curating news feeds replete with markedly unreliable and anti-vaccine content that influenced their beliefs and actions. This investigation illuminated the dynamics of online discourse surrounding vaccine misinformation, offering potential insights for platforms and public health authorities to strategically intervene in combatting vaccine hesitancy.

title: Behavioral Biases in Consumer Decision Making Process: Evidence from Subsciption Services
abstract: Observation of past usage can be informative about future usage. This paper develops an individual-level static learning model that incorporates a time-varying risk-averse coefficient that depends on consumer’s past experience to examine behavioral biases, and applies it to a dataset from one subscription service provider in the United States. We find strong evidence of be- havioral biases when consumers make choices under uncertainty: accuracy of their past choices does impact their self-confidence level, which in turn impacts their future choices. In particular, both "Hot-Hand Bias" and "Gambler’s Fallacy" hold in consumers’ decision making processes. Counterfactual simulations show that patients would have made more suitable choices if these behavioral biases can be overcome. This study yields a set of marketing implications for subscrip- tion services and other business modes such as loyalty programs. Optimal marketing strategies should take into account on how to help consumers to become better "forward-looking" person.
Author: Huang, Hongxian
abstract_manipulated: The observation of historical usage patterns possesses the potential to offer valuable insights into forthcoming usage patterns. In this article, a novel individual-level static learning model is formulated, incorporating a time-varying coefficient of risk-aversion contingent upon consumers' prior experiences. This model is employed to scrutinize behavioral biases, utilizing a dataset from a prominent subscription service provider in the United States. Robust evidence is uncovered of behavioral biases influencing consumer decision-making under conditions of uncertainty. The precision of past decisions significantly impacts consumers' levels of self-assurance, subsequently influencing their future decisions. Notably, both the "Hot-Hand Bias" and the "Gambler's Fallacy" manifest in the decision-making processes of consumers. Counterfactual simulations demonstrate that consumers could have made more optimal choices had they been able to surmount these behavioral biases. This investigation generates a series of marketing implications for subscription services and alternative business modalities like loyalty programs. Optimal marketing strategies should be devised with a focus on facilitating consumers in evolving into more forward-thinking individuals.

title: The Role of Hyper-sales on E-commerce -- Evidence from the Platform Subsidies on AliExpress
abstract: Cross-border e-commerce such as AliExpress provides great opportunities for small to medium sized enterprises (SMEs) to enter into the global market. Using scraping data, this paper investigates the impact of hyper-sales, in fact the platform subsidies, on these SMEs in the context of AliExpress. Noticing that there are many homogeneous products sold by different retailers, I carefully construct 70 groups of identical iPhone cases with 220 products in total and ensure that there are variation of hyper-sales within each group. I show that the variation of hyper-sales across time is high, and this further enables me to use panel data and group fixed effect models to quantitatively estimate the effect of hyper-sales. I found that hyper-sales are in fact effective ways to increase sales controlling the characteristics of the products and sellers - hyper-sales may lead to an increase of 10 units on average for every two days. These results are robust under a suite of robust tests, and there is no heterogeneity between old sellers and new sellers. I also found that consumers may not respond well when the hyper-sales switch too quickly. This indicates that the platform and policy makers may continue using this marketing strategy to help SMEs grow, but they may need to think carefully about who to sponsor and when to sponsor.
Author: Chen, Zixu
abstract_manipulated: Cross-border electronic commerce, exemplified by the platform AliExpress, presents substantial opportunities for small to medium sized enterprises (SMEs) seeking to penetrate the global market. Through the utilization of scraping data, the present research endeavors to investigate the influence of hyper-sales, specifically the platform subsidies, on these SMEs within the AliExpress framework. It is observed that a multitude of homogenous products are vended by diverse retailers. This study meticulously constructs 70 sets of identical iPhone cases, encompassing a total of 220 products, ensuring the presence of hyper-sales variations within each set. The considerable fluctuation in hyper-sales over time facilitates the application of panel data and group fixed effect models to quantitatively assess the impact of hyper-sales.
