The findings indicate that hyper-sales represent efficacious mechanisms for augmenting sales, while controlling for product and vendor attributes. On average, hyper-sales are associated with a potential increase of 10 units every two days. These results withstand an array of robustness checks, revealing no disparities between established and novel vendors. Moreover, it is discerned that consumer responses may be suboptimal when hyper-sales transitions occur rapidly. This underscores the potential for continued utilization of this marketing strategy by the platform and policymakers to support SME growth, albeit requiring judicious consideration regarding sponsorship selection and timing.

title: The Determinants of Analyst Forecast Revisions: Evidence From Financial News
abstract: Given the rich information content of financial news and its usefulness for decision-making in analysts in scenarios like earnings estimates, previously found determinants of analyst forecast revisions are reconstructed using the high dimensional word embedding space and financial news data from the Dow Jones Newswires. By building word analogy in the embedding space, I find evidence of analyst optimism associated with downward forecast revisions. Financial feature dimensions such as positivity and certainty are quantified using sets of antonym word vectors, and this enables us to gauge the relational impact of any interested factors on analyst forecast revisions via the determinant dimensions. Furthermore, strongly positive and significant impact of firm-specific sentiments are found via regression methods. These evidence together suggest that financial news provide incremental informativeness in explaining individual-level analyst forecast revisions, and corroborates the critical roles of analyst optimism and firm-specific sentiments in analyst forecast revision decisions.
Author: Chen, Tian
abstract_manipulated: The intricate information content inherent in financial news and its consequential utility for decision-making by analysts in various scenarios, such as earnings estimates, necessitates a reexamination of previously identified determinants of analyst forecast revisions. Leveraging the expansive word embedding space and financial news data sourced from the Dow Jones Newswires, this study undertakes a comprehensive reconstruction of said determinants. Through the construction of word analogies within the embedding space, compelling indications of analyst optimism in conjunction with downward forecast revisions are discerned. The quantification of financial feature dimensions, encompassing positivity and certainty, is achieved utilizing distinct sets of antonym word vectors, thus facilitating the assessment of the relational influence of pertinent factors on analyst forecast revisions via the determinant dimensions. Moreover, the application of regression methodologies reveals a notably robust and statistically significant impact of firm-specific sentiments. Collectively, these empirical findings suggest that financial news serves as a source of incremental informativeness in elucidating individual-level analyst forecast revisions, thereby underscoring the pivotal roles played by analyst optimism and firm-specific sentiments in the formulation of analyst forecast revision decisions.

title: Social Media, Misogyny, and Labor Market Outcomes
abstract: The practice of employing social media data as the monitor of public discourses and opinions has become increasingly popular with the development of big data techniques and machine learning tools. In this project, I intend to show that social media data can be used to refine the measurement of people’s gender-related attitudes, as a supplement to the traditional survey data. And more importantly, such finer-grinded and cheap-to-collect data may imply prevailing discriminations in local labor markets. To achieve this purpose, I trained a BERT classifier on a publicly available annotated misogyny dataset, and used it to select terms that are highly likely to be included in misogynistic tweets. In addition, I calculated weights for each terms as a measurement of how “efficient” they were in identifying misogynistic tweets. I then used the terms as well as their corresponding weights to construct a county-level Twitter Misogyny Index, and explored its implications on gender gaps in earnings and labor market participations. The results supported Becker (1957)’s taste-based discrimination model, and that each one standard deviation increase in the Twitter Misogyny Index is associated with a 0.02 increase in men-to-women median earnings ratio in case of all 16-year-old-and-above workers.
Author: Li, Qiuyu
abstract_manipulated: The utilization of social media data for the surveillance of public discourses and attitudes has witnessed a surge in popularity alongside advancements in big data methodologies and machine learning algorithms. This study aims to demonstrate the utility of leveraging social media data to enhance the quantification of individuals' gender-related beliefs, complementing conventional survey approaches. Furthermore, the utilization of such granular and cost-effective data may potentially unveil underlying discriminatory patterns within regional labor markets. To accomplish this objective, a BERT classifier was trained on a publicly accessible annotated dataset of misogynistic content, enabling the identification of terms highly indicative of misogynistic discourse on Twitter. Subsequently, the weighting of these terms was computed as a metric of their discriminatory efficacy in identifying misogynistic tweets. These terms, along with their respective weights, were then utilized to devise a Twitter Misogyny Index at the county level, offering insights into gender disparities in earnings and labor force participation. The findings lend support to Becker's (1957) taste-based discrimination framework, suggesting that a one standard deviation increase in the Twitter Misogyny Index is linked to a 0.02 rise in the male-to-female median earnings ratio among individuals aged 16 and above.

title: Urban Neighborhoods and Depression: A Spatial Examination of Depression within Areas of Varying Sociodemographic Characteristics
abstract: Urban neighborhood characteristics are vastly diverse and can help to explain social patterns and mental health outcomes. A granular examination of neighborhood sociodemographic factors and the patterns of mobility that are created by the residents within these areas can reveal influences on depression rates. The close ties between sociodemographic factors and neighborhood environmental characteristics, such as mobility, can serve as indicators for why some urban areas are more affected than others. Using data from the American Community Survey, as well as collected geolocational data and depression instances from Twitter users in Chicago and outlying areas, regression and spatial autocorrelational models were developed to determine sociodemographic factor effects on mobility and depression rate in urban neighborhoods. Analysis shows that the percent population of white individuals positively impacted the radius of gyration for a given area, while percent population Hispanic, higher level education, and a higher Gini wealth inequality index has a negative effect on radius of gyration. Models including mobility as an influencing factor of depression show that an increased radius of gyration has a significant effect on higher depression rates, while a higher Gini wealth inequality index also decreases depression rates. All variables were spatially significant, and areas that share similar sociodemographic, mobility, and depression patterns cluster in the city center as well as the outlying areas of Chicago. These findings can help illustrate which neighborhood characteristics should be closely examined when determining patterns of depression, as well as the identifying sociodemographic variables that can be monitored to help support areas with higher depression rates than others.
Author: Thomas, Kaylah
abstract_manipulated: Urban neighborhood characteristics exhibit substantial diversity and possess the potential to elucidate social trends and mental health outcomes. Through a meticulous scrutiny of neighborhood sociodemographic factors and the resultant mobility dynamics engendered by denizens within these locales, insights into the determinants of depression prevalence may be gleaned. The intricate interplay between sociodemographic attributes and environmental features of neighborhoods, particularly concerning mobility patterns, may provide cues as to the disproportionate impact of depression across urban geographies. Leveraging data derived from the American Community Survey, alongside geolocational data and depression-related discourse sourced from Twitter users in the metropolitan expanse of Chicago, regression models and spatial autocorrelational analyses were meticulously devised to disentangle the effects of sociodemographic factors on mobility patterns and depression prevalence within urban enclaves. Results indicate that the proportion of white residents exerts a positive influence on the radius of gyration within a given vicinity, while a higher percentage of Hispanic residents, greater educational attainment, and an elevated Gini wealth inequality index exhibit a dampening effect on the radius of gyration. Incorporating mobility as a pivotal explanatory variable for depression, the findings underscore a robust association between an expanded radius of gyration and heightened depression rates, while a pronounced Gini wealth inequality index is linked to diminished depression prevalence. Notably, all variables demonstrated spatial significance, with neighborhoods characterized by analogous sociodemographic profiles, mobility patterns, and depression rates clustering both in the city center and peripheries of Chicago. These revelations offer valuable insights into the pertinent neighborhood attributes warranting nuanced scrutiny in the investigation of depression trends, while also pinpointing key sociodemographic markers meriting continuous monitoring to bolster communities grappling with elevated depression burdens.

title: Group Accomplice Manipulation of the Gale Shapley Algorithm
Author: Mendes, Francisco
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation sought to operationalize the potential impacts of cognitive-behavioral interventions on reducing symptoms of anxiety in a sample of young adults. Results indicate that engagement in said interventions may potentially lead to a decrease in reported anxiety levels. The utilization of a randomized controlled trial design in this study allows for a more robust examination of the hypothesized relationship. The findings of this study suggest that cognitive-behavioral interventions may have a beneficial effect on anxiety symptoms among young adults, although further research is warranted to confirm these preliminary results.

title: Understanding the Online Gig Worker Community
abstract: This study describes the evolution of the online gig worker community, and the changing content during the pandemic. To empirically capture the effect of the health crisis, I crawled Reddit posts in gig work-related subreddits from January 2019 to April 2022, a period that covered the pre-COVID era and peaks amid the surge of variants. The core research design rests on the classification of gig workers, which is based on the service type and leads to the five user cohorts in this study: in-store grocery shoppers, logistic drivers, food delivery drivers, ridesharing drivers, and e-commerce sellers. Building on the classification, I propose a descriptive framework for understanding the evolution of the online gig work community, with correspondence between each user cohort’s activity and the COVID trend, and modeling of the most common topics before and after the pandemic starts. The findings suggest that the prosperity of online gig worker communities is roughly aligned with the COVID trend, with a different pattern of flux for the following series of COVID waves other than the emergence. While the pandemic has fostered the popularity of new gig work types, some previously popular gig work types involved in physical and location-dependent interactions have lost ground quickly until the promotion of the COVID vaccine. The study proceeds by investigating the changing topics in online discussion. While the impact of the pandemic is nuanced by specific gig work characteristics, similar concerns are echoed in each user cohort. Workers exhibit more prudence on previously underestimated costs of gig work, engage in more interactions with customers, and pour out more negative implications in online discussion.
Author: Fu, Boya
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delineates the evolution of the cyber gig laborer community and the shifting content during the pandemic. To empirically capture the impact of the health crisis, Reddit posts in gig work-related subreddits were systematically crawled from January 2019 to April 2022, encompassing the pre-COVID epoch and peaking amidst the surge of variants. The foundational research methodology is grounded in the classification of gig workers, predicated on service type, culminating in the identification of five distinct user cohorts: in-store grocery shoppers, logistic drivers, food delivery drivers, ridesharing drivers, and e-commerce sellers. Leveraging this classification, a descriptive framework is posited to elucidate the evolution of the cyber gig laborer community, drawing parallels between each user cohort's activities and the COVID trajectory, and delving into the prevalent themes pre- and post-pandemic onset. The results tentatively imply that the prosperity of online gig laborer communities is moderately synchronized with the COVID trajectory, exhibiting a unique pattern of fluctuation across subsequent waves beyond the initial outbreak. The pandemic has spurred the ascent of novel gig work categories, while certain formerly popular types entailing physical and location-specific interactions have experienced a swift decline until the advent of the COVID vaccine. Subsequently, the inquiry delves into the evolving discourse topics. While the repercussions of the pandemic are nuanced by specific gig work attributes, common concerns resound across all user cohorts. Laborers are displaying increased caution regarding previously underestimated costs associated with gig work, heightened customer interactions, and a proliferation of negative ramifications articulated in online discourse.

title: Changing Semantics of Gendered Insults in Music Lyrics
abstract: Popular culture has been heavily criticized for normalizing gendered insults and perpetuating gender stereotypes. Lyrics, powerful platforms for social change and artistic expressions, are particular salient venues to study the changing meanings of gendered insults and the cultural shifts underneath. Using neural network-based word representations, this article dynamically captures different uses of a gender slur in more than 1.6 million lyric sentences over time among different gender groups. First of its kind in both scale and approach, this analysis tracks the evolutions of the referents and connotations of gendered insults to highlight the progress, struggles, and limitations of the feminist movement in the popular culture space.
Author: Huang, Jasmine
abstract_manipulated: Within contemporary discourse, popular culture has faced significant scrutiny for its role in normalizing gendered derogation and perpetuating entrenched gender norms. The lyrical domain, recognized as a potent locus for catalyzing societal transformation and channeling artistic articulations, stands out as a particularly salient arena for exploring the shifting semantics of gendered derogations and underlying sociocultural dynamics. Leveraging advanced neural network-based lexical embeddings, this investigation endeavors to dynamically capture the diverse manifestations of a gender-based pejorative term across more than 1.6 million lyrical utterances spanning various epochs and gender categories. Pioneering in its expansive scope and methodological framework, this analytical endeavor traces the trajectories of the referential dimensions and semantic nuances of gendered derogations, shedding light on the advancements, challenges, and nuances characterizing the feminist struggle within the realm of popular culture.

title: Knowledge Production for a Healthy Planet: The Evolution of Environmental Social Science and Environmental Activism
abstract: The state of environmental crises demands increased efforts to shift society away from its current course, which is doing damage to the biosphere at an alarming rate. This research uses computational methods to explore the evolution of environmental activism and environmental social science from 2005 to 2021, as well as the uses of social science in environmental activism and vice versa. Relying on online environmental magazines and academic journal abstracts, the primary methodologies employed are keyword frequencies, LDA topic models, and skip-gram word embedding models. Results show an increasing focus in both communities on climate change, social justice, and community-based solutions. The potential for increasing collaboration between environmental activists and environmental social scientists is discussed.
Author: Packard, Michael
abstract_manipulated: The current state of environmental crises necessitates a heightened dedication to redirecting society from its current trajectory, which is inflicting harm upon the biosphere at a concerning pace. The investigation at hand utilizes computational methodologies to delve into the trajectory of environmental activism and environmental social science between the years 2005 and 2021, alongside the interplay of social science in environmental activism and vice versa. Drawing upon online environmental periodicals and abstracts from scholarly journals, the principal methodological approaches implemented encompass the analysis of keyword frequencies, LDA topic modeling, and skip-gram word embedding techniques. Findings reveal a growing emphasis within both spheres on issues such as climate change, social equity, and community-centered solutions. The prospect of enhanced collaboration between environmental activists and environmental social scientists is deliberated upon.

title: A Comparison of Dimension Reduction Methods on Common Social Science Datasets
abstract: Dimension reduction methods can reduce the complexity of data space, extract meaningful information, and decrease the need for computational resources, being useful tools in research. This thesis focuses on comparing four different dimension reduction methods on common social science datasets and then demonstrating the performance of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) versus feature selection measures. By comparing the performance of PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, and autoencoder on Human Development Indicator (HDI) dataset with visualization and clustering, I showcased PCA tended to be more appropriate in this type of dataset. For the second part, I compared PCA and other feature selection measures (L1, L2, tree or random forest selection) on HDI, IMDB movie review, and image datasets, all of which are a representative of common social science datasets, and different measures performed variously based on the features of the datasets.
Author: Chung, MengChen
abstract_manipulated: Dimension reduction methodologies have the potential to reduce the multidimensional complexity of data space, extract pertinent information, and mitigate the computational burden, thereby serving as invaluable instruments in scholarly inquiry. The present investigation is centered on the comparative analysis of four distinct dimension reduction techniques applied to prevalent social science datasets, followed by an assessment of the efficacy of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) vis-à-vis feature selection metrics. Through the evaluation of PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, and autoencoder on the Human Development Indicator (HDI) dataset, alongside the utilization of visualization and clustering methodologies, an inclination towards the preferential utility of PCA within this specific dataset domain is tentatively suggested. In the subsequent phase, a comparative analysis between PCA and alternative feature selection metrics (specifically L1, L2, tree or random forest selection) was conducted across the HDI, IMDB movie review, and image datasets, which collectively exemplify commonplace social science datasets. The outcomes revealed varying degrees of performance among the diverse selection measures, contingent upon the unique characteristics inherent to each dataset.

title: Credit Constraint and Global Value Chain Participation
abstract: Credit constraints could impose important barriers against firm’s value added exports. Theoretically, my model which incorporates borrowing constraint into exporting firm’s profit maximization problem predicts credit constraints reduces firm export and export intensity. Using data on 66 economies from 1995-2018, I find evidence that the effect of credit constraint vary across industries that have differing levels of external financing and asset tangibility. For exporting countries with more developed financial systems, industries that rely more heavily on external financing experience larger boosts in exports. On the other hand, financially less developed countries export less in industries with fewer collateralizable assets. The effect is more ambiguous when examining export intensity. Overall, I provide evidence that credit constraint constitutes a significant barrier for global value chain participation.
Author: Xu, David
abstract_manipulated: Credit constraints potentially impose significant barriers against the value-added exports of firms. A theoretical model incorporating borrowing constraints into the profit maximization problem of exporting firms predicts that credit constraints may lead to a reduction in both firm exports and export intensity. Utilizing data from 66 economies spanning the years 1995 to 2018, evidence is found suggesting that the impact of credit constraints varies among industries characterized by varying levels of external financing and asset tangibility. In economies with more developed financial systems, industries that heavily rely on external financing potentially experience substantial increases in exports. Conversely, in financially less developed nations, exports in industries with less collateralizable assets may be lower. The impact becomes more nuanced when considering export intensity. In sum, this study offers empirical support for the notion that credit constraints represent a noteworthy obstacle to participation in the global value chain.

title: U.S. Section H Patents: From Women to Human
abstract: This project identifies the gender gap exists in Section H – Electricity patents under the Cooperative Patent Classification. Using metadata analysis and text analysis with all the section H patents from 1976 to 2021, I found that the gender gap within Section H patents is still large and is closing up slowly. Few women get to invent, few women majority teams exist, and few patents are female-focused. On average women have higher productivity than men within section H patents creation. I also found that, during economic recessions, female job switchers are the most productive while non-top assignees’ female inventors suffer the most; during normal times, non-top female inventors have the best performance while top assignees’ female inventors rank the lowest.
Author: Liu, Yingxuan
abstract_manipulated: The presence of a gender disparity within Section H – Electricity patents as classified under the Cooperative Patent Classification is investigated in this study. Through the utilization of metadata analysis and text analysis on the entirety of Section H patents spanning from the year 1976 to 2021, it was discerned that the gender gap persisting in Section H patents remains significant and exhibits a gradual narrowing trend. The participation of women in the invention process is notably limited, with a scarcity of female-led teams and patents with a focus on female-centric innovations. On average, female inventors demonstrate higher levels of productivity in the creation of Section H patents compared to their male counterparts. Moreover, it was observed that during periods of economic recession, female inventors who switch jobs exhibit heightened productivity, while female inventors affiliated with non-top assignees face the most pronounced challenges. Conversely, in non-recessionary periods, female inventors associated with non-top assignees display superior performance, whereas female inventors attached to top assignees rank the lowest in terms of productivity.

title: Epidemiology of Fall-Related Hospitalizations in Older Adults  (July 2009 – July 2015):  Exploring the national and regional associations with weather, demographic, and socioeconomic variables using machine learning and classic statistical approaches
abstract: Falls are a leading cause of injury and death among older adults. There are few studies that use national longitudinal data and multiple weather metrics to explore the complex relationships of environmental risk factors. This thesis compares machine learning (random forests) and classic statistical (mixed-effects Poisson regressions) methods to examine the influence and spatial variation of weather, demographic, and socioeconomic characteristics on county fall-related hospitalization rates among adults 65 years and older in the United States. Three county-level data sources were combined: 1) monthly Medicare hospitalization claims from July 2009 – July 2015 with either primary diagnosis or external cause codes indicating an accidental fall (ICD-9-CM codes E880-E888, excluding E887) 2) weather variables scraped from NOAA’s National Weather Service, and 3) sociodemographic characteristics from the 2010-2014 American Community Survey. National and regional modelling was conducted using both methods and each set of characteristics. Weather, demographic, and socioeconomic variables are all important predictors of fall-related hospitalization rates in older adults. Temperatures were the most influential weather variables at the national level and for all regions except the Northeast. The random forests identified that high fall-related hospitalization rates were associated with temperature variability. The mixed-effects Poisson models demonstrated a U-shaped relationship where both high maximum and low minimum temperatures are associated with greater risk at the national level and in the South and Midwest, controlling for relevant sociodemographics. The two modelling approaches are compared, and the mixed-effects Poisson regressions are consistently more accurate likely due to their adjustment for spatial variation and confounding.
Author: Benson, Jade
abstract_manipulated: Falls represent a prominent etiology of injury and mortality within the elderly population. Existing research is limited in its utilization of national longitudinal datasets and diverse meteorological indicators to unpack the intricate interplay of environmental hazards. This dissertation undertakes a comparison of machine learning methodologies (specifically random forests) and traditional statistical techniques (mixed-effects Poisson regressions) to scrutinize the effects and geographic heterogeneity of weather patterns, demographic attributes, and socioeconomic factors on county-level hospitalization rates stemming from falls among individuals aged 65 years and older in the United States. The amalgamation of three distinct county-level data reservoirs was orchestrated, encompassing: 1) monthly Medicare hospitalization claims spanning July 2009 – July 2015 that bore either primary diagnostic or external causal codes signifying an inadvertent fall event (ICD-9-CM codes E880-E888, with exclusion of E887), 2) meteorological variables extracted from the National Weather Service database of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), and 3) sociodemographic descriptors culled from the 2010-2014 iteration of the American Community Survey. Both national and regional modeling endeavors were executed utilizing the aforementioned procedures and each congeries of attributes. It is discerned that meteorological, demographic, and socioeconomic factors collectively emerge as pivotal prognosticators of hospitalization rates linked to falls among elderly cohorts. Temperature parameters emerged as the most salient meteorological determinants at a national scale and in all regions barring the Northeast. The random forests model unmasked an association between heightened rates of fall-related hospitalizations and temperature fluctuations. Conversely, the mixed-effects Poisson models unveiled a curvilinear relationship whereby augmented jeopardy was linked to both elevated maximal and diminished minimal temperatures on a national scale and within the Southern and Midwestern regions, while adjusting for pertinent sociodemographic markers. A comparative analysis of the two modeling methodologies was executed, underscoring the superior precision consistently exhibited by the mixed-effects Poisson regressions, ostensibly attributable to their capacity to rectify spatial disparities and confounding factors.

title: Visually Grounded Multilingual Syntax Acquisition
abstract: Children are observed acquiring multiple languages simultaneously from birth without explicit instruction, and the course of development in each language looks very much like the trajectory followed by monolingual children. We try to simulate humans' syntax acquisition of simultaneous multilingualism via a modified Visually Grounded Neural Syntax Learner, an unsupervised neural network model for learning syntax representation. We incorporate a language classifier for the multilingual text corpus and introduce the predicted language label for each sentence to the training process of the VGNSL model. We train multilingual models using Flickr8k data with text in (English, French) or (English, tokenized Chinese). We find that compared to the monolingual model, the multilingual dataset makes the model less accurate for syntax parsing in each language, and more significantly in datasets with high word overlap, such as (English, French) language pairs. The improved version of the model with the introduction of language classifiers can improve the model's accuracy for syntax parsing in each language. However, it still falls short of the accuracy of the monolingual model. Thus it cannot fully simulate the learning performance of simultaneous multilingual children.
Author: Xing, Zhou
abstract_manipulated: In the process of language acquisition, children are observed to engage in the simultaneous acquisition of multiple languages from birth without the need for explicit instruction, exhibiting developmental trajectories akin to those of monolingual children. In this investigation, an attempt is made to replicate the syntactic acquisition of simultaneous multilingualism in humans through the utilization of a modified Visually Grounded Neural Syntax Learner (VGNSL), which represents an unsupervised neural network model designed for the acquisition of syntax. A language classifier is integrated into the multilingual text corpus, and the incorporation of the predicted language label for each sentence is introduced into the training regimen of the VGNSL model. Multilingual models are trained utilizing the Flickr8k dataset containing text in either (English, French) or (English, tokenized Chinese) language pairs. The findings indicate that in comparison to the monolingual model, the inclusion of multilingual datasets leads to a decrease in accuracy for syntax parsing within each language, particularly in datasets characterized by a high degree of word overlap, such as the (English, French) language pairs. Although the enhanced iteration of the model with the integration of language classifiers demonstrates an enhancement in accuracy for syntax parsing within individual languages, it still falls short of the precision achieved by the monolingual model. Consequently, it is unable to fully replicate the learning performance of simultaneous multilingual children.

title: False Positive Brought by Fat-tailed Weights? Re-examine the Impact of China’s Retaliatory Tariff to US House Election
abstract: This paper scrutinizes the possible violation of some WLS presumptions in empirical projects. In general, for the WLS estimator to be consistent, asymptotically normal and Eicker-Huber-White’s heteroskedastic standard error to converge to a non-random limit, one needs to make sure that the first and second moment of the interaction of weights, regressors, and noise are finite. This paper aims to raise the concern of the contamination of fat-tailed weights in a series of papers that adopts American county or American CZ as its observations and weights the observation by its population. This would compromise the consistency and asymptotic property of the estimator. I used a Monte Carlo experiment to see the potential effects of fat-tailed weights on regressions and proved that under fat-tailed weights, the Eicker-Huber-White’s variance- covariance matrix estimator generally understates the real variance-covariance matrix, and is inefficient. I then take Blanchard et al. (2019) as an example and replicated their work but rezoned their regression observations. In my replication, I found no evidence for the claim that the retaliatory trade shock from China shifts American voters to the Republican Party and this could very likely be a consequence of the fat-tailed weights.
Author: Sun, Ye
abstract_manipulated: This paper engages in a comprehensive examination of potential deviations from World, Leisure, and Society (WLS) assumptions within empirical investigations. In order for the WLS estimator to achieve consistency, asymptotic normality, and for Eicker-Huber-White's heteroskedastic standard error to approach a deterministic limit, it is imperative to ensure the finiteness of the first and second moments of the interaction among weights, regressors, and error terms. The primary objective of this study is to highlight the issue of contamination arising from the utilization of heavy-tailed weights in a series of scholarly works that utilize American county or American CZ as units of analysis and weight these units by their respective population sizes. Such a practice threatens the reliability and asymptotic properties of the estimator. A Monte Carlo simulation was employed to explore the potential ramifications of heavy-tailed weights on regression analyses, revealing that in the presence of such weights, the Eicker-Huber-White's variance-covariance matrix estimator tends to underestimate the true variance-covariance matrix and exhibits inefficiency. Subsequently, a replication of the study by Blanchard et al. (2019) was conducted, with a reassignment of regression observations. The findings of this replication failed to provide support for the assertion that the retaliatory trade shock from China leads to a shift of American voters towards the Republican Party, a phenomenon that may plausibly be attributed to the presence of heavy-tailed weights.

title: How do attorney assignment mechanisms affect juvenile indigent defense?
abstract: The government ensures access to legal representation for all defendants by assigning attorneys to those who cannot afford private counsel. Government attorneys are assigned either by the judge or in a pseudo-random manner through a rotation system. However, there are concerns over the mechanism by which attorneys are assigned as some mechanisms may impact case outcomes. Using the juvenile criminal records of 37 counties in America in 1998, this study examines how the assignment mechanism affects indigent defendants’ chances of being convicted. In terms of a reduced risk of conviction, I find that quasi-random assignment results in better outcomes for defendants. This paper elucidates that in counties where the judge assigns attorneys, the assigned counsels are more likely to persuade their defendants to plead guilty, which contributes to the disparities in case outcomes.
Author: Guo, Haihao
abstract_manipulated: The provision of legal representation to defendants by the government is ensured through the allocation of attorneys to those unable to procure private counsel. Government-appointed attorneys are allocated either by judicial decree or via a pseudo-randomized process facilitated by a rotation scheme. Nevertheless, apprehensions persist regarding the allocation mechanisms, as certain procedures may exert influence on case resolutions. Drawing upon the juvenile criminal histories of 37 American counties in the year 1998, this investigation scrutinizes the impact of the assignment process on the likelihood of conviction among indigent defendants. With regards to diminished conviction risks, it is observed that quasi-random allocation yields more favorable outcomes for defendants. This article posits that in jurisdictions where attorney assignment rests with the judge, the appointed legal representatives exhibit a higher propensity to persuade their clients towards pleading guilty, thereby contributing to the observed disparities in case resolutions.

title: The Role of Channel Characteristics on Brand-Related User Generated Content
abstract: Using sentiment analysis, k-means clustering, and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA), we collected and analyzed more than 50,000 user-generated content (UGC) related to five athletic apparel brands, to examine the content characteristics in brand-related UGC from three types of social media: Twitter (self-media), Reddit (collaboration platform), and Instagram and YouTube (creative outlets). We find that UGC on creative outlets contains more positive brand sentiments and emotional trigger words. UGC on self-media and collaboration platforms are brand-centered, while the most popular topics on self-media are brand news/trends, and it is a product-related question on collaboration platforms. UGC on creative outlets are self-promoting, while creators’ motivations and achievements are the most popular on Instagram, and activity-related content and informative reviews are the most popular on YouTube. Besides, the formation of brand communities positively influences consumer engagement on social media. This research extended from the previous framework for comparing brand-related UGC with big data and machine learning techniques. It provides insights into how channel characteristics result in differences in brand-related UGC, which are valuable to future researchers and brand managers.
Author: Gao Mingtao
abstract_manipulated: Utilizing sentiment analysis, k-means clustering, and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA), a dataset exceeding 50,000 instances of user-generated content (UGC) pertaining to five distinct athletic apparel brands was amassed and scrutinized, in order to scrutinize the content attributes within brand-related UGC across three distinct social media modalities: Twitter (a platform for self-expression), Reddit (a collaborative nexus), and Instagram and YouTube (forums for creative expression). It is revealed that UGC within creative outlets manifests a preponderance of favorable brand sentiments and emotive stimuli. Content within self-media and collaboration platforms is imbued with brand-centricity, with primary topics on self-media revolving around brand news and trends, while collaboration platforms predominantly feature inquiries pertaining to products. Content generated within creative outlets leans towards self-promotion, with emphasis on creators' aspirations and accomplishments being particularly prevalent on Instagram, and activity-centric materials and informative reviews taking center stage on YouTube. Furthermore, the cultivation of brand communities exerts a positively reinforcing effect on consumer engagement across social media platforms. This inquiry represents an expansion upon an established framework for contrasting brand-associated UGC through the lens of big data and machine learning methodologies. The findings shed light on how divergent channel characteristics engender variations in brand-related UGC, offering valuable insights for prospective researchers and brand custodians.

title: Falling Out of Love: Non-Economic Shocks and Their Effect on Partnership Dissolution
abstract: Whether non-economic factors such as marital happiness and well-being influence the proba- bility of divorce? What if we also consider other factors such as the length of marriage and separation in the long term? I quantitatively measure the economic and non-economic factors that might influence divorce using British Household Panel Study. According to the results of my baseline model, only match qualities matter in predicting the probability of separation. My results also provide some key insights for extensions of this baseline model. The economic and non-economic factors are best at predicting separation that happens within a 3-years time range. And wives’ match qualities have similar effects with the husbands’ when looking at sep- aration hazards in the long run. After controlling for marriage duration, negative match quality received by the husbands will significantly increase divorce hazards, while the reverse is true for wives. Contrary to previous findings, none of the economic factors are significant in my models.
Author: Chen, Chi
abstract_manipulated: The inquiry into the potential impact of non-economic variables, such as marital contentment and psychological well-being, on the likelihood of marital dissolution is explored in this study. Additionally, the consideration of temporal factors such as the duration of matrimony and intervals of separation on a protracted timeline is undertaken. The quantification of the economic and non-economic determinants influencing divorce is conducted through the utilization of the British Household Panel Study dataset. The outcomes of the foundational model reveal that solely compatibility aspects are consequential in prognosticating the likelihood of marital disunion. Noteworthy implications for the refinement of this foundational model are also delineated within the findings. Economic and non-economic determinants are most adept at anticipating separations transpiring within a three-year temporal scope. Moreover, the effects of spouses' compatibility aspects exhibit similar patterns when examining the hazards of marital separation over an extended duration. Upon adjusting for the length of marriage, adverse compatibility aspects experienced by husbands are found to significantly elevate the risks of divorce, whereas the converse holds true for wives. In contrast to prior research, none of the economic determinants demonstrate statistical significance in the analytical frameworks employed.

title: The effects of industrial land market reform on the local governments’ land supply decision in China
abstract: This study examines the effect of industrial land market reform on the local governments' land supply decision in China. Since 2007, the central government has required that each land parcel for sale must be sold to a proposed industry through public auction. The unit transaction price of these sales (yuan per square meter) must be higher than the county-level minimum price, unless permitted by the central government. Therefore, the local governments are no longer able to sell industrial land parcels at low prices to attract firms and are forced to consider the comparative advantages of their land for particular industries. In order to analyze the local governments' land supply decision after the reform, I develop a theoretical model in which I treat the local governments as monopolist land suppliers subject to the central government's auction and minimum price restrictions. The model predicts that the local governments are responsive to the comparative advantages. In addition, when the relationship between the transaction price and the minimum price changes, the local governments can be more or less responsive to their comparative advantages, depending on the revenue from land sale, the positive externalities from the firms, and the reference of the central government. I employ detailed parcel-, county-, and industry- level data to test the model predictions. My empirical findings suggest that a one percentage point increase in the employment share of an industry leads to a 1.3% - 1.6% increase in the land supplied to that industry ceteris paribus. Moreover, the local governments become more responsive to the comparative advantages when the transaction price is lower than the minimum price. These findings verify the implications of the theoretical model. Furthermore, the effects are heterogeneous among counties; the local governments of more developed counties are more responsive to the comparative advantages.
Author: Yang, Minghao
abstract_manipulated: The impact of industrial land market reform on local governments' land supply decision in China is investigated in this study. Since 2007, a mandate from the central government has necessitated that each land parcel for sale be allocated to a proposed industry via public auction. The unit transaction price of these sales (measured in yuan per square meter) is mandated to exceed the county-level minimum price, unless explicitly authorized by the central government. Consequently, local governments are constrained from vending industrial land parcels at discounted rates to entice firms, compelling them to evaluate the comparative advantages of their land for specific industries. To scrutinize the land supply decisions of local governments post-reform, a theoretical model is formulated, treating local governments as monopolist land suppliers subject to the central government's auction and minimum price constraints. The model posits that local governments are influenced by the comparative advantages. Moreover, variations in the relationship between the transaction price and the minimum price can alter the extent to which local governments respond to their comparative advantages, contingent on factors such as revenue from land sales, positive externalities from firms, and the central government's directives. Comprehensive parcel-, county-, and industry-level data are utilized to evaluate the model's predictions. The empirical results indicate that a one percentage point rise in an industry's employment share corresponds to a 1.3% - 1.6% upsurge in the land allocated to that industry, all else being equal. Notably, local governments exhibit heightened responsiveness to comparative advantages when the transaction price falls below the minimum price threshold. These findings substantiate the theoretical model's implications. Furthermore, the impacts are discernibly divergent across counties; local governments in more developed counties exhibit greater receptivity to comparative advantages.

title: Why Do Women Get Less in Job Market?  --Evidence on the Gender Pay Gap from Twitter Data
abstract: The gender pay gap is one of the serious issues in the labor market today and closing the gap is the major theme in the Equal Pay movement. This gap cannot be simply explained by any common labor market characteristics that form the basis of wage determination. In this thesis, I intend to understand the connection between wage gap and factors including workplace discrimination, education, occupational segregation, and, to uncover the challenges of the Equal Pay movement by identifying the main issues arise around the movement. Specifically, I performed a Twitter-based user generated content analysis (UGC) to extract twitter data with the hashtag #GenderPay and #EqualPay to further understand the issues around the equal pay movement. In the UGC analysis, I built a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model to identify top 20 topics in the dataset. I classified the tweets into three categories - positive, negative, and neutral - and plotted two word-clouds. My findings underscore the following two topics: (i) motherhood penalty (ii) racial inequality. The identified topics can be a starting point for future research on pay equity in the work environment as well as provide insights to close the gender inequality problem around the world.
Author: He, Jielu
abstract_manipulated: The phenomenon known as the gender pay gap persists as a significant concern within the contemporary labor market and efforts to mitigate this disparity stand as a central tenet of the Equal Pay advocacy. This disparity eludes straightforward explication through conventional labor market determinants. This research endeavors to elucidate the intricate interplay between wage differentials and variables encompassing workplace bias, educational attainment, occupational partitioning, and the manifold complexities that impede progress within the Equal Pay campaign. A methodological approach involving a Twitter-based examination of user-generated content (UGC) was undertaken to scrutinize discourse associated with the hashtags #GenderPay and #EqualPay. Through the employment of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) modeling, the investigation sought to unveil the foremost themes within the dataset. Categorization of tweets into positive, negative, and neutral domains was executed, leading to the construction of visual representations in the form of word-clouds. The research findings underscore the salience of two prevailing themes: the penalization of motherhood and the perpetuation of racial disparities. These identified themes serve as a foundational platform for prospective inquiries into pay parity within organizational settings and offer valuable insights aimed at addressing gender inequities on a global scale.

title: Digital Fandom in Metrics:  How Does Capitalized Emotion in  Algorithmic Culture Raise Idol Trainees Up?
abstract: This paper looked at digital fandom under an algorithmic culture. We investigated the fan structure and their affective labor based on an idol selection contest that offers promising opportunities with our special focus on its underlying metric-competing mechanism. We also used computational simulation to verify if fans’ effort on improving visible rankings on social media secures a position for their idols. By addressing these issues, we analyzed why fans are willing to trade their labor and how the capitalism logic empowers and exploits the fan community for their emotion and labor.
Author: Yijia Lyu
abstract_manipulated: This research delved into the phenomenon of digital fandom within the context of an algorithmic culture. The fan structure and their engagement in affective labor were examined through the prism of an idol selection contest that presents alluring prospects, with a particular emphasis on the underlying metric-competitive mechanism. A computational simulation was employed to ascertain whether the efforts of fans to enhance their idols' visibility on social media platforms result in a bolstered position for said idols. By engaging in this inquiry, an analysis was conducted on the motivations driving fans to exchange their labor, and the ways in which the logic of capitalism both empowers and exploits the fan community by capitalizing on their emotional investments and labor contributions.

title: The Relationship between Economic Freedom and Income Equality
abstract: This paper empirically examines the relationship between economic freedom and income inequality by using a machine-learning constructed economic freedom indicator that addresses three major methodological problems in the currently popular economic freedom indices. This paper selects the optimal Support Vector Regression (SVR) model with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel to construct the new economic freedom indicator, after comparing four different kernel types. The empirical results show that there exists a robust invert-U shaped relationship between economic freedom and income inequality.
Author: Zhou, Anqi
abstract_manipulated: This study empirically investigates the nexus between economic freedom and income inequality through the utilization of a novel economic freedom indicator generated via machine learning, which aims to rectify three prevalent methodological dilemmas present in conventional economic freedom indices. The most suitable Support Vector Regression (SVR) model with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel is selected for the development of the innovative economic freedom metric following a comprehensive comparison of four distinct kernel variants. The findings derived from the empirical analysis indicate the presence of a robust inverted-U pattern in the association between economic freedom and income inequality.

title: The Twitter Network of Think Tank Experts in D.C.
abstract: The influence of a political idea rarely relies only on a few scholars and politicians. Instead, it emerges, evolves, and transforms into feasible policies when policy experts of various backgrounds and reputations cooperate and communicate through organizations and their personal networks. Previous research on the roles of policy experts in the policy making process, however, focus primarily on celebrities whose personal connections with other political actors are more publicly visible and politically influential. Two narratives are thus missing: First, what are the roles of the majority of lesser-known experts? Second, how much are experts’ cooperation and communication shaped by organizations such as think tanks? To provide a more complete picture, I construct a Twitter follow graph which covers 609 experts with valid Twitter accounts, which is about 70% of all listed experts on the websites of the Brookings Institution, the American Enterprise Institute, the Center for American Progress, and the Heritage Foundation. Then, I analyze the structure of this Twitter follow graph and explore the implication and limits of Twitter-based connections. With a DeepWalk Algorithm, I argue that the Twitter connections among experts are highly reciprocal and organizationally based. Finally, with edge-based regressions, I show that the organizational based connection between experts is significant even when the similarity of experts’ policy interest is controlled. That is, social media usage seems ineffective in exposing experts to opinions from those beyond the reach defined by their offline constraints. In all, I construct a framework to study the expert network through Twitter data and provide a complementary narrative to the existing qualitative discussions on how opinions and information may circulate among policy expert networks.
Author: Liang, Chen
abstract_manipulated: The impact of a political concept seldom hinges solely on a select few scholars and politicians. Instead, it emerges, evolves, and morphs into viable policies through the collaborative efforts and communication of policy specialists from diverse backgrounds and reputations via organizational structures and personal networks. Prior examinations of the roles played by policy specialists in the policymaking process, however, tend to predominantly center on renowned figures whose interpersonal ties with other political actors are more prominently visible and wield greater political sway. Consequently, two narratives remain unexplored: firstly, what roles do the majority of less prominent experts undertake? Secondly, to what extent are the collaborative and communicative endeavors of experts influenced by organizations like think tanks? To present a more comprehensive view, a Twitter follow graph encompassing 609 experts with authenticated Twitter accounts, representing approximately 70% of all enumerated experts on the websites of the Brookings Institution, the American Enterprise Institute, the Center for American Progress, and the Heritage Foundation, is constructed. Subsequently, an analysis is conducted on the configuration of this Twitter follow graph, delving into the ramifications and constraints associated with connections established on Twitter. Leveraging the DeepWalk Algorithm, it is posited that the Twitter connections among experts exhibit a high degree of reciprocity and organizational grounding. Furthermore, through the application of edge-based regressions, it is demonstrated that the organizationally rooted connections between experts carry significance even when adjusting for the alignment of experts’ policy interests. Thus, the utilization of social media appears to be ineffective in broadening experts’ exposure to perspectives beyond the confines delineated by their offline limitations. In sum, a framework is developed for scrutinizing the expert network using Twitter data, offering an additional perspective to the extant qualitative dialogues on the circulation of opinions and information within policy expert networks.

title: Promoting nationalism or becoming apolitical? Understanding China’s response to the crisis on Weibo
abstract: Online social media has become one of the most important ways for authoritarian regimes to maintain their resilience. When authoritarian regimes are faced with challenges, what options would they take to defend their regime? This paper takes Hong Kong protests and early-stage COVID-19 as two examples to study how the Chinese official Weibo accounts responded to these crises. This paper leverages text analysis to mine, explore, and learn from Weibo posts related to these crises. This paper seeks to answer how do these Weibo accounts change during and after the crises, including their word frequency, topic, keywords, and sentiment scores.
Author: Li, Yimin
abstract_manipulated: The utilization of online social media platforms has emerged as a pivotal mechanism employed by authoritarian regimes in fortifying their resilience. In times of adversity, authoritarian regimes are confronted with the imperative of devising strategies to safeguard their regime. This study utilizes the Hong Kong protests and the nascent stages of the COVID-19 pandemic as illustrative instances to scrutinize the responses of Chinese official Weibo accounts to these crises. Employing text analysis as the methodological framework, this research delves into the mining, exploration, and extraction of insights from Weibo posts pertaining to these critical junctures. The primary objective of this investigation is to elucidate the shifts observed in these Weibo accounts throughout and subsequent to the crises, encompassing alterations in word frequency, thematic orientations, key terms, and sentiment patterns.

title: SHAP-Guided Adversarial Training for Robust Interpretability in Asset Pricing
abstract: During the post-COVID volatility regime, many traditional and machine-learning asset pricing models failed to provide stable explanations of return drivers. Shifts in factor attributions undermined confidence in reported risk exposures and led to costly portfolio adjustments, such as the misclassification of quality factors during the 2020 market shock. While predictive accuracy is often the primary benchmark, our objective is to improve the stability of model explanations over time so they remain both economically relevant and reliable for decision-making. To our knowledge, this is the first framework to embed attribution metrics directly into adversarial training for non-differentiable financial models. We develop a SHAP-guided adversarial training approach that constructs perturbations along SHAP-derived directions, simulating adversarial stress without requiring gradient access. This design directly regularizes attribution-relevant input sensitivity during training. Perturbation strength is calibrated through an epsilon-sweep, revealing a non-monotonic trade-off between interpretability and performance: while epsilon = 0.05 maximizes attributional stability, a more moderate epsilon = 0.005 balances robustness with economic signal retention. We evaluate the framework using monthly U.S. stock data from CRSP spanning 1990–2024, partitioned into training (1990–2010), validation (2011–2019), and test (2020–2024) periods. Model performance is assessed along three dimensions: predictive accuracy (MSE, IC), economic relevance (Q10–Q1 portfolio spreads), and attributional robustness (SHAP variance, rank correlation, and directional mean drift). A naive random-sign adversarial baseline is included for comparison. Empirically, SHAP-guided models deliver substantial gains in attributional coherence: SHAP variance falls by over 90%, and feature rank correlation across regimes improves from 0.20 to 0.90. These improvements transform highly unstable explanations into consistent, regime-robust factor profiles. The explanatory focus shifts from fragile, regime-dependent signals like momentum toward persistent, risk-consistent features such as volatility and max down, aligning with investor behavior during crises. These results demonstrate that attributional stability can be explicitly optimized without sacrificing predictive performance, and that it carries clear economic value. In volatile markets, maintaining consistent explanations is as critical as predictive fit for ensuring reliable portfolio allocation, credible economic interpretation, and effective model governance. By design, the SHAP-guided adversarial training framework is model-agnostic and readily transferable to other asset classes and predictive tasks where interpretability is essential, enabling deployment in real-world investment and risk management workflows without imposing explicit economic priors.
Author: Zhang, Sitong
abstract_manipulated: During the period of post-COVID market volatility, numerous conventional and machine-learning models used for asset pricing encountered challenges in establishing consistent rationales for returns. Changes in factor allocations eroded confidence in documented risk exposures, necessitating costly adjustments to portfolios, such as the misclassification of quality factors during the market upheaval of 2020. While prognostic precision typically serves as the primary yardstick, our aim is to enhance the enduring coherence of model elucidations so that they remain both economically pertinent and trustworthy for decision-making. To the best of our knowledge, this framework represents the inaugural attempt to integrate attribution metrics directly into adversarial training for non-differentiable financial models. We have devised a method of adversarial training guided by SHAP which generates disturbances along SHAP-derived vectors, emulating adversarial stress sans the need for gradient access. This structural approach enforces direct regularization of input sensitivity germane to attribution during the training phase. The intensity of perturbations is fine-tuned through an epsilon-sweep, revealing a non-linear trade-off between interpretability and performance: while epsilon = 0.05 maximizes the stability of attribution, a more moderate epsilon = 0.005 strikes a balance between resilience and retention of economic signals. The efficacy of the framework is evaluated using monthly U.S. stock data from CRSP spanning the years 1990 to 2024, segregated into training (1990–2010), validation (2011–2019), and test (2020–2024) intervals. Model efficacy is gauged along three dimensions: predictive precision (MSE, IC), economic significance (Q10–Q1 portfolio spreads), and robustness in attribution (SHAP variance, rank correlation, and directional mean drift). For comparative purposes, a basic random-sign adversarial baseline is included. Empirically, models guided by SHAP produce significant enhancements in attributional consistency: SHAP variance plummets by more than 90%, and feature rank correlation across different market conditions escalates from 0.20 to 0.90. These enhancements convert highly erratic elucidations into uniform factor profiles resilient to market fluctuations. The focal point of explanations transitions from fragile, market-specific signals like momentum to enduring features aligned with risk, such as volatility and maximum drawdown, which mirror investor behavior during periods of crisis. These findings illustrate that the optimization of attributional stability can be explicitly pursued without compromising prognostic efficacy, and that it carries tangible economic benefits. In volatile market environments, maintaining coherent explanations is as pivotal as achieving predictive accuracy in ensuring dependable portfolio allocation, credible economic interpretation, and effective model governance. By design, the SHAP-guided adversarial training framework is agnostic to specific models and can be effortlessly applied to other asset classes and predictive tasks where interpretability is paramount, facilitating integration into real-world investment and risk management processes without imposing explicit economic assumptions.

title: Values in Crisis: How the Great Recession Reshaped the government’s personality Evidence from a Difference-in-Differences Framework Applied to Political Speech Data
abstract: The 2008 Global Financial Crisis significantly disrupted economies across the world, presenting a unique opportunity to examine how economic shocks influence the cultural values expressed by governments. This thesis investigates whether and how the crisis reshaped governmental value expression by analyzing political speeches from four European countries—Spain and Italy (treatment group) and Germany and the Netherlands (control group)—from 2006 to 2010. Leveraging Schwartz’s theory of basic human values and a novel OpenAI GPT-4o-based pipeline for computational value extraction, I quantify the relative emphasis placed on ten value dimensions within each speech. Employing a Difference-in-Differences (DiD) framework, the study isolates the causal impact of the crisis on governmental value orientation. Findings reveal that, contrary to theories predicting authoritarian or dominance-oriented reactions, crisis-affected governments significantly increased their emphasis on benevolence-oriented values. These results are robust across multiple specifications, placebo tests, and sensitivity analyses, including data sampling adjustments. This study contributes methodologically to value quantification using large language models, and theoretically to our understanding of how elite political discourse adapts toeconomic upheaval by signaling solidarity and moral responsiveness.
Author: Zhang, Hongkun
abstract_manipulated: The profound disruption caused by the 2008 Global Financial Crisis on global economies provided a distinct opportunity to explore the impact of economic shocks on the manifestation of cultural values through governmental channels. This thesis endeavors to ascertain the potential reshaping of governmental value expression in the aftermath of the crisis by scrutinizing political discourses in four European nations—Spain and Italy (treatment group) and Germany and the Netherlands (control group)—across the period spanning from 2006 to 2010. Drawing upon Schwartz’s theory of basic human values and utilizing an innovative computational value extraction pipeline based on OpenAI GPT-4o, the emphasis placed on ten value dimensions within each speech is quantified. Through the employment of a robust Difference-in-Differences (DiD) framework, the causal impact of the crisis on governmental value orientation is isolated. The results indicate a marked shift towards benevolence-oriented values by crisis-affected governments, contradicting anticipations of authoritarian or dominance-centric responses. These findings exhibit resilience across various specifications, placebo tests, and sensitivity analyses, encompassing adjustments in data sampling. This research not only enhances the methodological landscape of value quantification utilizing expansive language models but also contributes theoretically to the comprehension of how elite political discourse adapts to economic turmoil by signaling solidarity and moral responsiveness.

title: Strategic Patent Acquisitions: The Role of Technological Proximity and Patent Portfolio Size
abstract: I develop a theoretical model in which firms make strategic patent acquisition decisions based on technological proximity and patent portfolio size. Firms derive higher value from patents that closely align with their existing technology, while the marginal benefit of additional patents diminishes as portfolios grow. Using a two-step estimation approach, I analyze patent transaction data and find that technological proximity positively affects patent value, whereas patent portfolio size exhibits diminishing returns. The negative effect of portfolio size is more pronounced for large firms and manufacturing industries. The results suggest that firms should strategically balance technological alignment and portfolio expansion.
Author: Li, Guankun
abstract_manipulated: A theoretical model is formulated wherein strategic decisions regarding patent acquisition are made by firms based on considerations of technological proximity and the scale of their patent portfolios. Patents that closely align with a firm's existing technology are posited to yield greater value, with the incremental benefits of additional patents tapering off as portfolios expand. Through the utilization of a two-step estimation methodology, patent transaction data is scrutinized, revealing a positive impact of technological proximity on patent value, juxtaposed with diminishing returns associated with larger patent portfolios. The attenuation in patent value due to portfolio size is particularly accentuated in the context of large firms and within the manufacturing sector. The findings tentatively imply that firms ought to meticulously calibrate their strategic approaches to strike a delicate equilibrium between technological congruence and portfolio augmentation.

title: Capital controls, Sterilization and Optimal Chinese policies
abstract: This research investigates Ramsey optimal monetary and fiscal policies in China under capital controls, nominal exchange rate targets, and costly sterilization, employing a New Keynesian DSGE model. Policymakers act as benevolent planners to maximize household welfare. Assuming an untraditional indistinct fiscal and monetary policy regime, it examines policy responses to external shocks and potential reforms over flexible exchange rate regime, offering rigorous insights into welfare trade-offs in a constrained economy.
Author: Ding, Xuzhou
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the optimization of monetary and fiscal strategies in China within the framework of capital controls, nominal exchange rate objectives, and resource-intensive sterilization processes, utilizing a New Keynesian Dynamic Stochastic General Equilibrium (DSGE) model. Decisions are made by policymakers assuming the role of altruistic strategists aiming to enhance household well-being. By postulating an unconventional and somewhat ambiguous regime for fiscal and monetary policies, the study scrutinizes reactions to external disturbances and potential modifications under a system featuring a pliable exchange rate mechanism, thereby providing robust insights into the intricate welfare trade-offs within a constricted economic setting.

title: Differential impact from individual versus collective misinformation tagging on the diversity of Twitter (X) information engagement and mobility
abstract: Fears about the destabilizing impact of misinformation online have motivated individuals and platforms to respond. Individuals have increasingly challenged others’ online claims with fact-checks in pursuit of a healthier information ecosystem and to break down echo chambers of self-reinforcing opinion. Using Twitter (now X) data, here we show the consequences of individual misinformation tagging: tagged posters had explored novel political information and expanded topical interests immediately prior, but being tagged caused posters to retreat into information bubbles. These unintended consequences were softened by a collective verification system for misinformation moderation. In Twitter’s new feature, Community Notes, misinformation tagging was peer-reviewed by other fact-checkers before revelation to the poster. With collective misinformation tagging, posters were less likely to retreat from diverse information engagement. Detailed comparison demonstrated differences in toxicity, sentiment, readability, and delay in individual versus collective misinformation tagging messages. These findings provide evidence for differential impacts from individual versus collective moderation strategies on the diversity of information engagement and mobility across the information ecosystem.
Author: Kim, Junsol ; Wang, Zhao ; Shi, Haohan ; Ling, Hsin-Keng ; Evans, James
abstract_manipulated: Fears surrounding the potential destabilizing effects of misinformation disseminated online have incited a response from both individuals and platforms. An increasing number of individuals have taken it upon themselves to challenge the veracity of online claims through the process of fact-checking, with the aim of fostering a more robust information ecosystem and dismantling echo chambers characterized by the reinforcement of like-minded opinions. Utilizing data from the platform Twitter (now X), our study delves into the repercussions of individual misinformation tagging. Our analysis reveals that individuals who were tagged for spreading misinformation had, in the period immediately preceding the tagging, delved into previously unexplored political information and broadened their scope of topical interests. However, upon being tagged, these individuals tended to retreat into insular information bubbles. It was observed that such unintended consequences were mitigated to some extent by the implementation of a collective verification mechanism for misinformation moderation. Notably, in Twitter's innovative feature known as Community Notes, the process of misinformation tagging underwent peer review by other fact-checkers before being brought to the attention of the offending poster. Through the adoption of collective misinformation tagging, posters exhibited a decreased inclination to disengage from diverse information sources. A detailed comparative analysis highlighted distinctions in the levels of toxicity, sentiment, readability, and temporal delay between messages pertaining to individual versus collective misinformation tagging. The findings of this study offer valuable insights into the disparate effects of individual versus collective moderation strategies on the promotion of information diversity and fluidity within the broader information ecosystem.

title: Computational Analysis of the United Nations General Assembly Resolutions: Prevailing Issue Areas and International Alliance Formation
abstract: Earlier attempts at understanding international alignments and alliance structures are often rigid in the sense that they prioritize a specific policy domain or time frame in international politics to present these alignments in easy to comprehend ways. Borrowing from the methodologies of Natural Language Processing, unsupervised machine learning, and social network analysis, I choose the United Nations General Assembly resolutions and voting data as a proxy for diplomatic alignment and show that different issue areas in international politics will yield alliance structures that are often in disagreement with the most widely used frameworks but align with our understanding of the dynamism of modern international politics. Computational approaches can provide a more precise and time-sensitive framework to think about countries’ alliance strategies.
Author: Pamukcu, Egemen
abstract_manipulated: Prior attempts at elucidating international alignments and alliance structures are frequently characterized by a certain inflexibility, as they tend to prioritize a particular policy domain or temporal framework within the realm of international politics to portray these alignments in easily digestible manners. Drawing upon the methodological paradigms of Natural Language Processing, unsupervised machine learning, and social network analysis, the utilization of United Nations General Assembly resolutions and voting data as a surrogate for diplomatic alignment is undertaken in this investigation. The empirical findings indicate that diverse issue areas in international politics yield alliance structures that often diverge from the prevailing frameworks but are congruent with our comprehension of the fluidity inherent in contemporary international relations. Through computational methodologies, a more precise and temporally sensitive analytical framework emerges for the exploration of countries' alliance strategies.

title: Deep Learning for Dynamic NFT Valuation
abstract: I study the price dynamics of non-fungible tokens (NFTs) and propose a deep learning framework for dynamic valuation of NFTs. I use data from the Ethereum blockchain and OpenSea to train a deep learning model on historical trades, market trends, and traits/rarity features of Bored Ape Yacht Club NFTs. After hyperparameter tuning, the model is able to predict the price of NFTs with high accuracy. I propose an application framework for this model using zero-knowledge machine learning (zkML) and discuss its potential use cases in the context of decentralized finance (DeFi) applications.
Author: He, Mingxuan
abstract_manipulated: The investigation delves into the intricate dynamics of non-fungible tokens (NFTs) and introduces a sophisticated deep learning framework for the dynamic evaluation of NFTs. Utilizing data sourced from the Ethereum blockchain and OpenSea, a deep learning model is meticulously trained on a comprehensive dataset comprising historical trades, prevailing market trends, and distinctive traits/rarity attributes of Bored Ape Yacht Club NFTs. Following meticulous hyperparameter optimization, the model demonstrates a notable capacity for precise prognostication of NFT prices. A conceptual framework is put forth for the operationalization of this model, leveraging zero-knowledge machine learning (zkML), with a theoretical exploration of its potential applications within the realm of decentralized finance (DeFi) systems being delineated.

title: The Relationship Between Religious Centers and Urban Development: Evidence from Ghana
abstract: A large number of churches and mosques have been recently established in Ghana. Local policymakers and foreign philanthropists politically and financially support establishing these houses of worship due to their assumption regarding a causal relationship between religious centers and neighborhood development. The current study aims to test whether the assumption regarding a causal link holds by extracting and analyzing features from satellite images and conducting a spatial clustering analysis. The results indicate that religious centers are more likely to be constructed in fast-growing neighborhoods with certain characteristics. However, there are no signs of them changing temporal trends in urban development.
