Religious centers are more likely to be established in middle-class neighborhoods with high population density and above-average rates of illiteracy and unemployment. In addition to testing the mentioned assumption, the results shed some light on the influence of demand-and-supply factors versus socioeconomic and accessibility variables on the spatial distribution of religious centers.
Author: Eshtiyagh, Javad
abstract_manipulated: A plethora of churches and mosques have recently been established in the Ghanaian context. Local policymakers and external philanthropic entities are actively involved in providing political and financial backing for the establishment of these religious edifices, under the guise of a hypothesized causal nexus between religious congregations and local area advancement. The present investigation endeavors to scrutinize the validity of this purported causal linkage by extracting and scrutinizing salient attributes from satellite imagery, coupled with the implementation of a spatial clustering analysis. The outcomes suggest a predilection for the construction of religious establishments within swiftly burgeoning neighborhoods exhibiting specific attributes. Nonetheless, there is no discernible indication of these installations influencing temporal trends in the urban developmental landscape. Notably, religious centers are inclined to materialize within socioeconomically middling neighborhoods characterized by heightened population density, coupled with elevated levels of illiteracy and unemployment. In addition to interrogating the aforementioned hypothesis, the findings offer some insight into the interplay between demand-supply dynamics and socioeconomic and accessibility parameters in shaping the spatial dispersion of religious centers.

title: Differential Impacts of Water Privatization in Sub-Saharan Africa
abstract: Countries in Africa have historically struggled with access to safe water. Over the past three decades, the World Bank has promoted the privatization of water systems through contracts with foreign companies as the solution to the water crisis. However, only a handful of privatization efforts in Africa have succeeded, with most governments taking back control of their water supplies after only a few years. Furthermore, recent evidence suggests that expanded water access resulting from privatization only occurs in wealthier communities, however this has not yet been confirmed. This thesis examines the relationship between functional water access points in Tanzania, Ghana, Mozambique, and Nigeria and the income levels of the neighborhoods in which they are located, analyzing how this relationship changes before and after privatization. This quantitative approach was accompanied by a qualitative analysis of the political, economic, and geographical features of each country. When combined, these two complementary avenues of analysis demonstrate that privatization did not significantly increase water access but there was insufficient evidence to make definitive conclusions about privatization’s effect on inequalities. This thesis argues that certain models of privatization should not be promoted as primary solutions to water access because they do not appear to increase water access. Furthermore, it demonstrates the inefficacy of a ‘one-size-fits-all’ approach to expanding water access and indicates the need for solutions that are adapted to local situations.
Author: Varma, Asha
abstract_manipulated: African nations have historically encountered challenges in accessing potable water. Over the preceding three decades, the World Bank has advocated for the privatization of water systems via contractual arrangements with foreign entities as a panacea for the water scarcity predicament. Nevertheless, only a select few privatization endeavors in Africa have achieved success, with most governmental bodies reclaiming authority over their water resources within a brief span of time. Moreover, recent empirical data indicates that the broadening of water availability resultant from privatization potentially manifests solely in affluent communities, although this assertion remains unverified. This scholarly endeavor delves into scrutinizing the nexus between operational water dispensing outlets in Tanzania, Ghana, Mozambique, and Nigeria and the socioeconomic strata of the vicinities in which they are sited, dissecting the alterations in this association pre- and post-privatization. Employing a quantitative methodology supplemented by a qualitative scrutiny of the political, economic, and geographic nuances of each nation, this study underscores that privatization did not yield a substantial amplification in water accessibility, notwithstanding the absence of conclusive evidence to affirm the impact of privatization on disparities. This thesis posits that specific privatization models should not be endorsed as principal remedies for water accessibility issues due to their apparent inefficacy in augmenting water availability. Furthermore, it underscores the inadequacies of a uniform approach in enhancing water access and advocates for context-specific solutions tailored to individual circumstances.

title: Empathy and redemption: Exploring the narrative transformation of online support for mental health across communities before and after Covid-19
abstract: This study examines the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on individuals’ mental health and their online interactions, particularly within Reddit’s mental health communities. By analyzing data from 15 subreddits categorized into mental health and control groups from 2018 to 2022, we observed that forums dedicated to mental health exhibited higher levels of user engagement and received more supportive responses than those in other categories. However, as the pandemic evolved, a significant decrease in online support was noted, especially within these mental health groups. This decline hints at a risk of emotional burnout among users, which poses a particularly acute challenge for individuals grappling with mental health issues. Intimate relationships have also an impact on online expression of mental health. The research underscores the pandemic’s effect on online support and interaction dynamics, signaling the necessity for a deeper understanding and the development of strategies to maintain support within online communities during times of crisis.
Author: Cai, Yuxuan ; Wei, Ertong ; Cai, Xintong
abstract_manipulated: This investigation examines the impact of the unprecedented global health crisis caused by the COVID-19 pandemic on the mental well-being of individuals and their virtual interactions, particularly within the specialized mental health communities on Reddit. Utilizing a dataset spanning from 2018 to 2022 encompassing 15 subreddits segmented into distinct mental health and control cohorts, it was observed that the forums dedicated to mental health manifested escalated levels of user engagement and elicited a greater influx of compassionate responses compared to the non-mental health categories. Nonetheless, as the pandemic progressed, a notable diminution in online support was discerned, particularly within these mental health enclaves. This decline alludes to a potential susceptibility to emotional exhaustion among participants, thereby posing a notably pressing challenge for individuals contending with mental health adversities. Furthermore, the influence of intimate relationships on the virtual articulation of mental health experiences was also explored. The investigation highlights the pandemic's transformative impact on the digital support landscape and interaction dynamics, thereby signaling the imperative need for a more profound comprehension and the formulation of strategies aimed at preserving support networks within virtual communities amidst times of crisis.

title: Gender Representation of Psychosocial Factors in Role-Playing Video Game Dialogue
abstract: This study investigates gender differences in the linguistic construction of character dialogue within role-playing video games, focusing on psychosocial factors. A corpus of game dialogue was analyzed using both a lexicon-based dictionary and a transformer-based emotion classification model. Basic linguistic analysis revealed that player characters spoke significantly more lines, sentences, and words than non-player characters but used simpler sentence structures. However, no significant gender differences were observed in these linguistic metrics. Word frequency analysis showed that female characters used significantly more language associated with cognitive reflection and social connection, whereas male characters exhibited higher rates of swear word usage. Emotion classification revealed that female non-player characters expressed a broader range of emotions, including more disgust, sadness, surprise, joy, and fear, while male characters showed a stronger association with anger. Principal component analysis identified an affective positivity–negativity axis that significantly predicted gender, with male characters clustering toward greater negativity. Analysis of neutral characters indicated that their psychosocial profiles most closely resembled male characters, supporting the notion of masculine defaults. These findings highlight how gendered psychosocial patterns are embedded in video game dialogue and reveal both persistent stereotypes and emerging shifts toward more balanced gender representation in interactive narratives.
Author: Yan, Weiwu
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into gender disparities in the linguistic delineation of character discourse within immersive role-playing digital environments, with a particular emphasis on psychosocial variables. A corpus of dialogic exchanges in gaming contexts underwent scrutiny through the utilization of a lexicon-based dictionary and an emotion classification model based on transformers. Preliminary linguistic scrutiny unveiled that protagonist avatars were responsible for a significantly greater number of utterances, sentences, and lexical units compared to non-playable characters, albeit employing less intricate syntactic configurations. Nevertheless, no statistically significant disparities based on gender were detected across these linguistic parameters. An analysis of word frequencies showcased that female characters exhibited a notably higher utilization of lexicon associated with cognitive introspection and interpersonal bonds, while male characters displayed a heightened propensity for the deployment of expletives. Emotion classification disclosed that female non-playable characters manifested a wider spectrum of affective states, encompassing augmented levels of revulsion, melancholy, astonishment, elation, and trepidation, while male characters showcased a pronounced inclination towards ire. Principal component analysis revealed the presence of an affective continuum ranging from positivity to negativity, which exhibited a marked predictive capacity for gender, with male characters gravitating towards a more negative emotional realm. Examination of neutral characters suggested that their psychosocial profiles bore a closer resemblance to those of male characters, thus buttressing the concept of masculine archetypes. These empirical revelations underscore the entrenchment of gendered psychosocial configurations within the fabric of video game dialogues, unveiling both enduring stereotypes and nascent trends towards a more equitable gender portrayal within interactive narrative landscapes.

title: A System-Agnostic Approach to Computational Ideology Detection: An analysis of Colombian congressional Speech (2000-2024)
abstract: This study proposes a system-agnostic, modular approach for detecting ideological structures in political discourse without relying on predefined ideological axes or external labels. Building on a sociological understanding of ideology as the rationalization and narrative construction of political action, the method combines topic modeling, narrative mining, and frequent itemset analysis to inductively reconstruct ideological patterns from large-scale political speech. Applying this pipeline to a corpus of Colombian congressional interventions (2000–2024) revealed three major attitudinal stances toward peace processes, alongside narrative bridges linking economic governance, institutional trust, and post-conflict reform. These findings demonstrate that ideological structures can be systematically detected through emergent speech patterns, offering a flexible and culturally sensitive alternative to traditional ideology detection models. The results highlight new possibilities for computational social science in fragmented political contexts, particularly in regions like Latin America, where labeled data is limited and ideological systems are fluid.
Author: Sarria-Morales, Alejandro
abstract_manipulated: The proposition of a system-agnostic, modular approach is set forth in this investigation to detect ideological structures in political discourse without reliance on predetermined ideological axes or external labels. Drawing upon a sociological comprehension of ideology as the rationalization and narrative construction of political action, the methodology integrates topic modeling, narrative mining, and frequent itemset analysis to inductively reconstruct ideological patterns from extensive political speech data. The application of this analytical framework to a collection of Colombian congressional interventions (2000–2024) resulted in the identification of three prominent attitudinal stances toward peace processes, as well as the discovery of narrative connections that traverse economic governance, institutional trust, and post-conflict reform domains. These findings serve to illustrate that ideological structures can be methodically discerned through emergent speech patterns, presenting a pliable and culturally attuned alternative to conventional ideology detection models. The outcomes underscore novel avenues for computational social science in fragmented political landscapes, particularly within regions such as Latin America characterized by limited labeled data and fluid ideological frameworks.

title: Neither Biased Algorithms nor Biased Humans are Desirable, But Combining Them is Permissible
abstract: Given the many notions of fairness, virtually any algorithm or human can be biased or unfair by some standard. Little is known about people’s preferences for potentially biased humans consulting potentially biased Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools. In two studies (N = 1,610), we found that (a) people prefer biased humans to consult unbiased AI, (b) people prefer unbiased humans to avoid consulting biased AI, and (c) people lean towards wanting biased humans to consult biased AI. Although people prefer not to utilize algorithmic aids when the human decision-maker is unbiased, people do not always react negatively to algorithms. Algorithms can be a more permissible decision aid for potentially biased humans. This work suggests that in real-world conditions where any human or algorithm can be biased by some standard, people may not object to potentially biased experts consulting with potentially biased AI.
Author: Wang, Sophie Jiarui
abstract_manipulated: In light of the myriad conceptions of equity, any algorithm or individual can conceivably exhibit bias or inequity according to certain criteria. The preferences of individuals regarding the prospect of potentially biased individuals collaborating with potentially biased Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools remain largely unexplored. In the context of two distinct investigations (N = 1,610), it was ascertained that (a) individuals tend to favor the engagement of biased individuals in consultation with unbiased AI systems, (b) individuals exhibit a preference for unbiased individuals steering clear of interactions with biased AI, and (c) there is a prevailing inclination towards the involvement of biased individuals in consultations with biased AI. Despite the general reluctance to rely on algorithmic assistance in scenarios where the human decision-maker exhibits impartiality, reactions to algorithms are not consistently unfavorable. Algorithms may serve as a more permissible aid in decision-making processes for potentially biased human agents. The implications of this research indicate that within real-world settings where any human or algorithm is susceptible to bias according to certain criteria, individuals may not raise objections to potentially biased experts engaging with potentially biased AI systems.

title: How can we use ChatGPT better: A research  of API-Enhanced ChatGPT in stock prediction
abstract: In this study, we propose an API-enhanced ChatGPT structure that incorporates stock  price and news data to improve stock price movement predictions. By integrating external  data sources and prompt engineering techniques, our approach demonstrates a significant  improvement in predictive performance compared to using only stock price data. The  inclusion of news data alongside stock prices results in an approximately 10% increase in  accuracy and F1 scores, as well as a 20% improvement in risk-adjusted returns, as measured  by Sharpe ratios and information ratios. Our findings highlight the potential of leveraging  conversational AI and large language models for stock market analysis, while also identifying  areas for further research and optimization, such as addressing stock-specific challenges and  developing cost-effective strategies for implementation. This study contributes to the limited  body of literature on the application of large language models in finance and paves the way  for future research in enhancing the capabilities of AI-driven investment decision-making  tools
Author: Zheng, Qichang
abstract_manipulated: Within this investigation, an API-enhanced ChatGPT framework is posited, wherein stock price and news data are integrated to enhance prognostications of stock price movements. Through the amalgamation of external data sources and techniques for prompt engineering, a notable enhancement in prognosticatory efficacy is evinced in contrast to sole reliance on stock price data. The incorporation of news data in conjunction with stock prices engenders an approximate 10% augmentation in accuracy and F1 scores, alongside a 20% amelioration in risk-adjusted returns, as quantified through Sharpe ratios and information ratios. Our discoveries underscore the latent utility in harnessing conversational AI and extensive language models for the analysis of stock markets, while also delineating avenues for further exploration and refinement, including the mitigation of stock-specific quandaries and the formulation of cost-efficient strategies for deployment. This inquiry augments the scant corpus of literature on the utilization of extensive language models in finance and sets the stage for forthcoming endeavors aimed at enhancing the capabilities of AI-driven tools for investment decision-making.

title: Investigating Job Seekers' Alignment with Job Market Skill Requirements: A Study of Data Scientist Profiles
abstract: The job search process is crucial for individuals seeking employment, particularly for those without work experience, such as recent graduates. This research aims to examine the behavior of job seekers by investigating whether their profiles align with the skills required in job postings, focusing on data scientists. The study seeks to understand job search patterns and provide recommendations to job seekers on skill acquisition and job fit to improve their chances of securing employment. The research employs an N-gram model to extract job skills from job postings and user profiles, cosine similarity to calculate person-job fit scores, and collaborative filtering methods for skill recommendations. Data is collected from two groups: employed data scientists and students actively seeking data scientist positions. The results indicate that job-seeking students possess skill sets closer to the job market demands compared to employed data scientists. This suggests that recent graduates or those transitioning into new careers may be better prepared to meet job market needs than those already employed in the field. Furthermore, job postings display a more even distribution of similarity to job seekers' skill sets, implying that job seekers have a wider range of opportunities available as their skills align with various job postings. The regression analysis conducted in this study shows that the similarity coefficient was positive but not statistically significant, indicating the potential of similarity as an indicator of employment status change for both job-seekers and employed data scientists. However, the explanatory power of the similarity score appeared to be lower for those actively seeking a job, possibly due to their increased activity in the job market. This research has the potential to guide job seekers in skill development and improve their chances of finding suitable employment.
Author: Chou, Yu-Hsuan
abstract_manipulated: The process of seeking employment is of paramount importance for individuals, particularly those lacking prior work experience, such as recent graduates. The primary objective of this investigation is to analyze the job seeker behavior through the alignment of their profiles with the requisite skills delineated in job postings, with a specific focus on individuals within the domain of data science. The study endeavors to comprehend the intricate patterns of job search and proffer recommendations to job seekers regarding skill acquisition and job compatibility to potentially augment their prospects of securing gainful employment. Employing an N-gram model for the extraction of job skills from both job postings and user profiles, cosine similarity for the computation of person-job fit metrics, and collaborative filtering techniques for skill suggestions, the research is underpinned by a methodological framework that is rigorous and systematic. Data is gathered from two distinct cohorts: employed data scientists and students actively pursuing positions in the realm of data science. The findings illuminate that students embarking on job searches exhibit skill sets that closely mirror the exigencies of the job market in contrast to their employed counterparts. This observation intimates that recent graduates or individuals transitioning into novel career trajectories may possess a higher degree of preparedness to meet the exigencies of the job market compared to those already entrenched in the field. Furthermore, the analysis reveals an equitable dispersion of similarity between job postings and job seekers' skill sets, thereby indicating a broad spectrum of opportunities available as their skills align with diverse job postings. The regression analysis undertaken in the course of this inquiry establishes a positive but non-significant association in the similarity coefficient, implying the potential utility of similarity as an indicator of transitions in employment status for both job seekers and employed data scientists. Nevertheless, the explanatory capacity of the similarity score appears to be somewhat diminished for individuals actively in pursuit of employment, conceivably owing to their heightened engagement in the job market. This research endeavor harbors the promise of steering job seekers towards a trajectory of skill enhancement, thereby enhancing their likelihood of securing fitting employment opportunities.

title: How Political Bias Manifests on the Digital Front Pages of US-Based News Media
abstract: Media bias is a topic that attracts significant scholarly attention. Today, digital news media has become the primary news source for the public, but the exact manner in which political bias is reflected on the digital front pages of news media remains an under-explored topic. This study aims to fill this gap by systematically analyzing the headlines from 44 prominent U.S. news sources representing a broad political spectrum, collected bi-hourly from January through February 2024. This paper is among the first to utilize high-frequency data collection methods to examine the highly dynamic front pages of news media. Focusing on political news topics on the front pages, the findings of this paper suggest that news outlets feature political figures with opposing ideologies more prominently than those that align with the media’s political stance. Additionally, it was observed that media organizations frequently report on similar political topics simultaneously, but the tone of their coverage varies according to their political bias. These preliminary results could have broad implications for future research aimed at understanding and addressing media bias. Furthermore, this research demonstrate the effectiveness of high-frequency data collection to study digital news.
Author: Zhang, Tianze
abstract_manipulated: Media bias is a subject that garners substantial scholarly interest. In contemporary times, digital news media has emerged as the primary source of information for the general public. However, the precise manifestation of political bias on the digital front pages of news media remains a topic that has not received extensive scrutiny. This research endeavor seeks to address this gap by conducting a systematic analysis of the headlines from 44 prominent U.S. news sources that span a wide political spectrum. The data were gathered bi-hourly over the months of January through February 2024. This paper stands as one of the pioneering works to employ high-frequency data collection techniques in scrutinizing the ever-evolving front pages of news media. Concentrating on political news themes prominently displayed on these front pages, the outcomes of this investigation tentatively suggest that news outlets prioritize featuring political figures espousing contrasting ideologies over those in alignment with the media's political predisposition. Moreover, it was noted that media establishments frequently cover similar political subjects concurrently, albeit with varying tonalities dictated by their political leanings. These preliminary findings may potentially have far-reaching implications for subsequent research endeavors aimed at comprehending and mitigating media bias. Additionally, this study illustrates the efficacy of high-frequency data collection methodologies in analyzing digital news landscapes.

title: The Language of Bias: Examining the Influence of Political Bias and Electoral Context on Emotional, Moral, Intergroup Language and Sentiment in News Media
abstract: In an era of increasing political polarization, the language used by news media has become a crucial area of investigation. This study investigates the relationship between political bias and language usage in news articles, focusing on the use of emotional, moral, intergroup language, and sentiment. By analyzing a comprehensive dataset of 2,510,592 news articles from 234 publishers spanning the years 2010 to 2022, I seek to shed light on the linguistic strategies used by media outlets to influence public perception and opinion. My approach combines a novel Transformer-Dictionary Hybrid method for identifying emotional, moral, and intergroup content with sentiment analysis using the VADER tool. The study addresses two key research questions: 1) How does the usage of emotional, moral, and intergroup language differ among news publishers with different political biases? 2) Do news outlets employ this language differently during election years compared to non-election years? ANOVA and mixed linear model analyses reveal significant differences in the usage of emotional, moral, intergroup language, and sentiment across publishers with different political biases. Liberal publishers tend to use more emotional and moral language compared to left-leaning and neutral publishers. Intergroup language usage is significantly lower among neutral publishers compared to liberal ones. Sentiment analysis shows that conservative publishers express a more negative sentiment compared to liberal publishers. A two-way ANOVA examining the effect of election years shows a significant interaction between language dimensions and election cycle. Mixed linear model analyses reveal a decrease in intergroup language usage and a slight decrease in positive sentiment during election years. The findings contribute to our understanding of how political bias and electoral context influence the linguistic choices in news media. The results suggest that partisan outlets strategically employ language to engage and potentially polarize their audience, while adapting to the heightened political climate of election years. These insights shed light on the complex interplay between language, media bias, and political polarization, underlining the importance of media literacy in navigating the contemporary media landscape.
Author: Jiang, Yutong
abstract_manipulated: In an epoch characterized by escalating political polarization, the linguistic discourse employed by news media entities has emerged as a critical domain warranting meticulous scrutiny. The present investigation delves into the intricate nexus between political partiality and linguistic utilization in news discourse, with particular emphasis on the deployment of emotional, moral, intergroup lexicon, and affective expressions. Through the examination of an extensive corpus comprising 2,510,592 news articles sourced from 234 publishers spanning the temporal expanse from 2010 to 2022, an endeavor is undertaken to elucidate the strategic linguistic maneuvers adopted by media establishments to shape public perception and sway opinion. The methodological framework adopted amalgamates a pioneering Transformer-Dictionary Hybrid technique for discerning emotional, moral, and intergroup thematic content with sentiment evaluation utilizing the VADER instrument. The inquiry is guided by two principal research inquiries: 1) How do variations in the utilization of emotional, moral, and intergroup lexicon manifest among news publishers characterized by divergent political predispositions? 2) To what extent do news outlets diverge in their linguistic strategies during electoral cycles vis-à-vis non-electoral periods? The employment of ANOVA and mixed linear model analyses unveils substantive disparities in the deployment of emotional, moral, intergroup lexicon, and affective expressions across publishers characterized by distinct political leanings. Noteworthy trends include a propensity among liberal publishers to exhibit a heightened proclivity towards emotional and moral phraseology in contrast to their left-leaning and neutral counterparts. Moreover, the incidence of intergroup lexicon employment registers a marked diminution among neutral publishers vis-à-vis their liberal counterparts. The sentiment analysis conducted indicates a predilection among conservative publishers for expressing a more unfavorable sentiment relative to their liberal counterparts. A two-way ANOVA exploration scrutinizing the impact of electoral cycles unveils a significant interplay between linguistic dimensions and the electoral milieu. The outcomes of mixed linear model analyses discern a reduction in intergroup lexicon utilization and a marginal downturn in positive sentiment during electoral years. The discernments proffered herein enrich our comprehension of how political predisposition and electoral circumstances shape the linguistic selections within the news media domain. The findings proffer indications that partisan media outlets tactically harness language to captivate and potentially accentuate divisions within their audience, while adapting to the intensified political ambiance characterizing electoral periods. These revelations illuminate the intricate intertwinement between language, media bias, and political polarization, accentuating the imperative of media literacy in navigating the multifaceted contemporary media terrain.

title: Understanding What People Need from Visiting Green Spaces During the Pandemic: An Analysis of Visitation Patterns by Integrating Multimodal Data
abstract: During the pandemic, visiting counts, dwell time, and travel distance to green space significantly declined. Previous studies have not fully explained why this pattern occurred, as they lack the simultaneous inclusion of both objective features and visitor’s experience, and do not explicitly distinguish visitation patterns into three categories. To examine how U.S. green space visitation patterns changed during the COVID-19 pandemic, I integrated Google reviews, Street View images, and the Social Vulnerability Index with GPS-based visitation data to examine how experience, environmental features, and socioeconomic sta- tus affected green space visitation, and further to identify features that fulfill basic and non-basic needs in different time periods. Machine learning models were used to transform raw data into numerical metrics and capture the non-linear impact of these features on green space visitation. The findings reveal that during the pandemic, basic needs such as green coverage and road are regarded as the most important. Non-basic features, such as water activities and sports, can still attract a smaller proportion of visitors, provided they have access to these green spaces.
Author: Chen, Anzhi
abstract_manipulated: Amidst the global health crisis, a notable decrease was observed in metrics such as visitor numbers, duration of stay, and distance traveled to verdant areas. Existing literature falls short in elucidating the underlying reasons for this trend, as it lacks a comprehensive integration of objective parameters and visitor experiences, and fails to explicitly differentiate between visitation typologies. In order to scrutinize the shifts in U.S. green space visitation patterns during the COVID-19 era, a methodological approach was devised that amalgamated Google reviews, Street View imagery, and the Social Vulnerability Index with GPS-derived visitation data. This approach facilitated an exploration of how visitor experiences, environmental attributes, and socioeconomic status influenced green space visitation, in addition to discerning features that catered to primary and secondary needs across distinct temporal segments. Utilization of advanced machine learning algorithms was instrumental in processing raw data into quantifiable indices and capturing the intricate, non-linear effects of these variables on green space visitation. The outcomes unveiled a paramount emphasis on basic necessities, such as green coverage and accessibility, amidst the pandemic. Conversely, supplementary amenities like water-based activities and sports managed to attract a smaller fraction of visitors, contingent upon their availability within these verdant locales.

title: Revenge, Redemption, and Rhythm: The Language of Gendered Violence in Colombian Popular Lyrics
abstract: This study explores gendered violence, emotional expression, and performative identities in the top 200 popular songs from Colombia on Spotify between 2017 and 2021, focusing on the Spanish lyrical content of solo and collaborative artists. Hypothesis 1 reveals that male collaborations use more derogatory language than male solo artists, amplifying violence through group dynamics. Hypothesis 2 shows that anger, as an automatic tool to assert dominance among Colombian male artists, aligns with Raewyn Connell’s concept of hegemonic masculinity. Hypothesis 3 reflects Judith Butler’s theory of gender performativity, demonstrating how women embody societal scripts of nurturing and compliance shaped by the male gaze. These findings support Veena Das’s notion of language as a tool of everyday violence.The results emphasize the role of popular music and culture in perpetuating gendered narratives and societal norms in Colombia. Future research should investigate emotional expression in Spanish-language lyrics, assess the severity of derogatory language, and examine the cultural construction of female desire. This work advances the understanding of how gender, power, and culture intersect in musical expression.
Author: Chowdhury, Tanzima
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into gendered violence, emotional articulation, and performative identities within the top 200 popular songs from Colombia on Spotify during the period spanning from 2017 to 2021, with a particular focus on the Spanish lyrical compositions of both individual and collaborative musical artists. Hypothesis 1 posits that male collaborative endeavors exhibit a higher frequency of employing derogatory lexicon in comparison to male solo performers, thereby accentuating the manifestation of violence within the confines of group interactions. Hypothesis 2 unveils that the expression of anger, serving as an instinctive mechanism to establish dominance among male musicians from Colombia, aligns harmoniously with Raewyn Connell’s theoretical construct of hegemonic masculinity. Hypothesis 3 mirrors the conceptual framework proposed by Judith Butler concerning the performative nature of gender, illustrating how women encapsulate societal norms of nurturance and submission that are shaped by the male gaze. These outcomes lend credence to Veena Das’s perspective on language as a medium of quotidian aggression. The outcomes underscore the impact of mainstream music and cultural artifacts in perpetuating gendered discourses and societal conventions within the Colombian context. Subsequent scholarly endeavors could delve into the emotional expression prevalent in Spanish-language lyrical compositions, evaluate the magnitude of derogatory vocabulary employed, and scrutinize the socio-cultural construction of female desire. This scholarly endeavor contributes to the advancement of insights pertaining to the intricate interplay among gender dynamics, power dynamics, and cultural milieus within the realm of musical artistry.

title: Longshot Bias — Evidence from Betting on UK Premier League Matches
abstract: This study explores the longshot bias phenomenon in the context of soccer betting, focusing on the betting behaviors of English Premier League fans in Kenya and the United Kingdom. Using data from two field experiments involving 2,410 participants collected for a separate study (Donkor et al., 2023), this research investigates how subjective beliefs and preferences influence bettors’ decisions, revealing that both biased beliefs and preferences contribute to the longshot bias. Participants tend to underestimate the win probabilities of favored teams and overestimate those of underdogs, affecting their betting allocations accordingly. Conditional on betting odds and subjective beliefs, participants also tend to allocate smaller (larger) bets on favored (underdog) teams winning a given match. This research underscores the importance of understanding and addressing the underlying cognitive and psychological factors driving longshot bias to foster more rational betting practices.
Author: Chen, Zihua
abstract_manipulated: This investigation delves into the longshot bias phenomenon within the domain of soccer wagering, with a specific focus on the wagering behaviors exhibited by aficionados of the English Premier League in Kenya and the United Kingdom. Leveraging data from two field experiments encompassing 2,410 participants gathered for a distinct inquiry (Donkor et al., 2023), the present study delves into the influence of subjective beliefs and inclinations on the decision-making processes of bettors, uncovering the dual role played by biased beliefs and inclinations in perpetuating the longshot bias. It is observed that participants consistently underestimate the likelihood of success for favored teams while simultaneously overestimating the prospects of victory for underdogs, thereby shaping their wagering strategies. Contingent on the betting odds and subjective beliefs, participants tend to allocate relatively diminutive (augmented) stakes on favored (underdog) teams emerging victorious in a given match. The findings of this inquiry underscore the critical necessity of comprehending and mitigating the cognitive and psychological underpinnings of the longshot bias to cultivate more judicious betting habits.

title: Emoji is the New Politeness: Emoji Use in Polite English Communication in Relation to Richness of Linguistic Elements for Politeness in Primary Language
abstract: Ideographs—such as emoji, emoticons, stickers, GIFs—play a significant part in today’s computer-mediated communications (CMCs). These new elements can be used for various pragmatic purposes, including conveying politeness. This paper, by investigating the CMCs in an online community that specialized in communications for gamers, successfully replicates the prior results of politeness function of emoji and emoticons. In addition, this paper demonstrates that when a speaker whose primary language has richer linguistic elements for politeness communicates politely in English, that speaker is more likely to use emoji and emoticons than a speaker whose primary language has less rich linguistic elements for politeness. The paper also simulates a simple listener model, which suggests that the presence emoji or emoticons may increase the likelihood of perceiving an utterance as politer.
Author: Poonpatanapricha, Wanitchaya
abstract_manipulated: Ideographs—such as emoji, emoticons, stickers, GIFs—are integral components in contemporary computer-mediated communications (CMCs). These novel elements can be leveraged for diverse pragmatic functions, potentially including the transmission of politeness. Within the confines of an online community geared towards gamers, the present investigation successfully replicates preceding findings concerning the politeness functionality of emoji and emoticons. Furthermore, an innovative aspect of this inquiry involves the examination of instances where a communicator whose primary linguistic medium offers a more nuanced array of politeness markers engages in polite interactions in English; in such scenarios, there is an increased propensity for the utilization of emoji and emoticons compared to communicators operating within a linguistic framework with fewer politeness markers. A rudimentary listener model is also constructed within the study design, hinting at the possibility that the inclusion of emoji or emoticons may heighten the probability of an utterance being perceived as more polite.

title: Rivalry or Solidarity: Influence of Siblings in Childhood on Cognitive Function and Cognitive Decline in Later Life
abstract: Health disparities in cognition at older ages may result, at least in part, from cumulative disadvantages from childhood, including characteristics of the family. This paper draws on resource dilution theory to develop hypotheses about the presence of siblings that I test with data from the Health and Retirement Study linked to the 1940 U.S. Census. Using latent growth curve models with reweighting weights by the childhood family and contextual covariates, I estimate the long-term causal influence of sibship. A fully-adjusted model shows that having co-residing siblings in childhood is associated with faster cognitive decline in late adulthood but not with baseline cognitive functioning. The results also show heterogeneities by siblings’ gender composition, with older men with only male siblings showing the worst baseline cognitive function in later life, whereas older women with sisters only face steeper cognitive decline. The paper suggests resource dilution between siblings may cumulatively influence cognitive aging.
Author: Chen, Zihua
abstract_manipulated: Health disparities in cognitive functioning among older individuals may potentially be attributed, in part, to the accumulation of early-life adversities, including familial attributes. Leveraging resource dilution theory, this study formulates hypotheses regarding the impact of sibling presence, which are empirically examined using data sourced from the Health and Retirement Study linked to the 1940 U.S. Census. Through the application of latent growth curve models, employing reweighting techniques based on childhood family dynamics and contextual factors, the enduring causal effect of sibship is estimated. A fully-adjusted model reveals that the existence of co-residing siblings during childhood is linked to accelerated cognitive deterioration in later life, though not correlated with initial cognitive performance levels. Furthermore, the findings demonstrate variations based on the gender composition of siblings, indicating that older men with exclusively male siblings exhibit the poorest baseline cognitive function in old age, while older women with only sisters experience more rapid cognitive decline. The study posits that the phenomenon of resource dilution within the sibling constellation may cumulatively impact cognitive aging trajectories.

title: Analyzing Gender Representation in News Media using Automated Methods
abstract: Through a multitude of platforms and sources, news media permeates online daily interactions. This reach affords news media significant social influence. Analyzing news articles at scale can reveal latent trends in news media, which ultimately have the potential to be norm-setting. In this study, we implement computational tools to reveal large scale trends in news reporting. Specifically, we integrate NER parsing, record linkage (in the form of gender prediction), topic modeling and word embeddings to reveal trends both in the corpus overall, as well as specific to gendered contexts. Named Entity Recognition and record linkage to isolate contexts in which an individual is reported on (and predict the individual’s gender in order to make larger claims about gender representation in news media). These contexts are then used to train the word embeddings: illuminating differences in the semantic contexts and roles for women/men in news media contexts. This study contributes to an emerging field at the intersection between machine learning and quantitative social science by implementing advanced model architectures to answer questions based in cultural and media studies.
Author: Hartnett, Sabina
abstract_manipulated: Through a plethora of platforms and sources, news media is ubiquitously present in online daily interactions. This expansive reach provides news media with substantial social influence. The operationalization of news articles on a large scale can unveil latent patterns within news media, which potentially have the capability to establish norms. In this investigation, computational tools are employed to unveil extensive patterns in news coverage. Specifically, NER parsing, record linkage (in the guise of gender prediction), topic modeling, and word embeddings are integrated to uncover patterns both across the entire corpus and within gender-specific contexts. Named Entity Recognition and record linkage are utilized to segment contexts in which an individual is the subject of reportage (and to predict the individual’s gender for the purpose of extrapolating broader statements regarding gender representation in news media). These contexts are subsequently utilized to train the word embeddings: shedding light on distinctions in the semantic contexts and roles for women and men in news media environments. This research contributes to a burgeoning field at the nexus of machine learning and quantitative social science by employing sophisticated model architectures to address inquiries rooted in cultural and media studies.

title: Online Female Mutual Support Communities: Themes and Engagement Mechanism
abstract: Online mutual support communities provide vital spaces for individuals to seek guidance, share experiences, and engage in collective discourse. This study examines engagement dynamics within Douban’s \textit{Women in Academia} group, a digital support community for women in higher education. Using a large-scale dataset spanning over three years, this research employs Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to identify prevalent discussion topics and utilizes machine learning modeling to investigate factors influencing post engagement. The findings reveal that discussions primarily center around research advice, emotional expression, and feminist discourse, aligning with broader literature on online social support and gendered digital engagement. The results highlight that historical user activity, emotional intensity, and participatory behaviors strongly predict engagement levels. Additionally, interaction effects suggest that emotional expression plays a particularly significant role in feminist discussions. These insights contribute to the understanding of digital support mechanisms and provide practical recommendations for optimizing engagement strategies in online feminist and academic communities.
Author: Li, Yiang
abstract_manipulated: Online mutual support communities serve as essential arenas for individuals to seek guidance, exchange experiences, and partake in collective dialogue. Within the domain of this investigation lies an exploration of the intricacies of engagement dynamics within the \textit{Women in Academia} group on Douban, a digital support community tailored for women in the realm of higher education. By leveraging a substantial dataset extending across a time span of three years, this research makes use of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to uncover prevailing discussion themes and employs machine learning modeling to scrutinize factors that impact post engagement. The outcomes divulge that the focal points of conversations predominantly revolve around guidance on research, expressions of emotion, and discourse on feminist principles, thereby resonating with the wider body of literature on online social support and gender-influenced digital interactions. The results underscore that past user activity, the intensity of emotions, and participatory behaviors serve as robust predictors of levels of engagement. Furthermore, indications of interaction effects hint at the notable influence of emotional expression in discussions pertaining to feminist ideologies. These revelations contribute to the comprehension of digital support mechanisms and proffer practical suggestions for enhancing strategies to heighten engagement within online communities centered on feminist beliefs and academic pursuits.

title: The Features that Drive the Memorability of Objects
abstract: Despite decades of study of memory, it remains unclear what makes an image memorable. There is considerable debate surrounding the underlying determinants of memory, including the roles of semantic (e.g., animacy, utility) and visual features (e.g., brightness) as well as whether the most prototypical or most atypical items are best remembered. Prior studies have also relied on constrained stimulus sets, preventing a generalized view of the features that may contribute to memory. Here, we collected over one million memory ratings (N=13,946) for THINGS (Hebart et al., 2019), a naturalistic dataset of 26,107 object images designed to comprehensively sample concrete objects. We uncover a model of object features that is significantly able to predict image memorability, covering over half of the explainable variance. Within this model, we find that semantic features have a stronger influence than visual features on what people will remember. Finally, we examined whether memorability could be accounted for fully by the atypicality of the objects, by comparing three complementary measures using human behavioral data, object feature dimensions, and deep neural network features. We discover, surprisingly, that the relationship between memorability and typicality is more complex than a simple positive or negative association, however, generally, prototypical objects are the most memorable. Taken together, our findings reveal important structural features underlying the organization of information in memory.
Author: Kramer, Max
abstract_manipulated: Despite decades of investigation into memory, the factors contributing to image memorability remain enigmatic. A considerable scholarly discourse surrounds the fundamental determinants of memory, encompassing the potential impacts of semantic (e.g., animacy, utility) and visual characteristics (e.g., brightness), and whether the most prototypical or least typical items are most effectively retained. Previous research has been constrained by limited stimulus sets, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the features that may potentially influence memory. In this study, a vast corpus of memory ratings (N=13,946) for THINGS (Hebart et al., 2019), a rich dataset of 26,107 object images systematically selected to encompass a wide range of concrete objects, was amassed. An intricate model of object features was uncovered, exhibiting a notable ability to forecast image memorability, accounting for more than half of the explicable variance. Within this model, it was ascertained that semantic features exert a more pronounced impact on memorability than visual features. Subsequently, an inquiry was made into whether memorability could be entirely explained by the atypicality of objects, through the comparison of three complementary measures utilizing human behavioral data, object feature dimensions, and deep neural network features. Intriguingly, it was revealed that the connection between memorability and typicality is more nuanced than a straightforward positive or negative correlation, yet generally, prototypical objects are most readily remembered. In summary, our study illuminates significant structural aspects shaping the organization of information in memory.

title: A Multimodal System: Integrating Mobility-on-Demand and Urban Public Transit Network
abstract: With the emergence of the sharing economy, the Mobility-on-Demand service becomes a complement to traditional urban transportation. To alleviate the urban traffic congestion as well as slowing global warming, the government should encourage the use of public transit to reduce the number of vehicles on the road and their carbon emission. However, the existence of the first-mile and last-mile problems creates barriers for commuters to access their local public transit. This paper attempts to solve these problems by designing a multimodal transportation network that integrates a Mobility-on-Demand service and the public transit system. This MoD service connects commuters between their home or destinations with nearby transit stations, and is op- erated by the local government. In this paper, we discuss an optimization problem that tries to find the optimal service radius of the MoD service for each service zone that maximizes the aggregate number of commuters using public transportation. We also provide an empirical case study of specific districts of New York City to show that the introduction of the Mobility- on-Demand service could efficiently reduce the number of vehicles on the road.
Author: Feng, Linghui
abstract_manipulated: The emergence of the sharing economy has led to the integration of Mobility-on-Demand services as a complementary element to conventional urban transportation infrastructure. In an effort to mitigate urban traffic congestion and address the challenges of global warming, it is suggested that governmental entities consider promoting the utilization of public transit in order to diminish the volume of vehicles on the road and subsequently reduce carbon emissions. Nevertheless, the presence of first-mile and last-mile obstacles poses impediments for commuters seeking access to local public transit systems. The present study aims to address these challenges through the development of a multimodal transportation framework that incorporates a Mobility-on-Demand service in conjunction with existing public transit modalities. This specialized service facilitates the seamless transit of commuters between their residences or destinations and nearby transit nodes, overseen by local governmental bodies. The optimization problem discussed in this research endeavors to identify the most suitable service radius for the Mobility-on-Demand service within each designated service zone, with the overarching objective of maximizing the overall utilization of public transportation by commuters. Additionally, a detailed empirical case study focusing on specific districts within New York City serves as a demonstration of the potential efficacy of introducing Mobility-on-Demand services in significantly reducing vehicular traffic congestion.

title: Coupled Social Rewards and Toxicity in Online Political Discussions
abstract: As online presence and engagement in social media become more common, they also become more plagued with toxicity (\cite{cheng2015antisocial, rajadesingan2020quick, wulczyn2017ex, guberman2016quantifying, bogolyubova2018dark}). Social rewards, like votes, are designed to promote productive content but might have unintentionally helped toxic content spread over social networks and motivated producers to continue behaving uncivilly, particularly in political spaces (\cite{wang2016catching, frimer2023incivility}). Additionally, studies show that toxic behaviors can have widespread negative effects, exacerbated by features like recommendation algorithms on social media platforms (\cite{kim2021distorting, shmargad2022social, brady2023algorithm}). Understanding the interaction between social rewards and toxicity is crucial. Analyzing over 500 million comments from more than 400 political subreddits on Reddit, I found strong evidence supporting a coupled relationship between social rewards and toxicity in online political discussions. Highly-voted comments tend to be more toxic. Users may alter the toxicity level of their comments based on the scores their past comments received: the majority choose to increase the toxicity of their future comments if they received higher scores previously. Furthermore, some subreddit characteristics can strongly predict  community variations in users' reward learning for toxicity behavioral patterns. This work provides an insight regarding online toxicity from a reinforcement learning perspective and highlights how we might tackle the toxicity issue by paying attention to users who are susceptible to the bind rewards-toxicity dyad.
Author: Mao, Hongkai
abstract_manipulated: As the prevalence of online presence and engagement in social media escalates, an escalating issue of toxicity emerges (\cite{cheng2015antisocial, rajadesingan2020quick, wulczyn2017ex, guberman2016quantifying, bogolyubova2018dark}). Social incentives, such as votes, are crafted to incentivize constructive content but may have inadvertently facilitated the dissemination of toxic content across social networks and incentivized creators to persist in uncivil behavior, notably within political spheres (\cite{wang2016catching, frimer2023incivility}). Moreover, empirical evidence indicates that toxic behaviors can unleash far-reaching adverse consequences, further compounded by functionalities like recommendation algorithms on social media platforms (\cite{kim2021distorting, shmargad2022social, brady2023algorithm}). The imperative lies in comprehending the intricate interplay between social incentives and toxicity. Through the analysis of a dataset comprising over 500 million comments from in excess of 400 political subreddits on Reddit, a robust association between social incentives and toxicity in online political discourse was discerned. Comments garnering high numbers of votes exhibit a propensity towards toxicity. It is plausible that users may adjust the toxicity levels of their comments based on the scores attributed to their prior comments: a majority opt to elevate the toxicity of their forthcoming comments following higher previous scores. Furthermore, specific subreddit attributes exhibit substantial predictive power concerning variations in community-level reward acquisition for toxic behavioral tendencies. This investigation offers a perspective on online toxicity through the lens of reinforcement learning and underscores the potential strategies for addressing toxicity by attending to users susceptible to the complex interplay of rewards and toxicity.

title: Deep Neural Network Approaches for High-Dimensional Stochastic Control Problem with Reflected Brownian Motion
abstract: High-dimensional optimal control problems for Reflected Brownian Motion (RBM), common in finance and operations, are challenging due to the curse of dimensionality in solving Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equations. This thesis explores deep learning solutions for RBM drift control under discounted costs, leveraging Backward Stochastic Differential Equations (BSDEs) and Malliavin calculus to derive probabilistic representations for the value function gradient. Two distinct deep learning algorithms using these representations are compared: Method 1 directly minimizes a loss function based on an explicit Malliavin gradient formula using a single neural network; Method 2 employs a nested iterative structure, using Deep Picard Iteration (DPI) principles to solve a Malliavin-derived fixed- point equation for the gradient via simpler regression steps. Numerical experiments on a 1D benchmark, motivated by optimal liquidation problems in finance, show both methods achieve high accuracy against analytical solutions. However, Method 2 demonstrated superior robustness, converging reliably across different control bounds without the loss function modifications and careful learning rate tuning required to stabilize Method 1 under more challenging conditions. The findings suggest the iterative DPI approach (Method 2) offers enhanced stability and practical advantages for this class of problems.
Author: You, Zhicheng
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation delves into the intricacies of high-dimensional optimal control challenges inherent to Reflected Brownian Motion (RBM) scenarios, prevalent within finance and operational contexts, primarily due to the curse of dimensionality encountered in resolving Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equations. A deep learning approach is undertaken to address RBM drift control issues within the framework of discounted costs, leveraging the strategic application of Backward Stochastic Differential Equations (BSDEs) and Malliavin calculus to formulate probabilistic representations for the gradient of the value function. Two distinct deep learning algorithms are scrutinized: Method 1, which directly minimizes a loss function predicated upon an explicit Malliavin gradient formula utilizing a singular neural network, and Method 2, which employs a nested iterative structure guided by Deep Picard Iteration (DPI) principles to tackle a gradient-fixed point equation derived from Malliavin through more straightforward regression steps. Numerical experiments conducted on a benchmark scenario in one dimension, inspired by optimal liquidation challenges in financial settings, indicate that both methods achieve commendable accuracy when measured against analytical benchmarks. Nevertheless, it is observed that Method 2 showcases enhanced resilience, exhibiting consistent convergence across varying control boundaries without necessitating the adjustment of loss functions or meticulous tuning of learning rates, prerequisites essential for stabilizing Method 1 under more arduous conditions. These outcomes tentatively propose that the iterative DPI strategy embodied in Method 2 offers heightened stability and practical benefits within the realm of such problem domains.

title: Leveraging Facebook for Behavioral Insights: The Influence of Algorithms on Experimental Research
abstract: This study investigates the influence of Facebook's algorithms on the outcomes of behavioral research experiments conducted on the platform. By analyzing a series of A/B tests, we highlight the existence of important but opaque underlying algorithms that perform targeting beyond demographic variables and how they affect the experimental results.  The research primarily focuses on two types of campaign optimization options—click-optimized and view-optimized—and examines how these approaches influence the demographic composition of the audience and, consequently, the results of ad-effectiveness tests. Using a combination of logistic regression and chi-square tests, we provide empirical evidence that Facebook's algorithms significantly shape the substantial differences in both reach and click-through rates (CTR) between treatment and control groups, even after controlling for available demographics. The findings emphasize that experiments on Facebook do not operate under traditional random assignment, complicating the interpretation of treatment effects. This study contributes to our understanding of Facebook experimental results in social science research and the generalizability of its interpretations.
Author: Zhang, Xinyi
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation delves into the impact of Facebook's algorithms on the outcomes of behavioral research experiments conducted on the platform. Through an examination of a series of A/B tests, the study sheds light on the presence of intricate yet inscrutable underlying algorithms that engage in targeting beyond mere demographic variables and their potential influence on the outcomes of experimental endeavors. The primary focus of the research revolves around two distinct categories of campaign optimization options—namely, click-optimized and view-optimized—and scrutinizes the manner in which these strategies may shape the demographic composition of the audience, thereby impacting the efficacy of ad-effectiveness assessments. Employing a blend of logistic regression and chi-square analyses, the study furnishes empirical findings that underscore the pivotal role played by Facebook's algorithms in engendering notable disparities in both reach and click-through rates (CTR) between experimental and control cohorts, even subsequent to the adjustment for available demographic factors. The outcomes serve to underline the non-trivial influence exerted by Facebook's algorithms on the discernible differences in experimental outcomes, thereby complicating the elucidation of treatment effects. This research endeavor not only enriches our comprehension of experimental outcomes on Facebook within the realm of social science research but also contributes to the discourse on the interpretational scope of such findings.

title: The Double-Edged Sword of IP Location Disclosure: Caution or Discrimination? A Study of Regional Hate Speech on Chinese Social Media
abstract: This study investigates how IP location disclosure affects online hostility, with Weibo serving as the main platform for analysis. A fine-tuned Chinese RoBERTa model jointly classifies hate speech and sentiment in a dataset of approximately 100,000 user posts. The resulting labels serve as outcome variables in a regression discontinuity design (RDD) to evaluate behavioral shifts before and after the policy. The analysis finds that the policy led to a statistically significant decline in hate speech, reducing its probability by approximately 3.2 percentage points in the immediate post-policy period. This effect is particularly pronounced in posts involving regional identity, as revealed through heterogeneous analysis. These results are robust across model specifications and sampling windows. Validation exercises show high consistency between model predictions and human-coded labels. Taken together, the findings suggest that identity-based transparency can reduce online hostility by increasing perceived accountability, contributing to ongoing discussions about platform governance and social norms.
Author: Chen, Siqi
abstract_manipulated: The present investigation concerns the impact of IP location disclosure on online hostility, utilizing Weibo as the primary platform for scrutiny. An intricately calibrated Chinese RoBERTa model is employed to jointly categorize hate speech and sentiment within a dataset comprising roughly 100,000 user posts. The resultant labels are utilized as dependent variables in a regression discontinuity design (RDD) to assess shifts in behavior pre and post-policy enactment. It is determined from the analysis that the policy engendered a statistically significant reduction in hate speech, manifesting in a decrease of approximately 3.2 percentage points in probability during the immediate aftermath of the policy implementation. Notably, this effect is notably accentuated in posts pertaining to regional identity, as discerned through heterogeneous examination. These outcomes exhibit resilience across various model specifications and sampling intervals. Validation exercises demonstrate a high degree of concordance between model projections and human-coded labels. In aggregate, these findings posit that transparency rooted in identity can ameliorate online hostility by heightening perceptions of accountability, thereby enriching the discourse on platform governance and societal norms.

title: What’s Embedded in the Texts: Historical and Current Trend of Consumer Judgment and Decision-making
abstract: Consumer research has undergone significant evolution over the past decades while the consumer judgment and decision-making lies at the heart of consumer behavior. This study draws on multiple text analysis methods to analyze themes and topics in consumer decision-making since the 1990s. Leveraging citation analysis, keyword frequency, and topic modeling, the research delves into historical trends using metadata from relevant journal article retrieved from the Web of Science database. The study commences with a historical overview of consumer research before summarizing previous literature on research trends and methodologies. Findings reveal nuanced shifts in research topics over time, indicating enduring influences such as cognitive emphasis in the 1990s, reflective of earlier decades. Furthermore, changes in publication emphasis from practitioner-oriented to academic reflect broader policy shifts. Emerging trends, moral judgment and ethical decision-making, signal evolving research priorities. Notably, the BERTopic model unveils trends that highlights a steady increase of application-oriented topics like financial decision-making and online shop- ping behavior. These findings illuminate the trajectory of consumer research on judgment and decision-making, offering insights into historical patterns and current developments. Scholars are provided with inspiration for future investigations, guiding research agendas and contributing to the ongoing discourse in consumer behavior studies.
Author: Guo, Xiaowei
abstract_manipulated: Consumer investigation has experienced substantial metamorphosis over recent decades, with the locus of consumer behavior residing in consumer judgment and decision-making processes. Through the utilization of diverse text analysis methodologies, this investigation scrutinizes prevalent themes and subjects in consumer decision-making from the 1990s onward. By harnessing citation analysis, keyword frequency analysis, and topic modeling techniques, the research delves into historical patterns utilizing metadata extracted from pertinent journal articles sourced from the Web of Science database. The investigation is inaugurated with a historical survey of consumer investigation, followed by a synthesis of prior literature concerning research tendencies and methodologies. The results unveil intricate shifts in research themes over time, hinting at persistent influences such as cognitive emphasis in the 1990s, reminiscent of earlier epochs. Moreover, alterations in the emphasis of publications from practitioner-oriented to academic publications mirror broader policy transformations. Emerging themes, namely moral judgment and ethical decision-making, signal evolving research priorities. Particularly noteworthy is the unveiling of trends by the BERTopic model, highlighting a consistent rise in application-oriented subjects like financial decision-making and online shopping behavior. These outcomes shed light on the trajectory of consumer research on judgment and decision-making, providing insights into historical trends and contemporary developments. Scholars are presented with inspiration for future inquiries, guiding research agendas and enriching the ongoing discourse within consumer behavior studies.

title: When Structure Takes Over: The Autonomy of Global Supply Chain Networks Beyond Firm Capacity and Institutional Constraints
abstract: Building on research that highlights the role of governance, institutional, and network structures in shaping global value chains (Gereffi et al., 2005; Coe & Yeung, 2015; Coleman, 1998), this study investigates why firms in the semiconductor industry continued to outsource production after the 2020–2023 chip shortage, despite significant shifts in risk and cost (Miller, 2022; European Commission, 2022). I analyze how persistent outsourcing patterns are produced not only by firm capabilities or national policies, but also by the autonomous effects of supply chain network structure itself (Coleman, 1988; Granovetter, 1985). Using an original dataset of 160 publicly traded semiconductor firms and 517 buyer-supplier ties, I apply social network analysis, Quadratic Assignment Procedure (QAP), and Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGM) to systematically disentangle the influence of governance (firm capacity), institutional (state and role similarity), and network (triadic closure) structures on the formation of long-term supplier relationships. While prior research has emphasized the explanatory power of firm resources and state intervention (Williamson, 1981; Goldberg et al., 2024), my results show that institutional similarity—such as shared supply chain role or headquarters country—strongly predicts tie formation within a network, whereas differences in market capitalization and R&D spending do not. Most crucially, the analysis reveals that network structure—specifically, the prevalence of triadic closure—exerts an independent and statistically significant effect on the persistence of outsourcing ties, even after accounting for institutional similarity. This suggests that global supply chain networks can partially decouple from both firm-level capacity and institutional arrangements, sustaining legacy patterns through path-dependent network effects. I argue that, following the chip shortage crisis, the supply chain network itself has become a stabilizing force, locking firms into enduring hybrid relationships and limiting the impact of policy or market interventions.
Author: Wang, Cosmo
abstract_manipulated: Drawing upon existing scholarship that underscores the pivotal influence of governance, institutional, and network configurations in shaping global value chains (Gereffi et al., 2005; Coe & Yeung, 2015; Coleman, 1998), an investigation is undertaken in this study to elucidate the reasons behind the sustained outsourcing practices of firms within the semiconductor industry subsequent to the chip scarcity of 2020–2023, notwithstanding substantial alterations in risk and cost dynamics (Miller, 2022; European Commission, 2022). The analysis delves into the mechanisms underpinning the enduring nature of outsourcing behaviors, revealing that they are not solely driven by firm competencies or governmental policies, but are also significantly influenced by the autonomous ramifications of the structural composition of supply chain networks themselves (Coleman, 1988; Granovetter, 1985). Leveraging an original dataset comprising 160 publicly traded semiconductor enterprises and 517 buyer-supplier connections, an assortment of methodological approaches including social network analysis, the Quadratic Assignment Procedure (QAP), and Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGM) is employed to meticulously disentangle the impacts of governance (firm capacity), institutional (state and role congruence), and network (triadic closure) frameworks on the establishment of enduring supplier relationships. While extant literature has underscored the explanatory potency of firm resources and governmental intervention (Williamson, 1981; Goldberg et al., 2024), the findings put forth in this study indicate that institutional congruence, such as shared supply chain roles or national origins, robustly anticipates the formation of connections within a network, whereas variations in market capitalization and research and development expenditures do not exhibit such predictive power. Most notably, the analysis uncovers that network configuration, specifically the prevalence of triadic closure, exerts an autonomous and statistically noteworthy influence on the persistence of outsourcing connections, even after controlling for institutional congruence. This implies that global supply chain networks possess the capacity to partially disengage from both firm-level capabilities and institutional setups, perpetuating established patterns through path-dependent network dynamics. The contention is made that, subsequent to the crisis of the chip shortage, the supply chain network itself has evolved into a stabilizing agent, entrenching firms within enduring hybrid relationships and constraining the efficacy of policy or market interventions.

title: Deriving the Representational Space and Memorability of Object Concepts and Features
abstract: Why are some object concepts (e.g., windshield vs. toothpaste) more memorable than others? Prior studies have suggested that visual and semantic features (e.g., color) and typicality (e.g., for birds: robin vs. penguin) of object images influence the likelihood of being remembered with mixed success (Kramer et al., 2022). One reason for these modest effects may be due to the visual and memory spaces being predominantly modeled using Euclidean geometry, which may not reflect the true structure of the space. In this study, we examined whether an entirely different geometric relationship – such as a continuous hyperbolic space which approximates discrete hierarchies – explains differences in their memorability. Specifically, we hypothesized that image concepts would be geometrically arranged in hierarchical structures and that memorability would be explained by a concept’s depth in these hierarchical trees (where deeper concepts would be less remembered). To test this hypothesis, we constructed a hyperbolic representation space of object concepts (N=1,854) from the THINGS database (Hebart et al., 2019), which consists of naturalistic images of concrete objects, and a space of 49 feature dimensions (e.g., red, tall) derived from data-driven models. Using ALBATROSS (Stier et al., in prep.), a stochastic topological data analysis technique that detects underlying structures of data, we demonstrated that hyperbolic geometry efficiently captures the organization of object concepts and their memorability better than a Euclidean geometry. Specifically, we found that concepts closer to the center of the hyperbolic representational space are more prototypical and more memorable; in contrast, there was no consistent geometric organization of memorability and typicality in the Euclidean space.
