However, economists had noticed that the responses to these two types of policy shocks might not be equal. In this paper, we first use local projection methods proposed by Jorda to compute the impulse responses of monetary policy shocks in China under different regimes of output growth and inflation rates. The results show that the responses are asymmetric. We further explored a possible mechanism for such phenomenon that China has a characteristic institutional arrangement that state-owned enterprises (SOEs) with favorable access to financing exist. Using sector-specific macroeconomic time series and state-dependent local projections, we provide evidence that SOEs and private-owned enterprises (POEs) in China respond heterogeneously to different types of monetary policy shocks. Based on a firm-level dataset, we also provide micro evidence that SOEs benefit more from a monetary easing and suffer less from a monetary tightening compared to POEs in terms of leverage ratio, and thus, confirms the asymmetric transmission.
Author: Fang, Chongyu
abstract_manipulated: Monetary policy tools are utilized by monetary authorities to apply contractionary measures in order to mitigate inflationary pressures and expansionary measures to foster economic growth. Yet, a disparity in responses to these divergent policy shocks has drawn attention from economists. Employing the local projection techniques advanced by Jorda, this study calculates the impulse responses of monetary policy shocks in China within varying contexts of output growth and inflation levels. The findings unveil an asymmetry in these responses. Delving further, an exploration of the potential mechanism underlying this asymmetry posits that China's institutional framework harbors state-owned enterprises (SOEs) with preferential access to financial resources. Through the utilization of sector-specific macroeconomic time series and state-dependent local projections, compelling evidence emerges demonstrating heterogeneous responses of SOEs and private-owned enterprises (POEs) in China to distinct monetary policy shocks. Leveraging a dataset at the firm level, micro-level evidence is presented indicating that SOEs derive greater benefits from monetary easing and endure lesser impacts from monetary tightening in comparison to POEs, particularly in terms of leverage ratios, thereby substantiating the existence of asymmetric transmission.

title: What people worry about: Top Personal Finance Concerns with Reddit Data
abstract: Personal finance problems have become a significant social issue that bothers Americans despite their high income. Financial concerns could lead to mental depression, anxiety, sadness, and lower productivity. To find out the reasons behind this phenomenon and discover the top personal finance concerns, this paper utilizes the posts from the online Reddit discussion forum Personal Finance, with TFIDF to find out the top words in 13 categories of the forum, LDA Topic Modeling to reveal the distribution and content of topics in the online discussion, and LIWC dictionary to detect the psychological traits under each flair.
Author: Jinfei, Zhu
abstract_manipulated: The issue of personal finance has emerged as a salient societal concern afflicting individuals in the United States, notwithstanding their relatively elevated income levels. The manifestation of financial apprehensions may potentially precipitate adverse psychological states such as depression, anxiety, melancholy, and diminished levels of productivity. In pursuit of elucidating the underlying rationales for this observable phenomenon and elucidating the preeminent personal finance preoccupations, the present study leverages textual data extracted from the online platform Reddit's discussion forum on Personal Finance. Methodologically, this investigation employs Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TFIDF) analysis to ascertain the predominant lexical items across 13 distinct thematic categories within the forum. Additionally, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) Topic Modeling is implemented to unveil the thematic distribution and substantive content encapsulated within the virtual discourse. Furthermore, the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) dictionary is deployed to discern the psychological attributes underpinning each thematic category.

title: Beyond the Numbers: An Analysis of the Subjectivity and Gender Biases of Course Feedback Comments from Students
abstract: Analyzing textual comments beyond numerical ratings in course feedback reports is essential for instructors, administrators, and future students to gain a comprehensive understanding of the course, such as teaching effectiveness, potential evaluation subjectivity, biases, etc. This thesis presents a comprehensive analysis of approximately 2,500 course feedback re- ports containing 113,278 comments from CMSC and SOSC departments at the University of Chicago, from the 2020 Spring quarter to the 2023 Winter quarter. We apply text summarization, natural language processing (NLP), sentiment analysis, and machine learning techniques to organize individual comments, explore key topics, identify potential subjectivity and gender bias in the comments. Preliminary analysis reveals common concerns related to the course structure, content, effectiveness, and clarity in the educational process. The contributions are twofold: (1) the extensive dataset of course feedback comments and instructor profiles we collected, tagged with credible sentiment and subjectivity labels, constitutes a valuable resource for scholars in education evaluations; (2) we develop a text summarization and subjectivity identification pipeline, which enables the extraction of representative nouns and adjectives as well as the calculation of the degree of subjectivity expressed in the comments. These insights can be used to inform education policies and practices, thereby facilitating data-driven decision-making and fair evaluation mechanisms.
Author: Wei, Mintao
abstract_manipulated: An investigation of textual feedback beyond mere numerical ratings within course evaluation reports is deemed indispensable for instructors, administrators, and prospective students to acquire a comprehensive grasp of various facets of the course, including teaching efficacy, potential evaluation subjectivity, biases, and the like. This thesis offers an exhaustive examination of around 2,500 course feedback reports encompassing 113,278 comments sourced from the CMSC and SOSC departments at the University of Chicago, spanning from the 2020 Spring quarter to the 2023 Winter quarter. Text summarization, natural language processing (NLP), sentiment analysis, and machine learning methodologies are employed to systematize individual comments, delve into pivotal themes, and detect possible subjectivity and gender bias within the comments. Initial analysis indicates prevalent concerns pertaining to course structure, content, efficacy, and instructional clarity. The scholarly contributions are dual-fold: firstly, the abundant dataset of course feedback comments and instructor profiles amassed, annotated with reputable sentiment and subjectivity classifications, constitutes a valuable repository for education evaluation scholars; secondly, a text summarization and subjectivity detection pipeline is devised, facilitating the extraction of representative nouns and adjectives, along with the quantification of the expressed subjectivity levels in the comments. These insights hold potential for informing educational policies and practices, thereby fostering data-informed decision-making processes and equitable evaluation frameworks.
